\hline AI_SN & 1 bit & - \\
\hline \} & - & - \\
\hline If (Encoding Mode = = 10) \{ & - & HARQ Incremental Redundancy \\
\hline If (ACKCH Allocation) \{ & - & - \\
\hline ACK CH Index & 5 bits & See DL Ack channel index in 8.4.5.4.24 \\
\hline \} Else \{ & - & - \\
\hline Reserved & 1 bit & Shall be set to zero. \\
\hline \} & - & - \\
\hline $\mathbf{N}_{\mathbf{E P}}$ & 4 bits & - \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 286y—AAS_SDMA_DL_IE (continued)}
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|}
\hline Syntax & Size & Notes \\
\hline $\mathbf{N}_{\mathbf{S C H}}$ & 4 bits & Indicator for the number of first slots used for data encoding in this SDMA allocation region \\
\hline SPID & 2 bits & - \\
\hline ACID & 4 bits & - \\
\hline AI_SN & 1 bit & - \\
\hline \} & - & - \\
\hline If (Encoding Mode = = 11) \{ & - & HARQ Conv. Code Incremental Redundancy \\
\hline If (ACKCH Allocation) \{ & - & - \\
\hline ACK CH Index & 5 bits & See DL Ack channel index in 8.4.5.4.24 \\
\hline Reserved & 2 bits & Shall be set to zero. \\
\hline \} Else \{ & - & - \\
\hline Reserved & 3 bits & Shall be set to zero \\
\hline \} & - & - \\
\hline DIUC & 4 bits & - \\
\hline Repetition Coding Indication & 2 bits & \begin{tabular}{l}
0b00: No repetition \\
0b01: Repetition of 2 \\
0b10: Repetition of 4 \\
0b11: Repetition of 6
\end{tabular} \\
\hline SPID & 2 bits & - \\
\hline ACID & 4 bits & - \\
\hline AI_SN & 1 bit & - \\
\hline \} & - & - \\
\hline If (CQICH Allocation Included) \{ & - & - \\
\hline Allocation Index & 6 bits & Index to the channel in a frame the CQI report should be transmitted by the SS \\
\hline Period (p) & 3 bits & A CQI feedback is transmitted on the CQI channels indexed by the (CQI Channel Index) by the SS in every $2^{\mathrm{p}}$ frames \\
\hline Frame offset & 3 bits & The MS starts reporting at the frame of which the number has the same 3 LSB as the specified frame offset. If the current frame is specified, the MS should start reporting in eight frames \\
\hline Duration (d) & 4 bits & A CQI feedback is transmitted on the CQI channels indexed by the (CQI Channel Index) by the SS for $2^{(\mathrm{d}-1)}$ frames. If d is 0b0000, the CQICH is de-allocated. If d is 0b1111, the MS should report until the BS command for the MS to stop. \\
\hline \} & - & - \\
\hline \} & - & End of User loop \\
\hline \} & - & End of Burst Region Loop \\
\hline Padding & variable & - \\
\hline \} & - & - \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

In an AAS zone with PUSC permutation, all AAS_SDMA_DL_IEs that define allocations in a given major group shall contain the same value for the 'Number of users' field. In AAS zone with PUSC, user \# $n$ uses the pilot pattern as defined for antenna $\# n$ in 8.4.8.

\section*{Insert a new subclause 8.4.5.3.27:}

\subsection*{8.4.5.3.27 PUSC ASCA Allocation}

In the DL-MAP, a BS may transmit DIUC $=15$ with the PUSC_ASCA_IE() to indicate that data is transmitted to a PUSC-ASCA supporting MS using the PUSC-ASCA permutation.(See Table 286z.)

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 286z-PUSC ASCA Allocation}
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|}
\hline Syntax & Size & Notes \\
\hline PUSC_ASCA_Alloc_IE \{ & - & - \\
\hline Extended DIUC & 4 bits & - \\
\hline Length & 4 bits & Length = 0x06 \\
\hline DIUC & 4 bits & - \\
\hline Short Basic CID & 12 bits & 12 least significant bits of the Basic CID \\
\hline OFDMA Symbol offset & 8 bits & - \\
\hline Subchannel offset & 6 bits & - \\
\hline No. OFDMA Symbols & 7 bits & - \\
\hline No. Subchannels & 6 bits & - \\
\hline Repetition Coding Information & 2 bits & \begin{tabular}{l}
0b00 = No repetition coding \\
0b01 = Repetition coding of 2 used \\
0b10= Repetition coding of 4 used \\
0b11 = Repetition coding of 6 used
\end{tabular} \\
\hline Permutation ID & 4 bits & - \\
\hline Reserved & 7 bits & Shall be set to zero. \\
\hline \} & - & - \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\section*{DIUC}

DIUC used for the burst.

\section*{Short Basic CID}

12 least significant bits of the Basic CID.

\section*{OFDMA Symbol offset}

The offset of the OFDMA symbol in which the burst starts, measured in OFDMA symbols from beginning of the downlink frame in which the DL-MAP is transmitted.

\section*{Subchannel offset}

The lowest index OFDMA subchannel used for carrying the burst, starting from subchannel 0 .

\section*{No. OFDMA Symbols}

The number of OFDMA symbols that are used (fully or partially) to carry the downlink PHY Burst.

\section*{No. of subchannels}

The number of subchannels with subsequent indexes, used to carry the burst.

\section*{Repetition coding Indication}

Indicates the repetition code used inside the allocated burst.

\section*{Permutation ID}

Identifies the PUSC ASCA permutation used to carry the burst.

\subsection*{8.4.5.4 UL-MAP IE format}

\section*{Change 8.4.5.4 as indicated:}

The OFDMA UL-MAP IE defines uplink bandwidth allocations. Uplink bandwidth allocations are specified either as block allocations (subchannel by symbol) with an absolute offset, or as an allocation with duration in slots with either a relative or absolute slot offset. Block allocations are used for fast feedback (UIUC $=0$ ), CDMA ranging and BW request allocations (UIUC $=12$ ) as well as PAPR/Safety zone allocations (UIUC $=13$ ). Slot allocations are used for all other UL bandwidth allocations. For UL allocations in nonAAS zones, the starting position for the allocation is determined considering the prior allocations appearing in the UL-MAP. For UL allocations in an AAS UL Zone, the starting position is included in the UL IE indicating an absolute slot offset from the beginning of the AAS zone. If an OFDMA UL-MAP IE with $\underline{\mathrm{UIUC}}=0$ or $\mathrm{UIUC}=12$ or UIUC $=13$ exists, they must be shall always be allocated first.

For the Fthe first OFDMA UL-MAP IE, with UIUC other than $\underline{0}, 12$ or 13 , the allocation shall start at the lowest numbered non-allocated subchannel on the first non-allocated OFDMA symbol defined by the allocation start time field of the UL-MAP message that is not allocated with UIUC $=0$ or UIUC $=12$ or UIUC $=13$ (See Figure 217 for an example). These IEs shall represent the number of slots provided for the allocation. For allocations not in an AAS zone, Eeach allocation IE shall start immediately following the previous allocation and shall advance in the time domainaxis. If the end of the UL frame zone has been reached, the allocation shall continue at the next subchannel at first OFDMA symbol (define by the allocation start time field allocated to that zone that is not allocated with UIUC $=0$ or UIUC $=12$ or UIUC $=13$. The CID represents the assignment of the IE to either a unicast, multicast, or broadcast address. A UIUC shall be used to define the type of uplink access and the burst type associated with that access. A Burst Descriptor shall be specified in the UCD for each UIUC to be used in the UL-MAP. For further details on allocations in an UL AAS zone, see 8.4.4.6.

The format of the UL-MAP IE is defined in Table 287.
Change Table 287 and the text that follows as indicated:

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 287—OFDMA UL-MAP IE format}
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|}
\hline Syntax & Size & Notes \\
\hline UL-MAP_IE() \{ & - & - \\
\hline CID & 16 bits & - \\
\hline UIUC & 4 bits & - \\
\hline if (UIUC == 11) \{ & & \\
\hline Extended UIUC 2 dependent IE & variable & See 8.4.5.4.4.2 \\
\hline 1 & & \\
\hline elseif (UIUC == 12) \{ & - & - \\
\hline OFDMA symbol offset & 8 bits & - \\
\hline Subchannel offset & 7 bits & - \\
\hline No. OFDMA symbols & 7 bits & - \\
\hline No. subchannels & 7 bits & - \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 287-OFDMA UL-MAP IE format (continued)}
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|}
\hline Syntax & Size & Notes \\
\hline Ranging method & 2 bits & \begin{tabular}{l}
0b00 - Initial Ranging/Handover Ranging over two symbols \\
0b01 - Initial Ranging/Handover Ranging over four symbols \\
0b10 - BW Request/Periodic Ranging over one symbol \\
0b11- BW Request/Periodic Ranging over three symbols
\end{tabular} \\
\hline Reserved Dedicated ranging indicator & 1 bit & shall be set to zere 0: the OFDMA region and Ranging Method defined are used for the purpose of normal ranging 1: the OFDMA region and Ranging Method defined are used for the purpose of ranging using dedicated CDMA code and transmission opportunities assigned in the MOB_PAG-ADV message or in the MOB_SCN-RSP message. \\
\hline \} else if (UIUC == 13) \{ & = & - \\
\hline PAPR Reduction and Safety Zone Allocation_IE & 32 bits & - \\
\hline \} else if (UIUC == 14) \{ & - & - \\
\hline CDMA_Allocation_IE() & 32 bits & - \\
\hline \} else if (UIUC == 15) \{ & - & - \\
\hline Extended UIUC dependent IE & variable & See subclauses following 8.4.5.4.3 \\
\hline \} else if (UIUC $==0$ ) \{ & = & = \\
\hline FAST-FEEDBACK_Allocation_IE() & 32 bits & = \\
\hline \} else \{ & - & - \\
\hline Duration & 10 bits & In OFDMA slots (see 8.4.3.1) \\
\hline Repetition coding indication & 2 bits & \begin{tabular}{l}
0b00 - No repetition coding \\
0b01 - Repetition coding of 2 used \\
0b10 - Repetition coding of 4 used \\
0b11 - Repetition coding of 6 used
\end{tabular} \\
\hline if (AAS or AMC UL Zone) \{ & = & AAS/AMC Allocations include absolute slot offset. \\
\hline Slot offset & 12 bits & Offset from start of the AAS or AMC zone for this allocation, specified in slots. \\
\hline 1 & - & - \\
\hline \} & - & - \\
\hline Padding nibble, if needed & 4 bits & Completing to nearest byte, shall be set to 0 . \\
\hline \} & - & - \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\section*{CID}

Represents the assignment of the IESS to which the IE is assigned.

\section*{UIUC}

UIUC used for the burst.

\section*{OFDMA Szymbol offset}

The offset of the OFDMA symbol in which the burst starts, the offset value is defined in units of OFDMA symbols and is relevant to the Allocation Start Time field given in the UL-MAP message.

\section*{Subchannel offset}

The lowest index subchannel used for carrying the burst, starting from subchannel 0. When allocation of mini-subchannels is used, this offset will always be even numbered and will point to the first subchannel of the couple splitted into mini-subehannels and used in the allocation.

\section*{No. OFDMA Ssymbols}

The number of OFDMA symbols that are used to carry the uplink Burst.

\section*{No. subchannels}

The number of subchannels with subsequent indices.

\section*{Duration}

Indicates the duration, in units of OFDMA slots, of the allocation.

\section*{Repetition coding indication}

Indicates the repetition code used inside the allocated burst. Repetition shall be used only for UIUC indicating QPSK modulation.

When a ranging allocation (UIUC $=12$ ) is present in the UL-MAP, and the SS is in ranging backoff state, it shall count the ranging opportunities present in the ranging region. Only ranging allocations allocated in permutation zones supported by the SS, and matching the type of backoff the SS (ranging or BW request) shall be considered as containing relevant ranging opportunities.

The subchannel offsets in all formats of UL-MAP_IE are referred to logical subchannels before applying the mapping indicated by UL subchannels bitmap in UCD and rotation scheme (see 8.4.6.2.6) for the uplink.

For Sub-UL-DL-MAPs, the current UL zone is automatically reset to the first UL zone at the beginning of a new Sub-MAP. The current UL zone is thereafter updated whenever an UL-MAP IE contains an explicit OFDMA symbol offset.

\section*{Insert new subclause 8.4.5.4.4.2:}

\subsection*{8.4.5.4.4.2 UL-MAP extended-2 IE format}

A UL-MAP IE entry with a UIUC value of 11, indicates that the IE carries special information and conforms to the structure shown in Table 290b. A station shall ignore an extended-2 IE entry with an extended-2 UIUC value for which the station has no knowledge. In the case of a known extended-2 UIUC value but with a length field longer than expected, the station shall process information up to the known length and ignore the remainder of the IE.

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 290b—UL-MAP extended-2 IE format}
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|}
\hline Syntax & Size & Note \\
\hline UL_extended-2_IE() \{ & - & - \\
\hline Extended-2 UIUC & 4 bits & 0x00 ... 0x0F \\
\hline Length & 8 bits & Length in bytes of Unspecified data field \\
\hline Unspecified Data & variable & - \\
\hline \} & - & - \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

Table 290c defines the encoding for Extended-2 UIUC that shall be used by UL-MAP extended-2 IEs.

\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 290c-Extended-2 UIUC Code Assignment for UIUC=11}
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|}
\hline Extended-2 Type (hexadecimal) & Usage \\
\hline 00 & CQICH_Enhanced Allocation_IE \\
\hline 01 & HO_Anchor_Active_UL-MAP_IE \\
\hline 02 & HO_Active Anchor UL MAP \\
\hline 03 & Anchor_BS_switch_IE \\
\hline 04 & UL_sounding_command_IE \\
\hline 05 & Reserved \\
\hline 06 & MIMO UL Enhanced IE \\
\hline 07 & HARQ UL MAP IE \\
\hline 08 & HARQ ACKCH Region Allocation IE \\
\hline 09 ...0D & Reserved \\
\hline 0E & AAS_SDMA_UL_IE \\
\hline 0F & Feedback_polling_IE \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\section*{Power measurement frame}

The 8 LSB of the frame number in which the BS measured the power corrections referred to in the message.

\section*{Change the title of 8.4.5.4.6 as indicated:}

\subsection*{8.4.5.4.6 AAS uplink IE format}

\section*{Change the subclause as indicated:}

Within a frame, the switch from non-AAS to AAS-enabled traffic is marked by using the extended UIUC $=15$ with the AAS_UL_IE() to indicate that the subsequent allocation until the end of the frame shall be for AAS traffic. The AAS_UL_IE defines a UL AAS Zone that spans continuous OFDMA symbols of length defined by the AAS zone length field. Multiple UL AAS Zones can exist within the same frame. When used, the CID in the UL-MAP_IE() shall be set to the broadcast CID. All UL bursts in the AAS portion of the frame may be preceded by an AAS preamble based on the indication in the AAS_UL_IE(). The preamble is defined in 8.4 .9 .4 .3 .48 .4 .4 .6 .4 .2 .

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 293-OFDMA uplink AAS uplink IE}
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|}
\hline Syntax & Size & Notes \\
\hline AAS_UL_IE() \{ & & \\
\hline Extended UIUC & 4 bits & AAS $=0 \mathrm{x} 02$ \\
\hline Length & 4 bits & Length = 0x034 \\
\hline Permutation & 2 bits & \begin{tabular}{l}
0b00 = PUSC permutation \\
0b01 = Optional PUSC permutation \\
0b10 = adjacent-subcarrier permutation \\
0b11 = Reserved
\end{tabular} \\
\hline UL_PermBase & 7 bits & \\
\hline OFDMA symbol offset & 8 bits & \\
\hline AAS zone length & 8 bits & Number of OFDMA symbols in AAS zone \\
\hline \begin{tabular}{l}
Preamble indication \\
Uplink_preamble_config
\end{tabular} & 2 bits & \begin{tabular}{l}
$0 \mathrm{~b} 00=$ No preamble \\
$\theta \mathrm{b} 01$ - Preamble used \\
0b10-0b11-Reserved \\
0b00-0 symbols \\
0b01-1 symbols \\
0b10-2 symbols \\
0b11-3 symbols
\end{tabular} \\
\hline Preamble type & 1 bit & \begin{tabular}{l}
0 - Frequency shifted preamble is used in this UL AAS \\
zone \\
1 - Time shifted preamble is used in this UL AAS zone
\end{tabular} \\
\hline First bin index & 6 bits & When Permutation $=0 \mathrm{~b} 10$, this indicates the index of the first band allocated to this AMC segment \\
\hline Last bin index & 6 bits & When Permutation $=0 \mathrm{~b} 10$, this indicates the index of the last band alloeated to this AMC segment \\
\hline Reserved & 4 bits & Shall be set to zero \\
\hline \} & & \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\section*{Permutation}

Defines the permutation used within the UL AAS Zone
UL PermBase
Permutation Base for specified UL AAS Zone

\section*{OFDMA Symbol offset}

The symbol offset of the UL AAS Zone. This is referenced to the 'Allocation Start Time' field in the UL-MAP.

\section*{Uplink preamble config}

Defines the number of UL AAS preambles to be used before each UL burst in the AAS Zone.
Following AAS IE indicating AMC permutation, the AMC type shall be $2 \times 3$ (2 bins by 3 symbols).

\subsection*{8.4.5.4.7 UL Zone switch IE format}

\section*{Change the first paragraph as indicated:}

In the UL-MAP, a BS may transmit UIUC $=15$ with the UL_ZONE_IE() to indicate that the subsequent allocations shall use a specific permutation. UL_Zone_IE() may appear ahead of all uplink allocation IE and indicate the permutation of the first and the following slots. If UL Zone IE() does not appear ahead of all uplink allocation IEs, tłhe uplink frame shall start in PUSC mode with UL_IDeelHPermBase as indicated in the UCD message. Allocations subsequent to this IE shall use the permutation it instructs. No burst allocation or ranging channel allocation shall span multiple zones.

\section*{Change Table 294 as indicated:}

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 294-OFDMA uplink ZONE IE format}
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|}
\hline Syntax & Size & Notes \\
\hline UL_ZONE_IE() \{ & & \\
\hline Extended ĐUIUC & 4 bits & UL_ZONE $=0 \mathrm{x} 04$ \\
\hline Length & 4 bits & Length = 0x03 \\
\hline OFDMA symbol offset & 7 bits & \\
\hline Permutation & 2 bits & \begin{tabular}{l}
0b00 = PUSC permutation \\
0001 - PUSC permutation \\
0b10 = Optional PUSC permutation \\
0b01 = Optional PUSC permutation \\
0b 410 = Adjacent subcarrier permutation 0b11 = Reserved
\end{tabular} \\
\hline PUSC UL_HeelHPermBase & 7 bits & \\
\hline AMC type & 2 bits & \begin{tabular}{l}
Indicates the AMC type in case permutation type $=0 \mathrm{~b} 10$, otherwise shall be set to 0 . \\
AMC type ( $\mathrm{NxM}=\mathrm{N}$ bins by M symbols): \\
0b00-1x6 \\
0b01-2x3 \\
0b10-3x2 \\
0b11 - Reserved
\end{tabular} \\
\hline Use All SC indicator & 1 bit & \begin{tabular}{l}
$0=$ Do not use all subchannels \\
1 = Use all subchannels
\end{tabular} \\
\hline Reserved & 5 bits & Shall be set to zero \\
\hline \} & & \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\section*{Insert the following at the end of 8.4.5.4.7}

This IE should not be used within SUB-DL-UL-MAP.

\section*{Change the definition of 'Permutation' field below Table 294 as indicated:}

\section*{Permutation}

Indicates the permutation that shall be used by the transmitter for allocations following this IE. Permutation changes are only allowed on a zone boundary. The UL_IDcellPermBase indicated by the IE shall be used as the basis of the permutation (see 8.4.6.2.2, 8.4.6.2.3).

\section*{Insert the following text after the end of Table 294:}

\section*{Use all SC indicator}

When the 'Use All SC indicator' is set to 0, subchannels indicated by allocated subchannel bitmap in UCD shall be used. Otherwise, all subchannels shall be used.

\subsection*{8.4.5.4.8 Mini-subchannel allocation IE}

\section*{Change Table 295 as indicated:}

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 295-Mini-subchannel allocation IE format}
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|}
\hline Syntax & Size & Notes \\
\hline Mini_subchannel_allocation_IE() \{ & & \\
\hline Extended ĐUIUC & 4 bits & Mini_subchannel_allocation $=0 \times 01$ \\
\hline Length & 4 bits & \begin{tabular}{l}
Length $(\mathrm{M})=0 \mathrm{x} 037$ if $M=2$ \\
0x049 if $M=3$ \\
$0 \times 06$ f if $M=6$
\end{tabular} \\
\hline CType & 2 bits & \begin{tabular}{l}
0b00-2 mini-subchannels (defines $M=2$ ) \\
0b01-2 mini-subchannels (defines $M=2$ ) \\
0b10-3 mini-subchannels (defines $M=3$ ) \\
0b11-6 mini-subchannels (defines $M=6$ )
\end{tabular} \\
\hline Duration & 106 bits & In OFDMA slots \\
\hline For ( $j=0 ; j<M ; j++$ ) \{ & & \\
\hline CID(j) & 16 bits & \\
\hline UIUC(j) & 4 bits & Allowed values are 1-10 \\
\hline Repetition(j) & 2 bits & \begin{tabular}{l}
Indicates the repetition code used inside the allocated burst for minisubchannel with index $j$ 0b00 - No repetition coding \\
0b01 - Repetition coding of 2 used \\
0b10 - Repetition coding of 4 used \\
0b11 - Repetition coding of 6 used \\
Repetition shall be used only for $\mathrm{M}=2$ or $\mathrm{M}=3$
\end{tabular} \\
\hline \} & & \\
\hline Padding & $n$ bits & \begin{tabular}{l}
Padding bits shall be set to zero \\
$n=0$ if $M=2$ \\
24 if $M=3$ \\
0 if $M=6$
\end{tabular} \\
\hline \} & & \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\subsection*{8.4.5.4.9 FAST-FEEDBACK message mapping}

\section*{Change the subclause as indicated:}

Each FAST-FEEDBACK message occupies one UL slot. FAST-FEEDBACK messages are mapped in to the region marked by UIUC $=0$ in the UL-MAP, in a timefrequency-first order, as shown in Figure 230.

\section*{Replace Figure 230 with the following figure:}

UL frame region for

\end{figure}

\section*{Insert the following text at the end of the subclause:}

The FAST-FEEDBACK region shall be allocated using the FAST-FEEDBACK allocation IE as defined in Table 295a.

\hline FASTFEEDBACK_allocation_IE() \{ & & \\

\hline Subchannel offset & 7 bits & \\

\hline No subchannels & 7 bits & \\

\section*{Change the title of 8.4.5.4.10 as indicated:}

\subsection*{8.4.5.4.10 FAST_FEEDBACK Fast-feedback channels}

\section*{Change the second sentence of the first paragraph as indicated:}

The allocations are done in unicast manner through the FAST_FEEDBACK MAC subheader (see 6.3.2.2.6), CQICH Control IE (see 6.3.2.3.43.5) or CQICH Allocation IE (see 8.4.5.4.12), and the transmission takes place in a specific UL region designated by UIUC $=0$.

\section*{Change the paragraph below Table 296 as indicated:}

The fast-feedback code words used in Table 296 belong to a set of orthogonal vectors and are mapped directly to the data subcarriers of a tile in frequency first order starting from the first OFDMA symbol (see 8.4.9.4.2), where subcarriers(0) is the lowest numbered data subcarrier in the tile, and the tile indices are defined in Equation (113) for PUSC and Equation (115) for optional PUSC by the permution (see 8.4.6.2). The vectors are defined in Table 297.

\section*{Replace Equation (106) with the following equation:}
$$
\begin{align*}
& P 0=\exp \left(j \cdot \frac{\pi}{4}\right) \\
& P 1=\exp \left(j \cdot \frac{3 \pi}{4}\right) \\
& P 2=\exp \left(-j \cdot \frac{3 \pi}{4}\right)  \tag{106}\\
& P 3=\exp \left(-j \cdot \frac{\pi}{4}\right)
\end{align*}
$$

\section*{Change the last paragraph as indicated:}

The fast feedback slot includes 4 bits of payload data, whose encoding depended on the instruction given in the FAST_FEEDBACK subheader, CQICH Control IE and CQICH Allocation IE. The following subclauses define these encodings.

\subsection*{8.4.5.4.10.1 Fast DL measurement feedback}

\section*{Insert the following text at the beginning of subclause 8.4.5.4.10.1:}

MIMO capable MS shall measure post processing CINR for each individual layers as shown in Figure 230a. When the FAST FEEDBACK subheader Feedback Type field is "00", the MS shall report the post processing average CINR (Avg_CINR) as defined in Equation (106a). When BS requests MS feedback through CQICH_Alloc_IE() or CQICH_Enhanced_Alloc_IE() with '00' feedback type field, MS shall report Avg_CINR or individual layer CINR as described in 8.4.5.4.12 and 8.4.5.4.16.

For vertically encoded MIMO system, defined the averaged CINR (Avg_CINR) as :
$$
\begin{equation*}
\text { Avg_CINR }=e^{C(d, y \mid H)}-1 \tag{106a}
\end{equation*}
$$
where $C(d, y \mid H)$ is the receiver-constrained mutual information conditioned on knowing the channel knowledge. Note that $d$ is the transmitted signal, $y$ is the post-processing receive signal and $H$ is the channel matrix
between transmit and receive antennas. For LMMSE receiver, the individual post-detector-processing signal to noise ratios are given as $\mathrm{CINR}_{1}, \ldots, \mathrm{CINR}_{\underline{\mathrm{N}}}$, as shown in Figure 230a and in Equation (106b).
$$
\begin{equation*}
C(d, y \mid H)=\frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=1}^{N} \log \left(1+C I N R_{n}\right) \tag{106~b}
\end{equation*}
$$

In this case,
$$
\begin{equation*}
\text { Avg_CINR }=\left(\prod_{n=1}^{N}\left(1+\text { CINR }_{n}\right)\right)^{1 / N}-1, \tag{106c}
\end{equation*}
$$
when the individual post-detector processing CINR is high, the average CINR is
$$
\begin{equation*}
\underline{A v g \_C I N R \approx}=\frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=1}^{N} C I N R_{n}(\mathrm{in} \mathrm{~dB}) \tag{106~d}
\end{equation*}
$$

For ML MIMO detectors case,
$$
\begin{equation*}
C(d, y \mid H)=\frac{1}{N} \log \operatorname{det}\left(I_{N}+H^{H} R^{-1} H\right) \tag{106e}
\end{equation*}
$$
where
$\underline{I}_{N}$ is an $N$ by $N$ identity matrix,
$\underline{R}$ is the correlation matrix of interference plus noise measured at MS.

\section*{Change 8.4.5.4.10.1 as indicated:}

When the FAST_FEEDBACK subheader Feedback Type field is " 00 " the SS shall report the SAN it measures on the DL. Equation (107) shall be used:
$$
\text { Payload bits nibble }=\left\{\begin{array}{cc}
0, & S / N<-2 d B  \tag{107}\\
n, & 2 \cdot n-4<S / N<2 \cdot n-2, \quad 0<n<15 \\
15, & S / N>26 d B
\end{array}\right.
$$

When the Feedback_type field in CQICH_Enhanced_Alloc_IE() is 0 b 000 with CQICH type 0b101, Equation (107) shall be used:
$$
\underline{\text { Payload bits nibble }}=\left\{\begin{array}{cc}
0, & S / N \leq 1-B  \tag{107}\\
n, & (2 n-1-B)<S / N \leq(2 n+1-B), \quad 0<n<15 \\
15, & S / N>29-B
\end{array}\right.
$$
where B is the positive integer value indicated in the SN Reporting Base TLV (see 11.7.26). B shall default to "3" if the SN Reporting Base TLV was not included in the REG-RSP.

\section*{Insert the following at the end of 8.4.5.4.10.1:}

For an MS that supports the feedback method by using Feedback header, if $M$ is defined as the indication flag in UCD, the MS shall set the payload bits nibble as M-1 instead of M, if the outcome of payload bits nibble calculation based on Equation (107) is $M$.

For band AMC operation, the SS shall report differential of CINR of four selected bands (increment: 1 and decrement: 0 with a step of 1 dB ) on its fast-feedback channel.

MIMO capable SS shall measure post processing CINR for each individual layers as shown in Figure 230a. The SS shall report the post processing CINR averaged over layers. When the BS requests SS feedback through CQICH_Alloc_IE() SS shall report average CINR or individual layer CINR as described in 8.4.5.4.12. The layer is defined as a separate coding/modulation path.

\section*{Insert new Figure 230a:}

\begin{figure}
\includegraphics[alt={},max width=\textwidth]{https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/a227cdd5-cf57-46a1-bc9f-bb964def4187-483.jpg?height=463&width=1509&top_left_y=867&top_left_x=300}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Figure 230a-Post processed CINR for MIMO region}
\end{figure}

For SS with more than one receive antennas, Equation (107a) shall be used:
$$
\text { Payload bits Nibble }=\left\{\begin{array}{lll}
0 & C I N R<(-2-\Delta d B)  \tag{107a}\\
n & 2 n-2-\Delta d B<C I N R<(2 n-2-\Delta d B) & 0<n<15 \\
15 & C I N R>26-\Delta d B &
\end{array}\right.
$$
where
$\underline{\Delta}=10 \log (N r)$ for the cases of single transmit antenna BS or 2 and 4 transmit antenna BS using matrix A transmission format,
$\underline{\Delta}=10 \log (N r / 2)$ for the case of 2 and 4 transmit antennas BS using matrix B transmission format. Nr is the number of receive antennas.

\subsection*{8.4.5.4.10.2 Fast MIMO feedback}

\section*{Insert the following text at the end of 8.4.5.4.10.2:}

When CQI Feedback Type field in CQICH_Enhanced_Alloc_IE() (see 8.4.5.4.15) is 0b011 and CQICH type is 0 b 101 , the MS shall report the MIMO coefficient the BS should use for best DL reception. The mapping for the complex weights is shown in Figure 231. For this type of feedback, if $N$ is the number of BS transmit antennas, then $(N-1)$ CQICH shall be allocated to the MS and MS shall report the desired antenna weights of antenna 1 through $N-1$ based on antenna 0 .

\subsection*{8.4.5.4.10.3 Mode Selection Feedback}

\section*{Change the first sentence of the first paragraph as indicated:}

For an SS that supports the STC option (see 8.4.8), Wwhen the FAST-FEEDBACK subheader Feedback Type field is "11" or at a specific frame indicated in the CQICH_Alloc_IE(), the SS shall send its selection in terms of MIMO mode (STTD versus SM) or permutation mode on the assigned FAST-FEEDBACK channel.

\section*{Insert new subclause 8.4.5.4.10.4:}

\subsection*{8.4.5.4.10.4 Effective CINR feedback for fast-feedback channel}

When the feedback type field in the CQICH_IE() is 0 b 01 or the effective CINR report is request by REPREQ, the SS shall report the effective CINR, as defined in 6.3.18, according to Table 298b. To avoid ambiguity, both the BS and the SS must know the FEC type assumed for this report. The FEC type assumed for the MCS column is the first FEC type in the table in 11.8.3.7.2 for which the SS and BS have successfully negotiated the capability exchange. If none of the FEC types in 11.8.3.7.2 have been successfully negotiated, then the mandatory FEC type shall be assumed.

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 298b-Effective CINR feedback encoding}
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|}
\hline Label & Encoding & MCS \\
\hline 0 & 0b0000 & QPSK 1/2, repetition 6 \\
\hline 1 & 0b0001 & QPSK 1/2, repetition 4 \\
\hline 2 & 0b0010 & QPSK 1/2, repetition 2 \\
\hline 3 & 0b0011 & QPSK 1/2 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 298b-Effective CINR feedback encoding (continued)}
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|}
\hline Label & Encoding & MCS \\
\hline 4 & 0b0100 & QPSK 3/4 \\
\hline 5 & 0b0101 & 16-QAM 1/2 \\
\hline 6 & 0b0110 & 16-QAM 3/4 \\
\hline 7 & 0b0111 & 64-QAM 1/2 \\
\hline 8 & 0b1000 & 64-QAM 2/3 \\
\hline 9 & 0b1001 & 64-QAM 3/4 \\
\hline 10 & 0b1010 & 64-QAM 5/6 \\
\hline 11 & 0b1011 & A decrease in CQICH duration is recommended (effective CINR has not changed from previous CQICH slot). This encoding shall not be repeated over consecutive CQI slots. \\
\hline 12-15 & 0b1100-0b1111 & Reserved \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\section*{Insert new subclause 8.4.5.4.10.5}

\subsection*{8.4.5.4.10.5 Enhanced Fast-feedback channels}

Enhanced Fast-feedback slots may be individually allocated to an MS for transmission of PHY related information that requires fast response from the MS. The allocations are done either in a unicast manner through the Fast-feedback allocation subheader (see 6.3.2.2.6), or through the CQICH_Control IE() (see 6.3.2.3.43.5), or through the CQICH_Alloc_IE() (see 8.4.5.4.12), or through the CQICH_Enhanced_Alloc_IE() (see 8.4.5.4.16), or through the MIMO Compact DL-MAP IE() (see 6.3.2.3.43.6.7), Dedicated MIMO DL Control IE (8.4.5.3.21.1), AAS_SDMA_DL_IE (8.4.5.4.26). The transmission takes place in a specific UL region designated by UIUC $=0$.

Each 3-bit MIMO Fast-feedback slot consists of $1 / 2$ OFDMA slots mapped in a manner similar to the mapping of ACK Channel. An Enhanced Fast-feedback slot uses QPSK modulation on the 24 data subcarriers it contains, and can carry a data payload of 3 bits. Table 298c defines the mapping between the payload bit sequences and the subcarriers modulation.

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 298c- 3-bit MIMO Fast-feedback channel subcarrier modulation}
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|}
\hline 3-bit payload (binary) & Fast Feedback vector indices per Tile Even $=\{\operatorname{Tile}(0)$, Tile(2),Tile(4)\} or Odd $=\{$ Tile(1), Tile(3),Tile(5)\} \\
\hline 000 & 0,0,0 \\
\hline 001 & 1,1,1 \\
\hline 010 & 2,2,2 \\
\hline 011 & 3,3,3 \\
\hline 100 & 4,4,4 \\
\hline 101 & 5,5,5 \\
\hline 110 & 6,6,6 \\
\hline 111 & 7,7,7 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

Each Enhanced Fast-feedback slot consists of 1 OFDMA slots mapped in a manner similar to the mapping of normal uplink data. An Enhanced Fast-feedback slot uses QPSK modulation on the 48 data subcarriers it contains and can carry a data payload of 6 bits. Table 298d defines the mapping between the payload bit sequences and the subcarriers modulation.

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 298d-Enhanced Fast-feedback channel subcarrier modulation}
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|}
\hline 6-bit Payload (binary) & Fast-feedback vector indices per Tile Tile(0), Tile(1), ... Tile(5) & 6-bit Payload (binary) & Fast-feedback vector indices per Tile Tile(0), Tile(1), ... Tile(5) \\
\hline 000000 & 0,0,0,0,0,0 & 100000 & 6,7,5,1,2,4 \\
\hline 000001 & 1,1,1,1,1,1 & 100001 & 7,6,4,0,3,5 \\
\hline 000010 & 2,2,2,2,2,2 & 100010 & 4,5,7,3,0,6 \\
\hline 000011 & 3,3,3,3,3,3 & 100011 & 5,4,6,2,1,7 \\
\hline 000100 & 4,4,4,4,4,4 & 100100 & 2,3,1,5,6,0 \\
\hline 000101 & 5,5,5,5,5,5 & 100101 & 3,2,0,4,7,1 \\
\hline 000110 & 6,6,6,6,6,6 & 100110 & 0,1,3,7,4,2 \\
\hline 000111 & 7,7,7,7,7,7 & 100111 & 1,0,2,6,5,3 \\
\hline 001000 & 2,4,3,6,7,5 & 101000 & 7,5,1,2,4,3 \\
\hline 001001 & 3,5,2,7,6,4 & 101001 & 6,4,0,3,5,2 \\
\hline 001010 & 0,6,1,4,5,7 & 101010 & 5,7,3,0,6,1 \\
\hline 001011 & 1,7,0,5,4,6 & 101011 & 4,6,2,1,7,0 \\
\hline 001100 & 6,0,7,2,3,1 & 101100 & 3,1,5,6,0,7 \\
\hline 001101 & 7,1,6,3,2,0 & 101101 & 2,0,4,7,1,6 \\
\hline 001110 & 4,2,5,0,1,3 & 101110 & 1,3,7,4,2,5 \\
\hline 001111 & 5,3,4,1,0,2 & 101111 & 0,2,6,5,3,4 \\
\hline 010000 & 4,3,6,7,5,1 & 110000 & 5,1,2,4,3,6 \\
\hline 010001 & 5,2,7,6,4,0 & 110001 & 4,0,3,5,2,7 \\
\hline 010010 & 6,1,4,5,7,3 & 110010 & 7,3,0,6,1,4 \\
\hline 010011 & 7,0,5,4,6,2 & 110011 & 6,2,1,7,0,5 \\
\hline 010100 & 0,7,2,3,1,5 & 110100 & 1,5,6,0,7,2 \\
\hline 010101 & 1,6,3,2,0,4 & 110101 & 0,4,7,1,6,3 \\
\hline 010110 & 2,5,0,1,3,7 & 110110 & 3,7,4,2,5,0 \\
\hline 010111 & 3,4,1,0,2,6 & 110111 & 2,6,5,3,4,1 \\
\hline 011000 & 3,6,7,5,1,2 & 111000 & 1,2,4,3,6,7 \\
\hline 011001 & 2,7,6,4,0,3 & 111001 & 0,3,5,2,7,6 \\
\hline 011010 & 1,4,5,7,3,0 & 111010 & 3,0,6,1,4,5 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 298d-Enhanced Fast-feedback channel subcarrier modulation (continued)}
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|}
\hline 6-bit Payload (binary) & Fast-feedback vector indices per Tile Tile(0), Tile(1), ... Tile(5) & 6-bit Payload (binary) & Fast-feedback vector indices per Tile Tile(0), Tile(1), ... Tile(5) \\
\hline 011011 & 0,5,4,6,2,1 & 111011 & 2,1,7,0,5,4 \\
\hline 011100 & 7,2,3,1,5,6 & 111100 & 5,6,0,7,2,3 \\
\hline 011101 & 6,3,2,0,4,7 & 111101 & 4,7,1,6,3,2 \\
\hline 011110 & 5,0,1,3,7,4 & 111110 & 7,4,2,5,0,1 \\
\hline 011111 & 4,1,0,2,6,5 & 111111 & 6,5,3,4,1,0 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

The Fast-feedback channel is orthogonally modulated with QPSK symbols. Let $M_{n, 8 m+k}(0 \leq k \leq 7)$ be the modulation symbol index of the $k$-th modulation symbol in the $m$-th uplink tile of the $n$-th Fast-feedback channel. The possible modulation patterns composed of $M_{n, m 8}, M_{n, 8 m+1}, \ldots M_{n, 8 m+7}$ the $m$-th tile of the $n$-th Fast-feedback channel are defined in Table 298e.

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 298e-Orthogonal Modulation Index in FAST_FEEDBACK Fast-feedback Gchannel}
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|}
\hline Vector index & $M_{n, m 8}, M_{n, 8 m+1}, \ldots M_{n, 8 m+7}$ \\
\hline 0 & P0, P1, P2, P3, P0, P1, P2, P3 \\
\hline 1 & P0, P3, P2, P1, P0, P3, P2, P1 \\
\hline 2 & P0, P0, P1, P1, P2, P2, P3, P3 \\
\hline 3 & P0, P0, P3, P3, P2, P2, P1, P1 \\
\hline 4 & P0, P0, P0, P0, P0, P0, P0, P0 \\
\hline 5 & P0, P2, P0, P2, P0, P2, P0, P2 \\
\hline 6 & P0, P2, P0, P2, P2, P0, P2, P0 \\
\hline 7 & P0, P2, P2, P0, P2, P0, P0, P2 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}
where
$$
\begin{aligned}
& P 0=\exp \left(j \cdot \frac{\pi}{4}\right) \\
& P 1=\exp \left(j \cdot \frac{3 \pi}{4}\right) \\
& P 2=\exp \left(-j \cdot \frac{3 \pi}{4}\right) \\
& P 3=\exp \left(-j \cdot \frac{\pi}{4}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$
$M_{n, 8 m+k}$ is mapped to a Fast-feedback channel tile, as shown in Figure 231a, for PUSC uplink subchannel, and in Figure 231b, for optional PUSC uplink subchannel. A Fast-feedback channel is mapped to one subchannel composed of six tiles.

\begin{figure}
\includegraphics[alt={},max width=\textwidth]{https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/a227cdd5-cf57-46a1-bc9f-bb964def4187-489.jpg?height=538&width=912&top_left_y=363&top_left_x=597}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Figure 231a-Subcarrier mapping of Fast-feedback modulation symbols for PUSC}
\end{figure}

\begin{figure}
\includegraphics[alt={},max width=\textwidth]{https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/a227cdd5-cf57-46a1-bc9f-bb964def4187-489.jpg?height=553&width=712&top_left_y=1224&top_left_x=674}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Figure 231b-Subcarrier mapping of Fast-feedback modulation symbols for optional PUSC}
\end{figure}

The Enhanced Fast-feedback slot includes 6 bits of payload data, whose encoding depends on the instruction given in the Fast-feedback subheader, the CQICH_Control IE(), the CQICH_Alloc_IE(), or through the CQICH_Enhanced_Alloc_IE(). The following subclauses define these encodings.

\section*{Insert new subclause 8.4.5.4.10.6:}

\subsection*{8.4.5.4.10.6 Fast DL measurement feedback for Enhanced Fast-feedback channel}

When the Fast-feedback allocation subheader Feedback Type field is 0 b 00 or the feedback is requested through CQICH_Alloc_IE (see 8.4.5.4.12), or the Feedback_type field in CQICH_Enhanced_Alloc_IE() is 0b000-0b010 with CQICH type 0 b 000 or 0 b 100 (see 8.4.5.4.15), the MS shall report the SNR it measures on the DL. Equation (107b) shall be used.
$$
\text { Payload bits }=\left\{\begin{array}{cc}
0, & S / N \leq-\mathrm{B}  \tag{107b}\\
n, & (n-1-B)<(S / N) \leq(n-B), 0<n<31 \\
31, & S / N>30-\mathrm{B}
\end{array}\right.
$$
where B is the positive integer value indicated in the SN Reporting Base TLV (see 11.7.26). B shall default to " 3 " if the SN Reporting Base TLV was not included in the REG-RSP.

For MIMO capable MSs, if the BS allocates a single CQICH to the MS in UL-MAP (CQICH_Num $=0$ ) for the purposes of Fast DL Measurement, the MS shall report the effective post processing SNR Eff_SNR as defined in 8.4.5.4.10.1. Otherwise, if the BS allocate multiple CQICHs to the MS in UL-MAP (CQICH_Num $>0$ ) for the purposes of Fast DL Measurement, the MS shall report post processing S/N of individual layers in order of layer indices.

Insert new subclause 8.4.5.4.10.7

\subsection*{8.4.5.4.10.7 Fast MIMO feedback of Quantized Precoding Weight for Enhanced Fast-feedback channel}

When the Fast-feedback allocation subheader Feedback Type field is 0 b 01 or 0 b 10 , or the CQI Type field in the MIMO Compact DL-MAP IE() (see 6.3.2.3.43.6.7) is 0b01, or the Feedback Type field in CQICH_Enhanced_Alloc_IE() (see 8.4.5.4.15) is 0 b 011 with CQICH type 0 b 000 or 0 b 100 , the MS shall report the MIMO coefficient the BS should use for best DL reception. The mapping for the complex weights is shown in Figure 231c. For this type of feedback, if $N$ is the number of BS transmit antennas, then ( $N-1$ ) CQICH shall be allocated to the SS and SS shall report the desired antenna weights of antenna 1 through $N-1$ based on antenna 0 .

\begin{figure}
\includegraphics[alt={},max width=\textwidth]{https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/a227cdd5-cf57-46a1-bc9f-bb964def4187-491.jpg?height=1372&width=1359&top_left_y=300&top_left_x=373}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Figure 231c-Mapping of MIMO coefficients for quantized precoding weights for enhanced fast MIMO feedback payload bits}
\end{figure}

\section*{Insert new subclause 8.4.5.4.10.8:}

\subsection*{8.4.5.4.10.8 MIMO Mode Feedback for Enhanced Fast-feedback channel}

When the Enhanced Fast-feedback channel is employed, the SS may report the MIMO mode feedback on the assigned CQICH when the Fast-feedback allocation subheader Feedback Type field is 0 b 00 ; or the Feedback Type field in the MIMO Compact DL-MAP IE() (see 6.3.2.3.43.6.7) is $0 \mathrm{~b} 000,0 \mathrm{~b} 001$, or 0 b 010 ; or the Feedback Type field in CQICH_Enhanced_Alloc_IE() (see 8.4.5.4.15) is $0 \mathrm{~b} 000,0 \mathrm{~b} 001$, or 0 b 010 with CQICH type 0b000 or 0b100. The encoding of payload bits is shown in Table 298f.

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 298f-Encoding of payload bits for MIMO Mode Feedback with Enhanced Fast-feedback Channel}
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|}
\hline Value (binary) & Description \\
\hline 101000 & STTD and PUSC/FUSC permutation \\
\hline 101001 & STTD and adjacent-subcarrier permutation \\
\hline 101010 & SM and PUSC/FUSC permutation \\
\hline 101011 & SM and adjacent-subcarrier permutation \\
\hline 101100 & Hybrid and PUSC/FUSC permutation \\
\hline 101101 & Hybrid and adjacent-subcarrier permutation \\
\hline 101110-110110 & Interpretation according to Table 298g, Table 298h or Table 298i, depending on if antenna grouping, antenna selection or a reduced precoding matrix codebook is used. \\
\hline 110111 & Closed loop precoding with 1 stream. \\
\hline 111000 & Closed loop precoding with 2 streams. \\
\hline 111001 & Closed loop precoding with 3 streams. \\
\hline 111010 & Closed loop precoding with 4 streams. \\
\hline 111011-111111 & Reserved \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\section*{Clarification of streams concept:}

The number of streams is the number of outputs from the space-time code.

\end{tabular} \\

\section*{Insert new subclause 8.4.5.4.10.9:}

\subsection*{8.4.5.4.10.9 Anchor BS report}

The MS may send its Anchor BS selection using the 8 codewords numbered from 32 to 39. Table 298j shows the encoding of payload bits for the Fast-feedback slot (see 8.4.5.4.9).

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 298j-Encoding of payload bits for Fast-feedback slot}
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|}
\hline Value (binary) & Description \\
\hline 100000 & Anchor BS for TEMP_BS_ID=0b000 \\
\hline 100001 & Anchor BS for TEMP_BS_ID=0b001 \\
\hline 100010 & Anchor BS for TEMP_BS_ID=0b010 \\
\hline 100011 & Anchor BS for TEMP_BS_ID=0b011 \\
\hline 100100 & Anchor BS for TEMP_BS_ID=0b100 \\
\hline 100101 & Anchor BS for TEMP_BS_ID=0b101 \\
\hline 100110 & Anchor BS for TEMP_BS_ID=0b110 \\
\hline 100111 & Anchor BS for TEMP_BS_ID=0b111 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

Also, the 40th codeword (i.e., 0b101000) is used as an acknowledgement for the Anchor_BS_Switch_IE.

When the 4 -by-3 uplink tile structure is used (see 8.4.6.2.1), the number of tiles in a channel, $N$, is 6 and the number of subcarriers in a tile, $L$, is 12 . When the 3-by-3 uplink tile structure is used (see 8.4.6.5.1), $N=6$ and $L=9$.

When the MS reports the measured S/N, each payload bit is repeated according to the predefined UEP ratio $R_{0}: R_{1}: R_{2}: R_{3}$, where $R_{0}, R_{1}, R_{2}$, and $R_{3}$ represent the repetition number for the first payload bit $b_{0}$ (MSB), the second payload bit $b_{1}$, the third payload bit $b_{2}$, and the fourth payload bit $b_{3}$ (LSB), respectively. In case of the 4-bit CQI payload, a ratio of $R_{0}: R_{1}: R_{2}: R_{3}=26: 19: 14: 7$ is used for the 4-by-3 uplink tile structure, and $R_{0}: R_{1}: R_{2}: R_{3}=19: 14: 10: 5$ is used for the 3-by-3 uplink tile structure.

The repeated bit sequence is interleaved according to Equation (107c) before binary DPSK modulation.
$$
\begin{equation*}
y=((x R) / N) \bmod (R)+\lfloor x / N\rfloor, \tag{107c}
\end{equation*}
$$
where
$y$ denotes the bit index in the interleaved bit sequence $(y=0,1,2, c, R-1)$,
$x$ denotes bit index in the repeated bit sequence $(x=0,1,2, c, R-1)$.
The length of the repeated bit sequence is $R=R_{0}+R_{1}+R_{2}+R_{3}=N(L-1)=66$ for the 4-bit CQI. The interleaved bit sequence is divided into $N$ groups and each group has $L-1$ bits. The $n$-th group ( $n=0,1, c_{N-1}$ ) is used for binary DPSK modulation on the subcarriers in the $n$-th uplink tile, as shown in the Figure 208b. The first subcarrier in each tile is used as a phase reference. The $L-1$ bits in the $n$-th ${ }^{h}$ group are mapped to $L$ DPSK symbols for the $n$-th tile as follows:
$$
C_{n, k}^{C Q I}=\left\{\begin{array}{cl}
1 & (k=0)  \tag{107~d}\\
C_{n, k-1}^{C Q I} & k>0 \quad \text { and }\left(B_{n, k-1}^{C Q I}=0\right) \\
-C_{n, k-1}^{C Q I} & k>0 \quad \text { and }\left(B_{n, k-1}^{C Q I}=1\right)
\end{array}\right.
$$
where
$C_{n, k}^{C Q I}$ mapping symbol of the $k$-th subcarrier of the $n$-th tile $(k=0,1, \ldots, L-1)$,
$B_{n, k}^{C Q I} k$-th bit of $n$-th group in the interleaved bit sequence $(k=0,1, \ldots, L-2)$.

\section*{Insert new subclause 8.4.5.4.10.10:}

\subsection*{8.4.5.4.10.10 UEP fast-feedback}

When the UEP fast-feedback is employed and the Fast-feedback allocation subheader Feedback type field is '00' or the BS requests the feedback through CQICH_Alloc_IE() or CQICH_Control IE(), the MS may report the feedback payload on the assigned CQICH by using the following UEP fast-feedback method. The UEP fast-feedback provides the payload bits carried by the fast-feedback channel with the unequal error protection (UEP) capability. The UEP fast-feedback repeats each payload bit according to a predefined repetition ratio, as illustrated in Figure 231d. The repeated bit sequence is interleaved and used for binary DPSK modulation on the subcarriers for the fast-feedback channel.

\begin{figure}
\includegraphics[alt={},max width=\textwidth]{https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/a227cdd5-cf57-46a1-bc9f-bb964def4187-496.jpg?height=1357&width=1431&top_left_y=285&top_left_x=320}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Figure 231d-Mapping of the payload bit sequence to a Fast-feedback channel}
\end{figure}

\section*{Insert new subclause 8.4.5.4.10.11:}

\subsection*{8.4.5.4.10.11 Band AMC differential CINR feedback for Enhanced Fast-feedback channel}

When the Band AMC operation is triggered, the SS shall report the differential of CINR for five selected bands (increment: 1 and decrement: 0 with a step of 1 dB ) on its enhanced or primary fast-feedback channel. The first 32 codewords with MSB of 0 are used.

Insert new subclause 8.4.5.4.10.12

\subsection*{8.4.5.4.10.12 Indication Flag Feedback}

For an MS that supports the feedback method using the Feedback header, the MS can send an indication flag on the Fast-feedback channel, the Enhanced Fast-feedback channel, or the primary/secondary Fast-feedback channel. The indication flag is a specific encoding of the payload bits on the Fast-feedback channel or the Enhanced Fast-feedback channel. The indication flag is used by the MS to indicate to the BS its intention to transmit a Feedback header or a Bandwidth Request header without the need to perform bandwidth request
ranging. After receiving the indication flag from the MS, the BS may allocate the required UL resource to the MS.

For the case of Fast-feedback channel or secondary Fast-feedback changeful the Indication Flag feedback operation is enabled, the specific encoding of the payload bits is defined in the Use CQICH indication flag TLV. This specific encoding is reserved for the purpose of indication flag and shall not be used to send other feedback information (see 8.4.5.4.10.1).

For the case of Enhanced Fast-feedback channel or primary Fast-feedback channel, the encoding of 0b111100 shall be used as the indication flag.

\section*{Insert new subclause 8.4.5.4.10.13}

\subsection*{8.4.5.4.10.13 Primary and secondary Fast-feedback channels}

A primary Fast-feedback slot consists of 1 OFDMA slots mapped in a manner similar to the mapping of normal uplink data. A primary Fast-feedback slot uses QPSK modulation on the 48 data subcarriers of UL PUSC tiles it contains, and can carry a data payload of 6 bits. The primary Fast-feedback slot has identical mapping between the payload bit sequences and the subcarriers modulation as the Enhanced Fast-feedback 6-bit payload slot except null pilot subcarriers within the slot. (See Figure 231e.)

\begin{figure}
\includegraphics[alt={},max width=\textwidth]{https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/a227cdd5-cf57-46a1-bc9f-bb964def4187-497.jpg?height=592&width=916&top_left_y=1168&top_left_x=586}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Figure 231e-Subcarrier mapping of Primary Fast-feedback modulation symbol for PUSC}
\end{figure}

A secondary Fast-feedback slot consists of 1 OFDMA slot mapped in a manner similar to the mapping of normal uplink data. A secondary Fast-feedback slot uses QPSK modulation on the 24 pilot subcarriers of UL PUSC tiles it contains, and can carry a data payload of 4 bits. Table 298k defines the mapping between the payload bit sequences and the subcarriers modulation.

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 298k-Secondary Fast-feedback channel subcarrier modulation with 4 bit}
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|}
\hline Four-bit payload (binary) & Vector indices per tile Tile(0), Tile(1),Tile(2), Tile(3),Tile(4), Tile(5) & Four-bit payload (binary) & Vector indices per tile Tile(0), Tile(1),Tile(2), Tile(3),Tile(4), Tile(5) \\
\hline 0000 & 0,0,0,1,1,1 & 1000 & 0,0,1,3,2,2 \\
\hline 0001 & 1,1,1,0,0,0 & 1001 & 1,3,2,2,3,1 \\
\hline 0010 & 2,2,2,3,3,3 & 1010 & 2,2,3,1,0,0 \\
\hline 0011 & 3,3,3,2,2,2 & 1011 & 3,3,1,0,1,1 \\
\hline 0100 & 0,1,2,3,0,1 & 1100 & 0,0,3,2,0,3 \\
\hline 0101 & 1,2,3,0,1,3 & 1101 & 1,2,0,2,2,0 \\
\hline 0110 & 2,3,0,1,2,3 & 1110 & 2,1,3,3,1,2 \\
\hline 0111 & 3,0,1,2,3,0 & 1111 & 3,2,2,1,1,2 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

The secondary Fast-feedback channel is orthogonally modulated with QPSK symbols. Let $\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{n}, 4 \mathrm{~m}+\mathrm{k}}(0<=k <=3$ ) be the modulation symbol index of the $k$-th modulation symbol in the $m$-th uplink PUSC tile of the $n$-th secondary Fast-feedback channel. The possible modulation patterns composed of $\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{n}, 4 \mathrm{~m}+\mathrm{k}}$ in the $m$-th tile of the $n$-th secondary Fast-feedback channel are defined in Table 2981.

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 298I—Orthogonal Modulation Index in Secondary Fast-feedback Channel}
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|}
\hline Vector index & $\mathbf{M}_{\mathrm{n}, 4 \mathrm{~m}}, \mathbf{M}_{\mathrm{n}, 4 \mathrm{~m}+1}, \mathbf{M}_{\mathrm{n}, 4 \mathrm{~m}+2}, \mathbf{M}_{\mathrm{n}, 4 \mathrm{~m}+3}$ \\
\hline 0 & P0, P0, P0, P0 \\
\hline 1 & P0, P2, P0, P2 \\
\hline 2 & P0, P1, P2, P3 \\
\hline 3 & P1, P0, P3, P2 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}
where
$$
\begin{aligned}
& P_{0}=\exp \left(j \frac{\pi}{4}\right) \\
& P_{1}=\exp \left(j \frac{3 \pi}{4}\right) \\
& P_{2}=\exp \left(-j \frac{3 \pi}{4}\right) \\
& P_{3}=\exp \left(-j \frac{\pi}{4}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$
$\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{n}, 4 \mathrm{~m}+\mathrm{k}}$ are mapped to secondary Fast-feedback channel tile as shown in Figure 231f for PUSC uplink subchannel. A secondary Fast-feedback channel is mapped to one subchannel composed of six tiles.

\begin{figure}
\includegraphics[alt={},max width=\textwidth]{https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/a227cdd5-cf57-46a1-bc9f-bb964def4187-499.jpg?height=595&width=919&top_left_y=396&top_left_x=585}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Figure 231f-Subcarrier mapping of secondary Fast-feedback modulation symbols for PUSC}
\end{figure}

\section*{Insert new subclause 8.4.5.4.10.14:}

\subsection*{8.4.5.4.10.14 Extended rtPS bandwidth request}

In the case of Extended rtPS service, the MS may request bandwidth allocation, which is defined as the Maximum Sustained Traffic Rate in service flow encodings. The codeword 0 b 111011 is used for that purpose.

\section*{Insert new subclause 8.4.5.4.10.15}

\subsection*{8.4.5.4.10.15 MIMO feedback for transmit beamforming}

Codebooks are defined for the feedback of MIMO transmit beamforming, whose codeword may be employed as the beamforming matrix in MIMO precoding in 8.4.8.3.6. The vector codebooks for $2 \times 1,3 \times 1$, and $4 \times 1$ with 3 -bit feedback index are listed in Table 298 m , Table 298 n , and Table 298 o . The notation $V\left(N_{t}\right.$, $S, L)$ denotes the vector codebook, which consists of $2^{L}$ complex, unit vectors of a dimension $N_{t}$, and $S$ denotes the number of streams. The integer $L$ is the number of bits required for the index that can indicate any vector in the codebook.

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 298m- $V(2,1,3)$}
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|l|l|l|l|l|}
\hline Vector index & 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 & 5 & 6 & 7 & 8 \\
\hline v1 & 1 & 0.7940 & 0.7940 & 0.7941 & 0.7941 & 0.3289 & 0.5112 & 0.3289 \\
\hline v2 & 0 & -0.5801 + $j 0.1818$ & 0.0576 + $j 0.6051$ & -0.2978$j 0.5298$ & 0.6038 + $j 0.0689$ & 0.6614 + $j 0.6740$ & 0.4754 $j 0.7160$ & -0.8779 j0.3481 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 298n- $V(3,1,3)$}
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|l|l|l|l|l|}
\hline Vector index & 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 & 5 & 6 & 7 & 8 \\
\hline v1 & 1 & 0.500 & 0.500 & 0.500 & 0.500 & 0.4954 & 0.500 & 0.500 \\
\hline v2 & 0 & -0.7201 $j 0.3126$ & -0.0659 + j0.1371 & -0.0063 + j0.6527 & 0.7171 + j0.3202 & 0.4819 j0.4517 & 0.0686 $j 0.1386$ & -0.0054 $j 0.6540$ \\
\hline v3 & 0 & 0.2483 $j 0.2684$ & -0.6283$j 0.5763$ & 0.4621 j0.3321 & - 0.2533 + $j 0.2626$ & 0.2963 $j 0.4801$ & 0.6200 + $j 0.5845$ & -0.4566 + $j 0.3374$ \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 298o- $V(4,1,3)$}
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|l|l|l|l|l|}
\hline Vector index & 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 & 5 & 6 & 7 & 8 \\
\hline v1 & 1 & 0.3780 & 0.3780 & 0.3780 & 0.3780 & 0.3780 & 0.3780 & 0.3780 \\
\hline v2 & 0 & -0.2698 $j 0.5668$ & -0.7103+ $j 0.1326$ & 0.2830 $j 0.0940$ & -0.0841 + $j 0.6478$ & 0.5247 + j0.3532 & 0.2058 j0.1369 & 0.0618 $j 0.3332$ \\
\hline v3 & 0 & 0.5957 + $j 0.1578$ & -0.2350 $j 0.1467$ & 0.0702 j0.8261 & 0.0184 + $j 0.0490$ & 0.4115 + $j 0.1825$ & $-0.5211+ j 0.0833$ & -0.3456+ $j 0.5029$ \\
\hline v4 & 0 & 0.1587 j0.2411 & 0.1371 + $j 0.4893$ & -0.2801 + j0.0491 & -0.3272 $j 0.5662$ & 0.2639 + $j 0.4299$ & 0.6136 $j 0.3755$ & $-0.5704+ j 0.2113$ \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

The codebooks in Table 298m, Table 298n, and Table 298o are produced by generating expressions one per table. These tables are optimized for generation efficiency and storage memory.

Three operations are employed and they employs floating point arithmetic in IEEE Std 754, whose final results are rounded to four decimal places. The first operation generates a unitary $N \times N$ matrix $H(v)$ using an $N$ vector $v$ as:
$$
H(\boldsymbol{v})=\left\{\begin{array}{cc}
\boldsymbol{I}, & \boldsymbol{v}=\boldsymbol{e}_{1}  \tag{107e}\\
\boldsymbol{I}-p \boldsymbol{w} \boldsymbol{w}^{H}, & \text { otherwise }
\end{array},\right.
$$
where
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \boldsymbol{w}=\boldsymbol{v}-\boldsymbol{e}_{1} \text { and } \boldsymbol{e}_{1}=\left[\begin{array}{lll}
1 & 0 & \ldots
\end{array}\right] ; \\
& p=\frac{2}{\left\|\boldsymbol{w}^{H} \boldsymbol{w}\right\|} ;
\end{aligned}
$$
$\boldsymbol{I}$ is the $N x N$ identity matrix;
()$^{H}$ denotes the conjugate transpose operation.

Two vector codebooks $V(3,1,6)$ and $V(4,1,6)$ are generated as follows. All the vector codewords $\boldsymbol{v}_{i}, i=2, \ldots 2^{L}$ are derived from the first codeword $\boldsymbol{v}_{1}$ as:
$\tilde{\boldsymbol{v}}_{i}=H(s) Q^{i}(\boldsymbol{u}) H^{H}(s) \boldsymbol{v}_{\boldsymbol{i}}$, for $i=2, \ldots 2^{L}, \boldsymbol{v}_{i}=\tilde{\boldsymbol{v}}_{i} e^{-j \phi_{1}}$, for $i=2, \ldots 2^{L}$,
where
$$
\begin{aligned}
& Q^{i}(\boldsymbol{u})=\operatorname{diag}\left(e^{j \cdot \frac{2 \pi}{2^{L}} \cdot u_{1} \cdot i}, \ldots, e^{j \cdot \frac{2 \pi}{2^{L}} \cdot u_{N_{t}} \cdot i}\right) \text { is a diagonal matrix; } \\
& \mathbf{u}=\left\lfloor u_{1} \ldots u_{N_{t}}\right\rfloor \text { is an integer vector; } \\
& \boldsymbol{v}_{1}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{N_{t}}}\left[\begin{array}{lll}
1 & e^{j \cdot \frac{2 \pi}{N_{t}}} & \ldots \\
& \left.e^{j \cdot \frac{2 \pi}{N_{t}} \cdot\left(N_{t}-1\right)}\right]^{T}
\end{array}\right.
\end{aligned}
$$
$\phi_{i}$ is the phase of the first entry of $\tilde{v}_{i}$.

The parameters for the generation of $V(3,1,6)$ and $V(4,1,6)$ are listed in Table 298p.

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 298p-Generating parameters for $\boldsymbol{V}(\mathbf{3 , 1 , 6})$ and $\boldsymbol{V}(\mathbf{4 , 1 , 6 )}$}
\begin{tabular}{|c|c|l|c|}
\hline $\boldsymbol{N}_{\boldsymbol{t}}$ & $\boldsymbol{L}$ & \multicolumn{1}{|c|}{$\mathbf{u}$ in $Q^{i}(\mathbf{u})$} & \multicolumn{1}{|c|}{$\boldsymbol{s}$ in $\mathbf{H}(\boldsymbol{s})$} \\
\hline 3 & 6 & {$\left[\begin{array}{lll}1 & 26 & 57\end{array}\right]$} & {$[1.2518-\mathrm{j} 0.6409,-0.4570-j 0.4974,0.1177+j 0.2360]$} \\
\hline 4 & 6 & {$\left[\begin{array}{llll}1 & 45 & 22 & 49\end{array}\right]$} & {$[1.3954-j 0.0738,0.0206+j 0.4326,-0.1658-j 0.5445,0.5487-j 0.1599]^{T}$} \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

The second operation generates an $N$ by $M+1$ unitary matrix from a unit $N$ vector and a unitary $N-1$ by $M$ matrix as:
$$
H C\left(\boldsymbol{v}_{N}, \boldsymbol{A}_{(N-1) M}\right)=H\left(\boldsymbol{v}_{N}\right)\left[\begin{array}{cccc}
1 & 0 & \ldots & 0  \tag{107f}\\
0 & & & \\
\ldots & & & \\
0 & & & \boldsymbol{A}_{(N-1) M}
\end{array}\right]
$$
where
$$
N-1 \geq M \text {; the } N-1 \text { by } M \text { unitary matrix has property } \mathbf{A}^{H} \mathbf{A}=\mathbf{I} \text {. }
$$

The third operation generates an $N$ by $M$ matrix from a unit $N$ vector, $\mathbf{v}_{\mathrm{N}}$, by taking the last $N-1$ columns of $\mathrm{H}\left(\mathbf{v}_{N}\right)$ as:
$$
\begin{equation*}
H E\left(\boldsymbol{v}_{N}\right)=H\left(\boldsymbol{v}_{N}\right)_{:, 2: N} \tag{107~g}
\end{equation*}
$$

The three operations jointly generate eleven matrix codebooks from vector codebooks as shown in Table 298 q , where each entry is the generating operation of one codebook.

Table 298q contains operations to generate codebooks $V\left(N_{t}, S, L\right)$ for $N_{t}=2,3,4, S=2,3,4$, and $L=3$ and 6:

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 298q-Operations to generate codebooks}
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|}
\hline Nt, $L \mid S$ & 2 & 3 & 4 \\
\hline 2, 3 & $\mathrm{H}(V(2,1,3))$ & - & - \\
\hline 3, 3 & HE( $V(3,1,3)$ ) & $\mathrm{H}(V(3,1,3))$ & - \\
\hline 4,3 & - & HE( $V(4,1,3)$ ) & $\mathrm{H}(V(4,1,3))$ \\
\hline 2,6 & $\mathrm{H}(V(2,1,6))$ & - & - \\
\hline 3,6 & HC( $V(3,1,3), V(2,1,3))$ & HC( $V(3,1,3), \mathrm{H}(V(2,1,3)))$ & - \\
\hline 4,6 & HC( $V(4,1,3), \mathrm{H}(V(3,1,3)))$ & HE( $V(4,1,6)$ ) & $\mathrm{H}(V(4,1,6))$ \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

The set notation $V\left(N_{t}, 1, L\right)$ in the input arguments of the operations denotes that each vector in the codebook $V\left(N_{t}, 1, L\right)$ is sequentially taken as an input to the operations. The output of the operation with one or more codebooks as input arguments is also a codebook. For example, in $\mathrm{HC}(V(3,1,3), \mathrm{H}(V(2,1,3)))$ has two codebooks as input. The first is $V(3,1,3)$ with 8 vectors and the second is $\mathrm{H}(V(2,1,3))$ with $82 \times 2$ matrices, which are computed from $V(2,1,3)$. The feedback index is constructed by sequentially concatenating all the indexes of the input argument vector codebooks in binary format. For example, the feedback index of $\mathrm{HC}(V(3,1,3), \mathrm{H}(V(2,1,3)))$ is constructed as $i_{2} j_{2}$, where $i_{2}$ and $j_{2}$ are the indexes of the vectors in codebooks $V(3,1,3)$ and $V(2,1,3)$ in binary format respectively.

Table 298r, Table 298s, Table 298t are included to illustrate codebooks generated using the rules defined above.

\subsection*{8.4.5.4.11 MIMO UL Basic IE format}

\section*{Change 8.4.5.4.11 as indicated:}

In the UL-MAP, a MIMO-enabled BS may transmit UIUC $=15$ with the MIMO_UL_Basic_IE() to indicate the MIMO mode of the subsequent uplink allocations described in this IE a specific MIMO enabled SS CID. The MIMO mode indicated in the MIMO_UL_Basic_IE() shall only apply to the subsequent uplink allocation within the IE until the end of frame (see Table 299). This IE may be used either for a MIMO enabled SS or for an SS that supports only collaborative SM. The IE may also be used to assign allocations in AAS zones to AAS enabled SSs that are capable of collaborative SM.

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 299-MIMO UL basic IE format}
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|}
\hline Syntax & Size & Notes \\
\hline MIMO_UL_Basic_IE () \{ & & \\
\hline Extended ĐUIUC & 4 bits & $\mathrm{MIMO}=0 \mathrm{x} 02 \underline{6}$ \\
\hline Length & 4 bits & Length of the message in bytes (variable) \\
\hline Num_Assign & 4 bits & Number of burst assignment \\
\hline For ( $j=0 ; j<$ Num_assign; $j++$ ) \{ & & \\
\hline Collaborative SM Indication & 1 bit & \begin{tabular}{l}
0: Non collaborative SM (Vertical coding assignment to a MIMO capable SS) \\
1: Collaborative SM (assignment to 2 collaborative SM capable SSs)
\end{tabular} \\
\hline If ( Collaborative SM Indication == 0) \{ & & \\
\hline CID & 16 bits & SS basic CID \\
\hline UIUC & 4 bits & \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 299-MIMO UL basic IE format (continued)}
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|}
\hline Syntax & Size & Notes \\
\hline MIMO_Control & 1 bit & \begin{tabular}{l}
For dual transmission capable SS \\
0: STTD \\
1: SM \\
For Collaborative SM capable SS \\
0 : pilot pattern A \\
1: pilot pattern B
\end{tabular} \\
\hline $\}$ Else \{ & & \\
\hline CID_A & 16 bits & Basic CID of SS that shall use pilot pattern A \\
\hline UIUC_A & 4 bits & UIUC used for the allocation that uses pilot pattern A \\
\hline CID B & 16 bits & Basic CID of SS that shall use pilot pattern B \\
\hline UIUC_B & 4 bits & UIUC used for the allocation that uses pilot pattern B \\
\hline 1 & & \\
\hline Duration & 10 bits & In OFDMA slots (see 8.4.3.1) \\
\hline \} & & \\
\hline padding & variable & Number of bits required to align to byte length, shall be set to zero. \\
\hline \} & & \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\section*{Num_assign}

This field specifies the number of assignments in this IE.

\section*{MIMO_Control}

MIMO_Control field specifies the MIMO mode of the corresponding UL burst. For a dual transmission capable SS, the value of 0 indicates STTD mode, the value of 1 indicates SM mode. For a collaborative SM- capable SS, the value of 0 indicates pilot pattern $\Lambda$, the value of 1 indicates pilot pattern B .

\section*{Insert new text and Table 299a as shown:}

Table 299a summarizes the modes of operation specified by MIMO_UL_Basic_IE(). For each it details the following:
$=\quad$ Number of antennas;
= Values of Collaborative SM_indication and MIMO_control;
= Number of different CID's stated in the appropriate case of the "if" statement;
= Implicit type and rate of coding.

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 299a-MIMO UL Basic IE operation modes}
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|l|l|l|}
\hline Mode & Number of Tx antennas per SS & Collaborative SM_ Indication & MIMO control & CIDs & Coding Type & Rate \\
\hline Collaborative MIMO, 2 SSs & $\underline{1}$ & $\underline{1}$ & N/A & CID_A != CID_B & Two SS, each transmits from antenna \#0 & $\underline{1}$ per SS \\
\hline Spatial Multiplexing, Vertical coding & $\underline{2}$ & $\underline{0}$ & $\underline{1}$ & Single CID & SM with Vertical coding for Single user & $\underline{2}$ \\
\hline STTD & $\underline{2}$ & $\underline{0}$ & $\underline{0}$ & Single CID & STTD & $\underline{1}$ \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\section*{Vertical coding}

Indicates transmitting the same coded stream over multiple antennas.
Rate
The number of QAM symbols signaled per array channel use.

\subsection*{8.4.5.4.12 CQICH Allocation IE Format}

\section*{Change the first sentence as indicated:}

CQICH_Alloc_IE(), is introduced to dynamically allocate or de-allocate a CQICH to an SS. Once allocated, the SS transmit channel quality information on the assigned CQICH on every subsequent frames, until the SS receives a CQICH_Alloc_IE() to de-allocate the assigned CQICH.

\section*{Change Table 300 as indicated:}

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 300-CQICH alloc IE format}
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|}
\hline Syntax & Size & Notes \\
\hline CQICH_Alloc_IE() () \{ & & \\
\hline Extended ĐUIUC & 4 bits & CQICH $=0 \times 03$ \\
\hline Length & 4 bits & Length of the message in bytes (variable). \\
\hline CQICH_ID & variable & Index to uniquely identify the CQICH resource assigned to the SS . The size of this field is dependent on system parameter defined in QUCD . \\
\hline Allocation offset & 6 bits & Index to the fast-feedback channel region marked by UIUC $=0$. \\
\hline Period (p) & 2 bits & A CQI feedback is transmitted on the CQICH every ${ }^{2} \mathrm{p} \underline{2}^{\mathrm{p}}$ frames. \\
\hline Frame offset & 3 bits & The SS starts reporting at the frame of which the number has the same 3 LSB as the specified frame offset. If the current frame is specified, the SS should start reporting in eight frames. \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|}

\hline Duration (d) & 3 bits & \begin{tabular}{l}
A CQI feedback is transmitted on the CQI channels indexed by the CQICH_ID for $10 \times 2{ }^{\mathrm{d}}$ frames. If $\mathrm{d}==0$, the CQI-CH is deallocated. \\
If $\mathrm{d}==0 \mathrm{~b} 111$, the SS should report until the BS command for the SS to stop.
\end{tabular} \\
\hline Report configuration included & 1 bits & Update to CINR report configuration is included. \\
\hline If (report configuration included == 1) \{ & & \\
\hline Feedback Type & 2 bits & \begin{tabular}{l}
0b00 = physical CINR feedback \\
0b01 = effective CINR feedback \\
0b10-0b11 = Reserved
\end{tabular} \\
\hline Report type & 1 bit & \begin{tabular}{l}
0: Report for preamble \\
1: Report for specific permutation zone
\end{tabular} \\
\hline If (Report type $==0$ ) \{ & & \\
\hline CINR preamble report type & 1 bit & \begin{tabular}{l}
The type of preamble-based CINR report \\
0: Frequency reuse factor $=1$ configuration. \\
1: Frequency reuse factor $=3$ configuration.
\end{tabular} \\
\hline 1 & & \\
\hline Else \{ & & report for permutation zone. \\
\hline Zone permutation & 3 bits & \begin{tabular}{l}
The type of zone for which to report \\
0b000 - PUSC with 'use all $\mathrm{SC}=0^{\prime}$ \\
0b001 - PUSC with 'use all $\mathrm{SC}=1^{\prime}$ \\
0b010 - FUSC \\
0b011 - Optional FUSC \\
0b100 - Safety Channel region \\
0b101 - AMC zone (only applicable to AAS mode) \\
0b110-111 - Reserved
\end{tabular} \\
\hline Zone type & 2 bits & \begin{tabular}{l}
0b00 - Non-STC zone \\
0b01 - STC zone \\
0b10-AAS zone \\
0b11 - Reserved
\end{tabular} \\
\hline Zone PRBS_ID & 2 bits & The PRBS_ID of the zone on which to report \\
\hline If (Zone type $==0 \mathrm{~b} 000$ or 0b001) \{ & & \\
\hline Major group indication & 1 bit & If ' 0 ' then the report may refer to any subchannel in the PUSC zone. \\
\hline If (Major group indication == 1) \{ & & \\
\hline PUSC Major group bitmap & 6 bits & \begin{tabular}{l}
Reported CINR shall only apply to the subchannels of PUSC major groups for which the corresponding bit is set. \\
Bit \#k refers to major group $k$.
\end{tabular} \\
\hline 夕 & & \\
\hline 1 & & \\
\hline CINR zone measurement type & 1 bit & \begin{tabular}{l}
0: measurement from pilot subcarriers and, if AAS zone, from AAS preamble. \\
1: measurement from data subcarriers
\end{tabular} \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 300-CQICH alloc IE format (continued)}
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|}
\hline Syntax & Size & Notes \\
\hline 1 & & \\
\hline If (feedback type == 0b00) \{ & & Physical CINR feedback \\
\hline Averaging parameter included & 1 bit & \\
\hline If (Averaging parameter included == 1) $\{$ & & \\
\hline Averaging parameter & 4 bits & Averaging parameter $\alpha_{\text {avg }}$ used for deriving physical CINR estimates reported through CQICH. This value is given in multiples of 1/16 in the range of [1/16..16/16] in increasing order. \\
\hline 1 & & \\
\hline 1 & & \\
\hline $\dot{L}$ & & \\
\hline MIMO_permutation_feedback_cycle & 2 bits & \begin{tabular}{l}
0b00 = No MIMO and permutation mode feedback 0b01 = The MIMO and permutation mode indication shall be transmitted on the CQICH indexed by the CQICH_ID every four frames allocated CQICH transmission opportunity. The first indication is sent on the eighthfourth allocated CQICH frame transmission opportunity. \\
0b10 = The MIMO mode and permutation mode indication shall be transmitted on the CQICH indexed by the CQICH_ID every eight frames allocated CQICH transmission opportunity. The first indication is sent on the eighth allocated CQICH frame transmission opportunity. \\
0b11 = The MIMO mode and permutation mode indication shall be transmitted on the CQICH indexed by the CQICH_ID every 16 frames allocated CQICH transmission opportunity. The first indication is sent on the 16th allocated CQICH frame transmission opportunity.
\end{tabular} \\
\hline Padding & variable & The padding bits is used to ensure the IE size is integer number of bytes.Number of bits required to align to byte length, shall be set to zero. \\
\hline \} & & \\

\end{tabular}
