\subsection*{8.4.8.3.1.2.2 STC Mapping for optional FUSC permutation}

For the optional FUSC permutation in STC zone, the data subchannels shall be allocated for two consecutive OFDMA symbols. For a 2 -antenna matrix A in 8.4.8.3.3, STC encoded data symbols shall be time mapped over two OFDMA symbols. The mapping starts at the lowest numbered subcarriers of lowest slot and continues in an ascending manner in subchannels first and then, if needed, proceeds to the next two symbols in time.

For a 2-antenna vertically encoded matrix B in 8.4.8.3.3 for the optional FUSC permutation, the data subchannels shall be allocated for two consecutive OFDMA symbols and the modulated data symbols shall be sequentially mapped for two transmit antennas along the subcarriers in the symbol. The mapping continues in an ascending manner in subchannels first and then proceeds to the next symbol in time. For a 2 -antenna horizontally encoded matrix $B$ in 8.4.8.3.3 each encoded stream is separately mapped to the corresponding antenna.

For a 3 - and 4 -antenna matrix A and matrix B in 8.4.8.3.4 and 8.4.8.3.4 for the optional FUSC permutation, STC encoded data symbols shall be mapped at two logical subcarriers over two OFDMA symbols. When the subcarrier pair (over two symbols) at logical frequency $k+1$ is allocated to pilots for antenna \#0 or \#1 and the pair at logical frequency $k+2$ is allocated to pilots for antenna \#2 or \#3, then the pair at logical frequency $k+3$ shall be jointly encoded with the pair at logical frequency $k$. The mapping starts at the lowest numbered subcarriers of the lowest slot and continues in an ascending manner in subchannels first and then proceeds to the
next two symbols in time if needed. Data puncturing for CC or truncation for CTC shall be performed in a similar manner as in the optional AMC zone.

For 3- and 4-antenna vertically/horizontally encoded matrix C in the optional FUSC permutation, the same mapping rule for 2-antenna vertically/horizontally encoded matrix B shall be applied on the same frequencytime block with the 3 - or 4 -antenna pilot pattern.

A mapping example of a downlink burst for the optional FUSC using 4-antenna transmission is provided in the following:

Parameters are:

ID_CELL $=1$, Symbol index $m=0$ (first symbol in STC zone), subchannel index $=0$, The number of subchannels $=1,1024 \mathrm{FFT}$,

The indices of 48 data subcarriers in subchannel \#0 for the optional FUSC are as follows:

\begin{tabular}{cccccccccccccc}
3 & 23 & 46 & 58 & 79 & 104 & 121 & 132 & 156 & 170 & 196 & 215 & 231 & 245 \\
254 & 273 & 293 & 316 & 328 & 349 & 374 & 391 & 402 & 426 & 440 & 466 & 485 & 501 \\
515 & 524 & 543 & 563 & 586 & 598 & 619 & 644 & 661 & 672 & 696 & 710 & 736 & 755 \\
771 & 785 & 794 & 813 & 833 & 856 & & & & & & & &
\end{tabular}

If Convolutional Coding is used for 4 Tx antennas, data tones at subcarrier indices $= \{245,254,515,524,785,794\}$ shall be punctured for additional pilots. If Convolutional Turbo Coding is used for 4 Tx antennas, the last 6 of 48 data tones shall be first truncated and the remaining 42 data tones shall be mapped at the following indices:,

\begin{tabular}{cccccccccccccc}
3 & 23 & 46 & 58 & 79 & 104 & 121 & 132 & 156 & 170 & 196 & 215 & 231 & 273 \\
293 & 316 & 328 & 349 & 374 & 391 & 402 & 426 & 440 & 466 & 485 & 501 & 543 & 563 \\
586 & 598 & 619 & 644 & 661 & 672 & 696 & 710 & 736 & 755 & 771 & 813 & 833 & 856
\end{tabular}

\subsection*{8.4.8.3.1.2.3 Burst packing of spatial multiplexed streams with CTC HARQ}

For multiple spatial rate transmission and HARQ CTC, the packet shall be formed by concatenating multiple $\mathrm{N}_{\text {EP }} / \mathrm{N}_{\text {SCH }}$ FEC codewords together. For the case of vertical encoding (number of layers $=1$ ), there shall be only 1 CRC check at the end of the last codeword. The first block is of size $\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{EP}}$ and the second block of size $\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{EP}^{-1}} 16$ bits. For the case of horizontal encoding (number of layers $>1$ ), each burst shall be a separate $\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{EP}} / \mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{SCH}}$ pair with separate CRC. The randomization seed shall be reset for all of the $\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{EP}} / \mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{SCH}}$ pairs in the combined codeword. Figure 251j shows an example of vertically encoded rate 2 with CTC HARQ transmission.

\section*{Insert new subclause 8.4.8.3.2:}

\subsection*{8.4.8.3.2 Symbol structure for the optional PUSC-ASCA}

Symbol structure is defined in 8.4.6.3.1, pilots division between antennas per cluster for the STC/MIMO operation shall follow the division in the PUSC mode as defined in 8.4.8.1 and 8.4.8.2. Pilots may optionally be beamformed or precoded.

\section*{Insert new subclause 8.4.8.3.3:}

\subsection*{8.4.8.3.3 Transmission schemes for 2-antenna BS in DL}

The following matrices define the transmission format with the row index indicating antenna number and column index indicating OFDMA symbol time. For both DL permutation zones with 2 -antenna BS, one of the following three transmission matrices shall be used:
$$
\begin{aligned}
A & =\left[\begin{array}{cc}
S_{i} & -S_{i+1}^{*} \\
S_{i+1} & S_{i}^{*}
\end{array}\right] \\
B & =\left[\begin{array}{c}
S_{i} \\
S_{i}+1
\end{array}\right] \\
C & =\frac{1}{\sqrt{1+r^{2}}}\left(\begin{array}{cc}
S_{i}+j r \cdot S_{i+3} & r \cdot S_{i+1}+S_{i+2} \\
S_{i+1}-r \cdot S_{i+2} & j r \cdot S_{i}+S_{i+3}
\end{array}\right), r=\frac{-1+\sqrt{5}}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$
where $\mathrm{S}_{i}$ and $\mathrm{S}_{i+1}$ in $B$ may be encoded in different rates.

\section*{Insert new subclause 8.4.8.3.4:}

\subsection*{8.4.8.3.4 Transmission schemes for 3-antenna BS in DL}

The following definitions are applicable to modes that support STC for 3-antenna Tx.
For 3-antenna BS, one of the three transmission matrices A, B, or C, shall be used:
Let the complex symbols to be transmitted be $x_{1}, x_{2}, x_{3}, x_{4}$, which take values from a square QAM constellation; let $s_{i}=x_{i} e^{j \theta}$ for $\mathrm{i}=1,2, \ldots, 8$, where $\theta=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)$; and let
$\tilde{s}_{1}=s_{1 I}+j s_{3 Q} ; \tilde{s_{2}}=s_{2 I}+j s_{4 Q} ; \tilde{s}_{3}=s_{3 I}+j s_{1 Q} ; \tilde{s}_{4}=s_{4 I}+j s_{2 Q}$ where $s_{i}=s_{i I}+j s_{i Q}$.
The proposed Space-Time-Frequency code (over two OFDMA symbols and two subcarriers) for 3Tx-Rate 1 configuration with diversity order 3 is given in three permuted versions:
$$
\begin{align*}
A_{1} & =\left[\begin{array}{cccc}
\tilde{S}_{1} & -\tilde{S}_{2}^{*} & 0 & 0 \\
\tilde{S}_{2} & \tilde{S}_{1}^{*} & \tilde{S}_{3} & -\tilde{S}_{4}^{*} \\
0 & 0 & \tilde{S}_{4} & \tilde{S}_{3}^{*}
\end{array}\right]  \tag{124a}\\
A_{2} & =\left[\begin{array}{cccc}
\tilde{S}_{1} & -\tilde{S}_{2}^{*} & \tilde{S}_{3} & -\tilde{S}_{4}^{*} \\
\tilde{S}_{2} & \tilde{S}_{1}^{*} & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & \tilde{S}_{4} & \tilde{S}_{3}^{*}
\end{array}\right] \\
A_{3} & =\left[\begin{array}{cccc}
\tilde{S}_{1} & -\tilde{S}_{2}^{*} & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & \tilde{S}_{3} & -\tilde{S}_{4}^{*} \\
\tilde{S}_{2} & \tilde{S}_{1}^{*} & \tilde{S}_{4} & \tilde{S}_{3}^{*}
\end{array}\right]
\end{align*}
$$
where the ML decoding can be achieved by symbol-by-symbol decoding.
The matrix $B$ is
$$
\begin{align*}
& B_{1}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}
\sqrt{\frac{3}{4}} & 0 & 0 \\
0 & \sqrt{\frac{3}{4}} & 0 \\
0 & 0 & \sqrt{\frac{3}{2}}
\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cccc}
\tilde{S}_{1} & -\tilde{S}_{2}^{*} & \tilde{S}_{5} & -\tilde{S}_{6}^{*} \\
\tilde{S}_{2} & \tilde{S}_{1}^{*} & \tilde{S}_{6} & \tilde{S}_{5}^{*} \\
\tilde{S}_{7} & \tilde{-}_{8}^{*} & \tilde{S}_{3} & -\tilde{S}_{4}^{*}
\end{array}\right]  \tag{124b}\\
& B_{2}=\left[\begin{array}{lll}
0 & 1 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 1 \\
1 & 0 & 0
\end{array}\right] B_{1} \\
& B_{3}=\left[\begin{array}{lll}
0 & 0 & 1 \\
1 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 1 & 0
\end{array}\right] B_{1}
\end{align*}
$$
where the definition for the remaining variables are as follows:
$$
\begin{equation*}
\tilde{s_{5}}=s_{5 I}+j s_{7 Q} ; \quad \tilde{s_{6}}=s_{6 I}+j s_{8 Q} ; \quad \tilde{s_{7}}=s_{7 I}+j s_{5 Q} ; \quad \tilde{s_{8}}=s_{8 I}+j s_{6 Q} \tag{124c}
\end{equation*}
$$

The matrix C is used for spatial multiplexing.
$$
C=\left[\begin{array}{l}
S_{1}  \tag{124~d}\\
S_{2} \\
S_{3}
\end{array}\right]
$$

The index, $k$, of the permuted version of Matrix A and Matrix B to use for a particular deployment is given by: $k=\bmod (\operatorname{floor}($ logical_data_subcarrier_number_for_first_tone_of_code-1 $) / 2), 3)+1$, where logical_data_subcarrier_number_for_first_tone_of_code $=1,2,3, \ldots$, Total $\#$ of data subcarriers.

\section*{Insert new subclause 8.4.8.3.4.1:}

\subsection*{8.4.8.3.4.1 Enhanced 3 Tx Matrix A with Antenna Grouping}

For 3-Tx-antenna BS, transmission matrix A in 8.4.8.3.4 may be employed with adaptive antenna grouping, which is fed back from MS.

When MS reports 0b000, 0b0111 or 0b101110 on its CQICH (See 8.4.5.4.10.3 and 8.4.5.4.10.7), then BS shall group antenna 0 and 1 for the first subcarrier and antenna 1 and 2 for the second subcarrier. In matrix form, it shall be read as
$$
A_{1}=\left[\begin{array}{cccc}
\tilde{s_{1}} & \tilde{s}_{2}^{*} & 0 & 0  \tag{124e}\\
\tilde{s_{2}} & \tilde{s}_{1}^{*} & \tilde{s}_{3} & \tilde{s}_{4}^{*} \\
0 & 0 & \tilde{s}_{4} & \tilde{s}_{3}^{*}
\end{array}\right]
$$

When MS reports $0 \mathrm{~b} 001,0 \mathrm{~b} 1000$ or 0 b 101111 on its CQICH, then BS shall group antenna 0 and 1 for the first subcarrier and antenna 0 and 2 for the second subcarrier. In matrix form, it shall be read as
$$
A_{2}=\left[\begin{array}{cccc}
\tilde{s_{1}} & \tilde{s}_{2}^{*} & \tilde{s_{3}} & -\tilde{s}_{4}^{*}  \tag{124f}\\
\tilde{s_{2}} & \tilde{s}_{1}^{*} & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & \tilde{s}_{4} & \tilde{s}_{3}^{*}
\end{array}\right]
$$

When MS reports 0 b 010 , 0 b 1001 or 0 b 110000 on its CQICH, then BS shall group antenna 0 and 2 for the first subcarrier and antenna 1 and 2 for the second subcarrier. In matrix form, it shall be read as
$$
A_{3}=\left[\begin{array}{cccc}
\tilde{s_{1}} & \tilde{s}_{2}^{*} & 0 & 0  \tag{124~g}\\
0 & 0 & \tilde{s_{3}} & \tilde{s}_{4}^{*} \\
\tilde{s_{2}} & \tilde{s}_{1}^{*} & \tilde{s_{4}} & \tilde{s}_{s}^{*}
\end{array}\right]
$$

\section*{Insert new subclause 8.4.8.3.4.1:}

\subsection*{8.4.8.3.4.2 Enhanced 3 Tx Matrix B with Antenna Grouping}

For 3 Tx antenna BS, transmission matrix B for rate 2 may be employed with antenna grouping information that is fed back on a CQICH from MS.

When MS reports 0b000, 0b1010, or 0b110001 on its allocated CQICH, then BS shall transmit in the following transmission matrix:
$$
B_{1}=\left[\begin{array}{cccc}
\tilde{s}_{7} & \tilde{s}_{8} * & \tilde{s}_{3} & \tilde{s}_{4}^{*}  \tag{124~h}\\
\tilde{s}_{1} & \tilde{s}_{2}^{*} & \tilde{s}_{5} & \tilde{s}_{6}^{*} \\
\tilde{s}_{2} & \tilde{s}_{1}^{*} & \tilde{s}_{6} & \tilde{s}_{5}^{*}
\end{array}\right]
$$

When MS reports 0b001, 0b1011, or b110010 on its allocated CQICH, then BS shall transmit in the following transmission matrix:
$$
B_{2}=\left[\begin{array}{cccc}
\tilde{s}_{1} & \tilde{s}_{2}^{*} & \tilde{s}_{5} & \tilde{s}_{6}^{*}  \tag{124i}\\
\tilde{s}_{7} & \tilde{s}_{8}^{*} & \tilde{s}_{3} & \tilde{s}_{4}^{*} \\
\tilde{s}_{2} & \tilde{s}_{1}^{*} & \tilde{s}_{6} & \tilde{s}_{5}^{*}
\end{array}\right]
$$

When MS reports 0b010, 0b1100, or 0b110011 on its allocated CQICH, then BS shall transmit in the following transmission matrix:
$$
B_{3}=\left[\begin{array}{cccc}
\tilde{s_{1}} & \tilde{s}_{2} * & \tilde{s_{5}} & -\tilde{s}_{6}^{*}  \tag{124j}\\
\tilde{s_{2}} & \tilde{s}_{1}^{*} & \tilde{s}_{6} & \tilde{s}_{5}^{*} \\
\tilde{s}_{7} & \tilde{s}_{8}^{*} & \tilde{s}_{3} & \tilde{s}_{4}^{*}
\end{array}\right]
$$

\section*{Insert new subclause 8.4.8.3.4.3:}

\subsection*{8.4.8.3.4.3 3 Tx Matrix C with Antenna Selection}

For the transmission matrix C , when k sub-streams are configured, $\mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{i}}=\left[\mathrm{s}_{1}, \mathrm{~s}_{2}, . . \mathrm{s}_{k}\right], k=1, \ldots, M, M=1,2$ Transmission matrix is adaptively changed according to the CQICH. For 3-transmit antennas BS, the transmission matrix is listed in Table 316f, where the mapping of the matrix $C_{n}$ to the CQICH is shown. The active antenna is power boosted.

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 316f-Enhanced 3 Tx Matrix C with Antenna Grouping}
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|l|}
\hline \multirow{2}{*}{Streams, $\mathbf{k}$} & \multicolumn{3}{|c|}{CQICH (binary)} & \multirow{2}{*}{Power Boosting} \\
\hline & 0 b 110000 (option 1) & 0b110001 (option 2) & 0b110010 (option 3) & \\
\hline & \begin{tabular}{l}
$$
C_{1}=c\left[\begin{array}{l}
s_{1} \\
0 \\
0
\end{array}\right]
$$ \\
$C_{1}=c\left[\begin{array}{c}s_{1} \\ 0 \\ 0\end{array}\right]$
\end{tabular} & \begin{tabular}{l}
$$
C_{2}=c\left[\begin{array}{c}
0 \\
s_{1} \\
0
\end{array}\right]
$$ \\

\end{tabular} & $C_{3}=c\left[\begin{array}{c}0 \\ 0 \\ s_{1}\end{array}\right]$
$$
C_{3}=c\left[\begin{array}{l}
0 \\
s_{1}
\end{array}\right]
$$ & \begin{tabular}{l}
$$
c=1
$$ \\
$c=1$
\end{tabular} \\
\hline 2 & $C_{1}=c\left[\begin{array}{c}s_{1} \\ s_{2} \\ 0\end{array}\right]$ & $C_{2}=c\left[\begin{array}{c}s_{1} \\ 0 \\ s_{2}\end{array}\right]$ & $C_{3}=c\left[\begin{array}{c}0 \\ s_{1} \\ s_{2}\end{array}\right]$ & $c=1 /(\sqrt{2})$ \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

Stream $k=2$ indicates TLV=176, with Bit \#1 and Bit\#16 set.

\section*{Insert new subclause 8.4.8.3.5:}

\subsection*{8.4.8.3.5 Transmission schemes for 4-antenna BS}

For all permutation zones using 4-antenna BS, one of the following three transmission matrices shall be used:
$$
\begin{align*}
& A=\left[\begin{array}{rrrr} 
& { }^{*} & & \\
s_{1} & -s_{2} & 0 & 0 \\
s_{2} & s_{1}^{*} & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & s_{3} & -s_{4}^{*} \\
0 & 0 & s_{4} & s_{3}^{*}
\end{array}\right]  \tag{124k}\\
& B=\left[\begin{array}{rrrr}
s_{1} & -s_{2}^{*} & s_{5} & -s_{7}^{*} \\
s_{2} & s_{1}^{*} & s_{6} & -s_{8}^{*} \\
& { }^{*} & & { }^{*} \\
s_{3} & -s_{4} & s_{7} & s_{5} \\
s_{4} & s_{3} & s_{8} & s_{6}
\end{array}\right] \tag{1241}
\end{align*}
$$
$$
C=\left[\begin{array}{l}
s_{1}  \tag{124~m}\\
s_{2} \\
s_{3} \\
s_{4}
\end{array}\right]
$$
where $s_{i}$ may have different rates.
The permuted matrix- $A$ (over two OFDMA symbols and two subcarriers) for 4Tx-Rate 1 is given in three permuted matrices:
$$
A_{1}=\left[\begin{array}{cccc}
S_{1} & -S_{2}^{*} & 0 & 0  \tag{124n}\\
S_{2} & S_{1}^{*} & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & S_{3} & -S_{4}^{*}{ }_{4} \\
0 & 0 & S_{4} & S_{3}^{*}
\end{array}\right], \quad A_{2}=\left[\begin{array}{cccc}
S_{1} & -S_{2}^{*} & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & S_{3} & -S_{4}^{*} \\
S_{2} & S_{1}^{*} & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & S_{4} & S_{3}^{*}
\end{array}\right], \quad A_{3}=\left[\begin{array}{cccc}
S_{1} & -S_{2}^{*} & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & S_{3} & -S_{4}^{*} \\
0 & 0 & S_{4} & S_{3}^{*} \\
S_{2} & S_{1}^{*} & 0 & 0
\end{array}\right] .
$$

The mapping of subscript $k$ to determine the matrix $A_{k}$ is given by the following formula:
$$
k=\bmod (\operatorname{floor}((\text { logical_data_subcarrier_number_for_first_tone_of_code-1 }) / 2), 3)+1
$$
where
$$
\text { logical_data_subcarrier_number_for_first_tone_of_code }=1,2,3, \ldots, \text { Total } \# \text { of data subcarriers. }
$$

The permuted matrix- $B$ (over two OFDMA symbols and two subcarriers) for 4-Tx-Rate 2 is given in six permuted matrices:
$$
\begin{align*}
B_{1}=\left[\begin{array}{cccc}
S_{1} & -S^{*}{ }_{2} & S_{5} & -S^{*}{ }_{7} \\
S_{2} & S^{*}{ }_{1} & S_{6} & -S^{*}{ }_{8} \\
S_{3} & -S^{*}{ }_{4} & S_{7} & S^{*}{ }_{5} \\
S_{4} & S^{*}{ }_{3} & S_{8} & S^{*}{ }_{6}
\end{array}\right], \quad B_{2}=\left[\begin{array}{cccc}
S_{1} & -S^{*}{ }_{2} & S_{5} & -S^{*}{ }_{7} \\
S_{2} & S^{*}{ }_{1} & S_{6} & -S^{*}{ }_{8} \\
S_{4} & S^{*}{ }_{3} & S_{8} & S^{*}{ }_{6} \\
S_{3} & -S^{*}{ }_{4} & S_{7} & S^{*}{ }_{5}
\end{array}\right], \quad B_{3}=\left[\begin{array}{cccc}
S_{1} & -S^{*}{ }_{2} & S_{5} & -S^{*}{ }_{7} \\
S_{3} & -S^{*}{ }_{4} & S_{7} & S^{*}{ }_{5} \\
S_{2} & S^{*}{ }_{1} & S_{6} & -S^{*}{ }_{8} \\
S_{4} & S^{*}{ }_{3} & S_{8} & S^{*}{ }_{6}
\end{array}\right] . \\
B_{4}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}
S_{1} & -S^{*}{ }_{2} & S_{5} \\
S_{4} & S^{*}{ }_{3}^{*} & S_{8} \\
S_{2} & S^{*}{ }_{6} \\
S_{3} & S_{6} & -S^{*}{ }_{8} \\
S_{7} & S_{7} & S^{*}{ }_{5}
\end{array}\right], \quad B_{5}=\left[\begin{array}{cccc}
S_{1} & -S^{*}{ }_{2} & S_{5} & -S^{*}{ }_{7} \\
S_{3} & -S^{*}{ }_{4} & S_{7} & S^{*}{ }_{5} \\
S_{4} & S^{*}{ }_{3} & S_{8} & S^{*}{ }_{6} \\
S_{2} & S^{*}{ }_{1} & S_{6} & -S^{*}{ }_{8}
\end{array}\right], \quad B_{6}=\left[\begin{array}{cccc}
S_{1} & -S^{*}{ }_{2} & S_{5} & -S^{*}{ }_{7} \\
S_{4} & S^{*}{ }_{3} & S_{8} & S^{*}{ }_{6} \\
S_{3} & -S^{*}{ }_{4} & S_{7} & S^{*}{ }_{5} \\
S_{2} & S^{*}{ }_{1} & S_{6} & -S^{*}{ }_{8}
\end{array}\right] . \tag{124o}
\end{align*}
$$

The mapping of subscript $k$ to determine the matrix $B_{k}$ is given by the following formula:
$$
k=\bmod (\operatorname{floor}((\text { logical_data_subcarrier_number_for_first_tone_of_code-1 }) / 2), 6)+1
$$
where
logical_data_subcarrier_number_for_first_tone_of_code $=1,2,3, \ldots$, Total $\#$ of data subcarriers.

\section*{Insert new subclause 8.4.8.3.5.1:}

\subsection*{8.4.8.3.5.1 Enhanced 4 Tx Matrix A with Antenna Grouping}

For 4 Tx antenna BS, transmission matrix A in 8.4.8.3.5 may be employed with adaptive antenna grouping, which is fed back from MS.

When MS reports 0 b 101110 on its CQICH, then BS shall group antenna 0 and 1 for the first subcarrier and antenna 2 and 3 for the second subcarrier. In matrix form, it shall be read as
$$
A_{1}=\left[\begin{array}{cccc}
s_{1} & -s_{2}{ }^{*} & 0 & 0  \tag{124p}\\
s_{2} & s_{1}{ }^{*} & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & s_{3} & -s_{4}{ }^{*} \\
0 & 0 & s_{4} & s_{3}{ }^{*}
\end{array}\right]
$$

When MS reports 0 b 101111 on its CQICH, then BS shall group antenna 0 and 2 for the first subcarrier and antenna 1 and 3 for the second subcarrier. In matrix form, it shall be read as
$$
A_{2}=\left[\begin{array}{cccc}
s_{1} & -s_{2}{ }^{*} & 0 & 0  \tag{124q}\\
0 & 0 & s_{3} & -s_{4}{ }^{*} \\
s_{2} & s_{1}{ }^{*} & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & s_{4} & s_{3}{ }^{*}
\end{array}\right]
$$

When MS reports 0 b 110000 on its CQICH, then BS shall group antenna 0 and 3 for the first subcarrier and antenna 1 and 2 for the second subcarrier. In matrix form, it shall be read as
$$
A_{3}=\left[\begin{array}{cccc}
s_{1} & -s_{2}{ }^{*} & 0 & 0  \tag{124r}\\
0 & 0 & s_{3} & -s_{4}{ }^{*} \\
0 & 0 & s_{4} & s_{3}{ }^{*} \\
s_{2} & s_{1}{ }^{*} & 0 & 0
\end{array}\right]
$$

\section*{Insert new subclause 8.4.8.3.5.2:}

\subsection*{8.4.8.3.5.2 Enhanced 4 Tx Matrix B with Antenna Grouping}

For 4 Tx antenna BS, transmission matrix B for rate 2 may be employed with antenna grouping information that is fed back on a CQICH from MS.

When MS reports 0 b 110001 on its allocated CQICH, then BS shall group antenna 0 and 1 for the first diversity pair and antenna 2 and 3 for the second diversity pair. In matrix form, it shall be read as
$$
B_{1}=\left[\begin{array}{ccccc} 
& & { }^{*} & &  \tag{124s}\\
s_{1} & -s_{2} & s_{5} & -s_{7} \\
& & { }^{*} & & \\
s_{2} & s_{1} & s_{7} & s_{5} \\
& & & & \\
s_{3} & -s_{4}{ }^{*} & s_{6} & -s_{8} \\
& & & & \\
s_{4} & s_{3} & & s_{8} & s_{6}
\end{array}\right]
$$

When MS reports 0 b 110010 on its allocated CQICH, then BS shall transmit in the following transmission matrix:

When MS reports 0 b 110011 on its allocated CQICH, then BS shall group antenna 0 and 2 for the first diversity pair and antenna 1 and 3 for the second diversity pair. In matrix form, it shall be read as
$$
B_{3}=\left[\begin{array}{cccc}
s_{1}-s_{2}{ }^{*} & s_{5} & -s_{7}{ }^{*}  \tag{124u}\\
s_{3}-s_{4}{ }^{*} & s_{6} & -s_{8}{ }^{*} \\
s_{2} & s_{1}{ }^{*} & & s_{7} \\
s_{4} & s_{3}{ }^{*} & s_{8} & s_{6}{ }^{*}
\end{array}\right]
$$

When MS reports 0 b 110100 on its allocated CQICH, then BS shall transmit in the following transmission matrix:

When MS reports 0 b 110101 on its allocated CQICH, then BS shall group antenna 0 and 3 for the first diversity pair and antenna 1 and 2 for the second diversity pair. In matrix form, it shall be read as
$$
B_{5}=\left[\begin{array}{ccccc}
s_{1}-s_{2} & { }^{*} & & s_{5} & -s_{7}{ }^{*}  \tag{124w}\\
s_{3}-s_{4} & { }^{*} & s_{6} & -s_{8}{ }^{*} \\
& & { }^{*} & & \\
s_{4} & s_{3} & s_{8} & s_{6}{ }^{*} \\
& & & & \\
s_{2} & s_{1} & s_{7} & s_{5}
\end{array}\right]
$$

When MS reports 0b110110 on its allocated CQICH, then BS shall transmit in the following transmission matrix:
$$
B_{6}=\left[\begin{array}{ccccc}
s_{1}-s_{2} & { }^{*} & & s_{5} & -s_{7}{ }^{*}  \tag{124x}\\
s_{4} & s_{3}{ }^{*} & s_{8} & s_{6}{ }^{*} \\
& & { }^{*} & & { }^{*} \\
s_{3}-s_{4} & s_{6} & -s_{8} \\
s_{2} & s_{1}{ }^{*} & & s_{7} & s_{5}
\end{array}\right]
$$

\section*{Insert new subclause 8.4.8.3.5.3:}

\subsection*{8.4.8.3.5.3 4 Tx Matrix $\mathbf{C}$ with Antenna Selection}

For 4-transmit antennas BS, the transmission matrix is listed in Table 316g, where the mapping of the matrix $C_{n}$ to the CQICH is shown. The active antenna is power boosted.

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 316g-Mapping of Pre-coding matrix and CQICH for 4 Tx Matrix C with Antenna Selection}
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|l|l|l|l|}
\hline \multirow{2}{*}{Streams, k} & \multicolumn{6}{|c|}{CQICH (binary)} & \multirow{2}{*}{Power boosting} \\
\hline & 0b110000 (option 1) & 0b110001 (option 2) & 0b110010 (option 3) & 0b110011 (option 4) & 0b110100 (option 5) & 0b110101 (option 6) & \\
\hline 1 & $C_{1}=c\left[\begin{array}{c}s_{1} \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ 0\end{array}\right]$ & $C_{2}=c\left[\begin{array}{c}0 \\ s_{1} \\ 0 \\ 0\end{array}\right]$ & $C_{3}=c\left[\begin{array}{c}0 \\ 0 \\ s_{1} \\ 0\end{array}\right]$ & $C_{4}=c\left[\begin{array}{c}0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ s_{1}\end{array}\right]$ & & & $c=1$ \\
\hline 2 & $C_{1}=c\left[\begin{array}{c}s_{1} \\ s_{2} \\ 0 \\ 0\end{array}\right]$ & $C_{2}=c\left[\begin{array}{c}s_{1} \\ 0 \\ s_{2} \\ 0\end{array}\right]$ & $C_{3}=c\left[\begin{array}{c}s_{1} \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ s_{2}\end{array}\right]$ & $C_{4}=c\left[\begin{array}{c}0 \\ s_{1} \\ s_{2} \\ 0\end{array}\right]$ & $C_{5}=c\left[\begin{array}{c}0 \\ s_{1} \\ 0 \\ s_{2}\end{array}\right]$ & $C_{6}=c\left[\begin{array}{c}0 \\ 0 \\ s_{1} \\ s_{2}\end{array}\right]$ & $c=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ \\
\hline 3 & $C_{1}=c\left[\begin{array}{c}s_{1} \\ s_{2} \\ s_{3} \\ 0\end{array}\right]$ & $C_{2}=c\left[\begin{array}{c}s_{1} \\ s_{2} \\ 0 \\ s_{3}\end{array}\right]$ & $C_{3}=c\left[\begin{array}{c}s_{1} \\ 0 \\ s_{2} \\ s_{3}\end{array}\right]$ & $C_{4}=c\left[\begin{array}{c}0 \\ s_{1} \\ s_{2} \\ s_{3}\end{array}\right]$ & & & $c=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$ \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

Stream $k=2$ indicates TLV=176, with Bit \#1 and Bit\#16 set
Stream $k=3$ indicates TLV=176, with Bit \#11 and Bit\#16 set

\section*{Insert new subclause 8.4.8.3.6:}

\subsection*{8.4.8.3.6 MIMO precoding}

The space time coding output can be weighted by a matrix before mapping onto transmit antennas:
$$
z=W x
$$
where $x$ is a $M_{t} \times 1$ vector with the output from the space-time coding (per-subcarrier), $\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{t}}$ is the number of streams at the output of the space-time coding scheme. The matrix $W$ is an $N_{t} \times M_{t}$ weighting matrix where the quantity $\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{t}}$ is the number of actual transmit antennas. The vector $z$ contains the signals after weighting for the different actual antennas. The labeling of the elements in the weighting matrix $W$ is performed in accordance with the example of $W$ given in Equation ( 124 y ) for the case of 4 actual antennas and 2 spacetime coding output streams:
$$
W=\left[\begin{array}{ll}
W_{11} & W_{12}  \tag{124y}\\
W_{21} & W_{22} \\
W_{31} & W_{32} \\
W_{41} & W_{42}
\end{array}\right]
$$

\section*{Short-term closed loop precoding:}

When $M_{t}=1$, then single stream precoding or beamforming shall be applied with the vector $W$ of dimension $N_{t} \times 1$. The transmission scheme before the precoder is the regular single antenna transmission. When $M_{t}=2,3$ or 4, then the two, three, or four STC output streams shall be transmitted with a precoding matrix of dimension $N_{t} \times 2, N_{t} \times 3, N_{t} \times 4$.

When using CQICH feedback type 0b100 (Index to precoding matrix in codebook) in Table 298a, the number of bits in the precoding matrix index in the codebook is determined by the number of bits in the CQICH Type (also in Table 298a).

\section*{Long-term closed loop precoding:}

The rank of the precoding matrix is indicated in the long-term precoding feedback from the SS . The number of columns in the precoding matrix equals its rank. The STC scheme used, Matrix A, B or C, is selected from the set of STC schemes associated with the number of transmit antennas equaling the rank of the longterm precoding matrix used. For example, if the rank of the long-term precoding matrix is 2 and the spatial rate used is 1 then the Matrix A scheme for 2 Tx antennas is used.

When the long-term closed loop precoding is turned on, the life span of short-term precoding information, the rank of the long-term precoding codebook used and the index to the precoding matrix in the specified long-term precoding codebook is fed back with MAC-header feedback messages 0 b 0000 and 0 b 0001 . If a short-term precoding matrix is available, the BS shall use this short-term matrix. If not, the BS shall use the fed back long-term precoding matrix, if available.

The long-term closed loop precoding uses the 6-bit codebook as specified in 8.4.5.4.10.12.

\section*{Feeding back multiple precoder for band AMC operation}

For band AMC the BS has the choice to request a common precoding matrix for all bands or can request a programmable number, N (see Table 298a), of precoding matrices to be fed back for the N best bands selected in an ordered fashion. In the latter case, the precoding matrices are associated with the bands with the highest $\mathrm{S} / \mathrm{N}$ values. As a secondary selection criteria, in case the ordering according to highest $\mathrm{S} / \mathrm{N}$ is not unique, the bands with the lowest band index are chosen first. The index for each precoder is mapped to a CQICH channel of the corresponding size. The precoders for the different bands, in the order described previously, is signaled in the corresponding CQICH channels.

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 316h-Feedback for long-term precoding in MAC feedback header message}
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|}
\hline MAC-header feedback type bit indication (binary) & Feedback element & Number of bits & Description \\
\hline 01000 & Feedback of index to long-term precoding matrix in codebook & 6 & Index to long-term precoding matrix element in codebook \\
\hline 01000 & Rank of precoding codebook & 2 & $k$, Rank of precoding codebook $= k+1$ \\
\hline 01000 & FEC and QAM feedback & 6 & FEC and QAM specification \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 316i-Feedback for life span of short-term precoding in MAC feedback header message 0b01001}
\begin{tabular}{|c|c|}
\hline \begin{tabular}{c} 
Bit field (N) \\
(binary)
\end{tabular} & Life span in number of frames \\
\hline $0000-1111$ & $0.125 * 2^{(N+1)}$ \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\section*{Precoding state feed forward and precoding application delay}

If the precoding state is not fed forward in the DL burst allocation IE, then the BS shall apply precoding according to the precoding feedback from the SS (antenna grouping, antenna selection or codebook based) with a predetermined number of frames delay.

\section*{Insert new subclause 8.4.8.4:}

\subsection*{8.4.8.4 STC for the optional zones in the uplink}

Two optional zones in the uplink, the optional PUSC and the optional AMC zones, are described in 8.4.6.2.5 and 8.4.6.3, respectively. STC may be used to improve system performance for these zones. Furthermore, two single transmit antenna MSs can perform collaborative spatial multiplexing onto the same subcarrier.

\section*{Insert new subclause 8.4.8.4.1:}

\subsection*{8.4.8.4.1 Allocation of pilot subcarriers}

For 2-antenna MS and the optional PUSC, pilots for each antenna shall be allocated as shown in Figure 2511.

\begin{figure}
\includegraphics[alt={},max width=\textwidth]{https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/a227cdd5-cf57-46a1-bc9f-bb964def4187-640.jpg?height=471&width=1288&top_left_y=483&top_left_x=380}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Figure 251—Uplink pilot allocation for 2-antenna MS for the optional PUSC zones}
\end{figure}

For 2-antenna MS and the optional AMC, pilot allocation pattern shall be identical to that for the downlink optional AMC with 2 antennas described in 8.4.8.3.1.1-all pilots in the even symbols shall be allocated for antenna 0 or pattern-A, while pilots in the odd symbols shall be allocated for antenna 1 or pattern-B. This is shown in Figure 251f.

Two single transmit antenna MSs can perform collaborative spatial multiplexing onto the same subcarrier. In this case, one MS should use uplink pilot allocation with pattern-A, and the other MS should use the uplink pilot allocation with pattern-B.

\section*{Insert new subclause 8.4.8.4.2:}

\subsection*{8.4.8.4.2 Allocation of data subchannels}

For the optional PUSC permutation with matrix A in 8.4.8.4.3, the data subchannels shall be allocated for two consecutive slots in time. As can be seen in Figure 251m, STC encoded data symbols shall be time mapped over two OFDMA symbols. The mapping starts at the lowest numbered subcarriers of lowest slot and continues in an ascending manner in subchannels first and then proceeds to the next two symbols in time.

For 2-antenna matrix B in the optional PUSC permutation, modulated data symbols shall be sequentially mapped for two transmit antennas along the subcarriers in the symbol. The mapping continues in an ascending manner in subchannels first and then proceeds to the next symbol in time.

\begin{figure}
\includegraphics[alt={},max width=\textwidth]{https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/a227cdd5-cf57-46a1-bc9f-bb964def4187-641.jpg?height=427&width=1370&top_left_y=302&top_left_x=360}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Figure 251m-Uplink data mapping for 2-antenna SS with matrix A for the optional PUSC permutation}
\end{figure}

For the uplink optional AMC permutation with matrix A and B , the data subchannels shall take $1 \times 6$ ( 1 bin for 6 symbols) format. The subcarrier permutation represented by Equation (112) in 8.4.6.3 shall not be applied for the uplink optional AMC permutation with matrix A and B. The data mapping rule is identical to that for the downlink AMC permutation with 2 antennas.

\section*{Insert new subclause 8.4.8.4.3:}

\subsection*{8.4.8.4.3 Transmission schemes for 2-antenna MS in UL}

The following matrices define the transmission format with the row index indicating antenna number and column index indicating OFDMA symbol time. For both UL permutation zones with 2 -antenna MS, one of the following two transmission matrices shall be used:
$$
\begin{aligned}
A & =\left[\begin{array}{cc}
S_{i} & -S_{i+1}^{*} \\
S_{i+1} & S_{i}^{*}
\end{array}\right] \\
B & =\left[\begin{array}{c}
S_{i} \\
S_{i+1}
\end{array}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$
where $S_{i}$ and $S_{i+1}$ may be encoded in different rates.
The matrix B may also be used for two single antenna MSs to share the same subchannel (collaborative spatial multiplexing).

\section*{Insert new subclause 8.4.8.5:}

\subsection*{8.4.8.5 MIMO midamble}

The MIMO midamble consists of one OFDM symbol that is mapped onto multiple antennas. Non-overlapping subcarriers are allotted to the transmit antennas.

For FUSC and optional FUSC, the antenna to subcarrier mapping is shown in Figure 251n. Subcarriers index starts from the first one after the left guard band. DC subcarrier is also included in the numbering but nulled prior to transmission. The midamble carrier-set is defined using the following formula:
$$
\text { Midamble_Carrier_Set }=-\left(N_{\text {used }} / 2\right)+n+2 k\left\lceil\frac{N_{t}}{2}\right\rceil
$$
where
$N_{t} \quad$ is the number of transmit antennas ( 2,3 , or 4 ),
$n \quad$ is the antenna index $\left(0,1, \ldots, N_{t}-1 ; N_{t} \leqq 4\right)$,
$k \quad$ is the subcarrier running index.
The subcarrier to antenna mapping is depicted in Figure 251n for the case when $N_{t}=4$. The midamble sequence has the identical IDcell and segment mapping as the preamble.

\begin{figure}
\includegraphics[alt={},max width=\textwidth]{https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/a227cdd5-cf57-46a1-bc9f-bb964def4187-642.jpg?height=367&width=773&top_left_y=792&top_left_x=693}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Figure 251n-Midamble FUSC structure (frequency domain)}
\end{figure}

For PUSC, the subchannel permutations and grouping remains same as for the data. Instead all the subcarriers are used as pilots. Only one symbol is used as the midamble. The midamble is allocated to the subcarriers and antennas as follows.
$$
\text { Midamble_Carrier_Set }=-\left(N_{u s e d} / 2\right)+n+N_{t} k
$$
where
$N_{t} \quad$ is the number of transmit antennas,
$n \quad$ is the antenna index $1, \ldots, N_{t}-1$,
$k \quad$ is the subcarrier running index.
Figure 251 o shows the antenna to subcarrier mapping for the case when $N_{t}=4$.

\begin{figure}
\includegraphics[alt={},max width=\textwidth]{https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/a227cdd5-cf57-46a1-bc9f-bb964def4187-642.jpg?height=407&width=1080&top_left_y=1929&top_left_x=517}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Figure 2510-Midamble PUSC structure (frequency domain)}
\end{figure}

\section*{Insert new subclause 8.4.8.5.1:}

\subsection*{8.4.8.5.1 Midamble Sequence for PUSC}

The midamble sequence for PUSC shall be obtained from the corresponding sequence used in the FUSC or optional FUSC for the relevant FFT size and number of antennas. For PUSC, however, all the used subcarriers shall further be clustered and divided into different segments as in the data traffic region.

\section*{Insert new subclause 8.4.8.5.2:}

\subsection*{8.4.8.5.2 Midamble Sequence for FUSC and optional FUSC}

The subcarrier locations and corresponding PN code BPSK modulation of subcarriers in a midamble are defined as in Equation (124z) and Equation (124aa). The DC carrier is nulled.
$$
\begin{align*}
& P_{I D_{\text {cell }} s}\left[k_{\text {foi }}\right]=\left\{\begin{array}{cc}
1-(2) q_{I D_{\text {cell }}}[m], & k_{\text {foi }}=2 m\left\lceil\frac{N_{t}}{2}\right\rceil-\frac{N_{\text {used }}}{2}+n, m=0,1, \ldots, \frac{N_{\text {used }}}{2\left\lceil\frac{N_{t}}{2}\right\rceil}-1 \\
0, & \text { otherwise }
\end{array}\right.  \tag{124z}\\
& q_{I D_{\text {cell }}}[m]=\{  \tag{124aa}\\
& R\left(8\left\lfloor\frac{m}{9}\right\rfloor+m \bmod 9\right), \\
& m \bmod 9=0,1,2, \ldots, 7 \\
& T\left(\left\lfloor\frac{m}{9}\right\rfloor, \quad\right. \\
& \left.m=0,1, \ldots, \frac{N_{\text {used }}}{2\left\lceil\frac{N_{t}}{2}\right\rceil}\right)-1 \\
& m \bmod 9=8
\end{align*}
$$

The sequence $R(r)$ in (2) is either one of the following two formulas, depending on $N_{\mathrm{FFT}}$ and $N_{\mathrm{T}}$. The choice of the sequence $R(r)$, and the length of sequence $\underline{N}_{\underline{r}}$ supporting the choice are shown in Table 316j.
$$
\begin{align*}
& R_{1}(r)=H_{128}\left(I D_{\text {cell }}+1, \Pi_{\left\lfloor\frac{r}{128}\right\rfloor}(r m o d 128)\right) ; r=8\left\lfloor\frac{m}{9}\right\rfloor+m \bmod 9=0,1, \ldots, N_{r}-1  \tag{124ab}\\
& R_{2}(r)=B_{I D_{\text {cell }}+1} g_{\Pi(r)} ; r=8\left\lfloor\frac{m}{9}\right\rfloor+m \bmod 9=0,1, \ldots, N_{r}-1 \tag{124ac}
\end{align*}
$$

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 316j- $N_{\mathrm{r}}$ [The length of sequence $R(r)$ ]}
\begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline $\boldsymbol{N}_{\boldsymbol{T}}$ & \multicolumn{4}{|c|}{$\mathbf{2}$} & \multicolumn{4}{c|}{ 3 or 4 } \\
\hline$N_{F F T}$ & 2048 & 1024 & 512 & 128 & 2048 & 1024 & 512 & 128 \\
\hline$R_{1}(r)$ & 768 & 384 & 192 & - & 384 & 192 & - & - \\
\hline$R_{2}(r)$ & - & - & - & 48 & - & - & 96 & 24 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

In $R_{1}(r), H_{128}(i, j)$ denotes the number at $(i, j)$ of order 128 Walsh Hadamard matrix, where $i, j=0,1, \ldots$, 127. The first low vector of $H_{128}$ is the all-one sequence and shall not be used. $\Pi_{\left\lfloor\frac{m}{128}\right\rfloor}(l) ; l=0,1, \ldots, 127$ is the $l$-th value of the $\left\lfloor\frac{m}{128}\right\rfloor^{\text {th }}$ permutation out of six predefined permutations shown in Table 316 k .

\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}

\hline $\Pi_{1}(l)$ & $25,77,103,114,57,93,111,118,59,92,46,23,74,37,83,104,52,26,13,71,98,49,89,109,119$, 122, 61, 95, 110, 55, 90, 45, 87, 106, 53, 91, 108, 54, 27, 76, 38, 19, 72, 36, 18, 9, 69, 99, 112, 56, 28, $14,7,66,33,81,105,117,123,124,62,31,78,39,82,41,85,107,116,58,29,79,102,51,88,44$, $22,11,68,34,17,73,101,115,120,60,30,15,70,35,80,40,20,10,5,67,96,48,24,12,6,3,64$, $32,16,8,4,2,1,65,97,113,121,125,127,126,63,94,47,86,43,84,42,21,75,100,50,0$ \\
\hline $\Pi_{2}(l)$ & $71,98,49,89,109,119,122,61,95,110,55,90,45,87,106,53,91,108,54,27,76,38,19,72,36$, $18,9,69,99,112,56,28,14,7,66,33,81,105,117,123,124,62,31,78,39,82,41,85,107,116,58$, $29,79,102,51,88,44,22,11,68,34,17,73,101,115,120,60,30,15,70,35,80,40,20,10,5,67$, $96,48,24,12,6,3,64,32,16,8,4,2,1,65,97,113,121,125,127,126,63,94,47,86,43,84,42,21$, $75,100,50,25,77,103,114,57,93,111,118,59,92,46,23,74,37,83,104,52,26,13,0$ \\
\hline $\Pi_{3}(l)$ & $69,99,112,56,28,14,7,66,33,81,105,117,123,124,62,31,78,39,82,41,85,107,116,58,29$, $79,102,51,88,44,22,11,68,34,17,73,101,115,120,60,30,15,70,35,80,40,20,10,5,67,96$, $48,24,12,6,3,64,32,16,8,4,2,1,65,97,113,121,125,127,126,63,94,47,86,43,84,42,21,75$, $100,50,25,77,103,114,57,93,111,118,59,92,46,23,74,37,83,104,52,26,13,71,98,49,89$, 109, 119, 122, 61, 95, 110, 55, 90, 45, 87, 106, 53, 91, 108, 54, 27, 76, 38, 19, 72, 36, 18, 9, 0 \\
\hline $\Pi_{4}(l)$ & $102,51,88,44,22,11,68,34,17,73,101,115,120,60,30,15,70,35,80,40,20,10,5,67,96,48$, $24,12,6,3,64,32,16,8,4,2,1,65,97,113,121,125,127,126,63,94,47,86,43,84,42,21,75$, $100,50,25,77,103,114,57,93,111,118,59,92,46,23,74,37,83,104,52,26,13,71,98,49,89$, $109,119,122,61,95,110,55,90,45,87,106,53,91,108,54,27,76,38,19,72,36,18,9,69,99$, $112,56,28,14,7,66,33,81,105,117,123,124,62,31,78,39,82,41,85,107,116,58,29,79,0$ \\
\hline $\Pi_{5}(l)$ & $70,35,80,40,20,10,5,67,96,48,24,12,6,3,64,32,16,8,4,2,1,65,97,113,121,125,127,126$, $63,94,47,86,43,84,42,21,75,100,50,25,77,103,114,57,93,111,118,59,92,46,23,74,37,83$, 104, 52, 26, 13, 71, 98, 49, 89, 109, 119, 122, 61, 95, 110, 55, 90, 45, 87, 106, 53, 91, 108, 54, 27, 76, $38,19,72,36,18,9,69,99,112,56,28,14,7,66,33,81,105,117,123,124,62,31,78,39,82,41$, $85,107,116,58,29,79,102,51,88,44,22,11,68,34,17,73,101,115,120,60,30,15,0$ \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

In $R 2(r)$, if the $k, 1 \leq k \leq 127$ can be converted into binary as $b_{6} b_{5} b_{4} b_{3} b_{2} b_{1} b_{0}$, define $b_{6}$ as the MSB, $b_{0}$ as the LSB, $b_{k}$ is a row vector to represent $B_{k}=\left[b_{0} b_{1} b_{2} b_{3} b_{4} b_{5} b_{6}\right]$. The $g_{u} ; 0 \leq u \leq N_{r}-1$ is $u^{\text {th }}$ column vector of the generator matrix $G . B_{k} g_{u}$ is an inner product of ( $1 \times 7$ ) row vector and ( $7 \times 1$ ) column vector. The generator matrix $G$ and the permutation $\Pi(l) ; l=0,1,2, \ldots, N_{r}-1$ for each $N_{F F T}$ and $N_{T}$ are shown below.

The sequence $T(k)$ is determined by IDcell and should be chosen to achieve low PAPR.

\section*{Insert new subclause 8.4.8.5.2.1:}

\subsection*{8.4.8.5.2.1 PAPR reduction sequence for BS with 2 antennas}

The PAPR reduction sequences for BS with 2 antennas are listed in Table 3161, Table 316m, Table 316n for the cases of FFT size 2048 point, 1024 point, 512 point respectively. The case of FFT size 128 point is dealt with by Table 316c and Table 316p, together with Equation (124ad).

\section*{Insert new subclause 8.4.8.5.2.2:}

\subsection*{8.4.8.5.2.2 PAPR Reduction Sequence for BS with 3 or 4 antennas}

For FFT size $=2048$, the sequence $T(k)$ is the same as $N_{t}=2, N_{F F T}=1024$ case.
For FFT size $=1024$, the sequence $T(k)$ is the same as $N_{t}=2, N_{F F T}=512$ case.
For FFT size = 512,

\section*{Insert new subclause 8.4.8.9:}

\subsection*{8.4.8.9 STC subpacket combining}

In the STC transmission, for both downlink and uplink, the STC sub-packet re-transmission can be generated by using the Space time code incremental redundancy version. The transmission rule for space-time coded incremental redundancy codes set is listed in Table 316u, Table 316v, and Table 316w.

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 316u-STC subpacket combining (2-transmit antenna case)}
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|}
\hline & Initial transmission & Odd re-transmission & Even re-transmission \\
\hline Space time code incremental redundancy for matrix A & $S_{2}^{(0)}=\left[\begin{array}{c}S_{1} \\ S_{2}\end{array}\right]$ & $S_{2}^{(\text {odd })}=\left[\begin{array}{c}-S_{2}{ }^{*} \\ S_{1}{ }^{*}\end{array}\right]$ & $S_{2}^{(\text {even })}=\left[\begin{array}{c}S_{1} \\ S_{2}\end{array}\right]$ \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 316v-STC subpacket combining (3-transmit antenna case)}
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|}
\hline & Initial transmission & Odd re-transmission & Even re-transmission \\
\hline Space time code incremental redundancy for matrix C & $S_{2}^{(0)}=\left[\begin{array}{l}S_{1} \\ S_{2} \\ S_{3}\end{array}\right]$ & $S_{2}^{(o d d)}=\left[\begin{array}{c}-S^{*}{ }_{2} \\ S^{*}{ }_{1} \\ S^{*}{ }_{3}\end{array}\right]$ & $S_{2}^{(\text {even })}=\left[\begin{array}{c}S_{1} \\ S_{2} \\ S_{3}\end{array}\right]$ \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 316w-STC subpacket combining (4-transmit antenna case)}
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|}
\hline & initial transmission & odd re-transmission & even re-transmission \\
\hline Space time code incremental redundancy for matrix C & $S_{2}^{(0)}=\left[\begin{array}{c}S_{1} \\ S_{2} \\ S_{3} \\ S_{4}\end{array}\right]$ & $S_{2}^{(\text {odd })}=\left[\begin{array}{c}-S^{*}{ }_{2} \\ S^{*}{ }_{1} \\ -S^{*}{ }_{4} \\ S^{*}{ }_{3}\end{array}\right]$ & $S_{2}^{(\text {even })}=\left[\begin{array}{c}S_{1} \\ S_{2} \\ S_{3} \\ S_{4}\end{array}\right]$ \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

The MS shall process the initial transmission, first re-transmission, and second re-transmission etc. in the form of space time decoding. The re-transmission of FEC codeword shall use the Chase combing retransmission.

\subsection*{8.4.9 Channel coding}

\section*{Change the first paragraph as indicated:}

Channel coding procedures include randomization (see 8.4.9.1), FEC encoding (see 8.4.9.2), bit interleaving (see 8.4.9.3), repetition (see 8.4.9.5) and modulation (see 8.4.9.4). When repetition code is used, allocation for the transmission shall always include an even number of adjacent subchannels. The basic block shall pass the regular coding chain where the first subchannel shall set the randomization seed used in 8.4.9.1, and the data shall follow the coding chain up to the mapping. The data outputted from the modulation (8.4.9.4) shall be mapped onto the block of subchannels allocated for the basic block and then it will be also mapped on the following consecutive allocated subchannels (for repetition coding of 2, another block of subchannels of the same size is used; for repetition coding of 4, another 3 blocks of subchannels of the same size are used; and for repetition of 6 , another 5 blocks of subchannels of the same size are used), the process of regular encoding and repetition encoding is shown in Figure 252.

\section*{Replace Figure 252 with the following figure:}

\begin{figure}
\includegraphics[alt={},max width=\textwidth]{https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/a227cdd5-cf57-46a1-bc9f-bb964def4187-657.jpg?height=175&width=1443&top_left_y=1946&top_left_x=343}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Figure 252-Channel coding process for regular and repetition coding transmission}
\end{figure}

\section*{Insert the following sentence as the end of the subclause:}

Repetition shall only be applied to QPSK modulation.

\subsection*{8.4.9.1 Randomization}

\section*{Change the first paragraph as indicated:}

Data randomization is performed on all data transmitted on the downlink and uplink, except the FCH. The randomization is initialized on each FEC block (using the first Subchannel offset and OFDMA symbol offset on which the FEC bloek is mapped. Symbol offset, for both UL and DL, shall be counted from the start of the frame, where the DL preamble shall be count 0 ). If the amount of data to transmit does not fit exactly the amount of data allocated, padding of 0 xFF (" 1 " only) shall be added to the end of the transmission block, up to the amount of data allocated. Here, the amount of data allocated means the amount of data that corresponds to the amount of $\left\lfloor N_{s} / R\right\rfloor$ slots, where $N_{\underline{s}}$ is the number of the slots allocated for the data burst and $R$ is the repetition factor used.

\section*{Change the paragraph above Figure 254 as follows:}

The randomizer is initialized with the vector ereated as shown in Figure 254. [LSB]0110111000101 01 [MSB].

Change Figure 253 as indicated:

\begin{figure}
\includegraphics[alt={},max width=\textwidth]{https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/a227cdd5-cf57-46a1-bc9f-bb964def4187-658.jpg?height=473&width=1133&top_left_y=1044&top_left_x=429}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Figure 253-PRBS generator for data randomization}
\end{figure}

\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{OFDMA randomizer initialization vector}
\end{figure}

Figure 254a-Creation of the OFDMA randomizer initialization vector for HARQ

Insert the following text below Figure 254a:
HARQ requires that the randomizer pattern be identical for each HARQ attempt. For HARQ operation, the randomizer shall be initialized with the vector created as shown in Figure 254a.

\subsection*{8.4.9.2 Encoding}

\section*{Replace any instance of the word 'subchannel' with word 'slot'.}

\section*{Change the text in 8.4.9.2 as indicated:}

Concatenation of a number of subchannelsslots shall be performed in order to make larger blocks of coding where it is possible, with the limitation of not passingexceeding the largest supported block under the samesize for the applied modulation and coding rate (the block defined by 64-QAM modulation). Table 318 specifies the concatenation of subchannels for different allocations and modulations. The parameters in Table 317 and Table 318 shall apply to the CC encoding scheme (see 8.4.9.2.1) and the BTC encoding scheme (see 8.4.9.2.2); for the CTC encoding scheme (see 8.4.9.2.3), the concatenation rule is defined in 8.4.9.2.3.1, and for the LDPC encoding scheme (see 8.4.9.2.5) the concatenation rule is defined in 8.4.2.9.5.4.

\section*{Change the second bullet of the fourth paragraph as indicated:}
— $n:$ _ number of allocated subchannelsslots/repetition factor ⟂

\section*{Change Table 317 as indicated:}

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 317-Subchanne\$lots concatenation rule}
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|}
\hline Number of subehannelsslots & Subehannels Slots concatenated \\
\hline $n \leq j$ & 1 block of $n$ subchannelsslots \\
\hline $n>j$ & \begin{tabular}{l}
If $(n \bmod j=0)$ \\
$k$ blocks of $j$ slots \\
else \\
( $k-1$ ) blocks of $j$ subchannelsslots \\
1 block of ceil( $(m+j) / 2$ ) subchannelsslots \\
1 block of floor $((m+j) / 2)$ subchannelsslots
\end{tabular} \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\subsection*{8.4.9.2.1 Convolutional coding (CC)}

Change the title of Table 319 to read: "The inner convolutional code with puncturing configuration"
Remove the column with value '5/6' for the rate field in Table 319.
Change the title of Table 320 to read: "Useful data payload for a subehenelslot"
Insert new subclause 8.4.9.2.1.1:

\subsection*{8.4.9.2.1.1 Incremental Redundancy HARQ support (optional)}

HARQ implementation is optional. An Incremental Redundancy (IR) based HARQ is taking the puncture pattern into account, and for each retransmission the coded block is not the same. Different puncture patterns
are used to create HARQ packets identified by SPID. The puncture patterns are predefined or can be easily deducted from the original pattern, and can be selected based on SPID. At the receiver, the received signals are de-punctured according to its specific puncture pattern, which is decided by the current SPID, then the combination is performed at bit metrics level.

The puncture pattern for the HARQ packet with SPID=0 is the same as the mandatory one in Table 320a. The puncture pattern for the HARQ packet with SPID $=1$ is the left cyclic shift of the one from SPID $=0$, as shown in Table 320a. Following the same rule, the puncture patterns for packets with SPID $=2$ and SPID $=3$ are shown in Table 320a.

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 320a-Puncture pattern definition for HARQ}
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|l|l|}
\hline \multicolumn{2}{|c|}{\multirow{2}{*}{}} & \multicolumn{4}{|c|}{Code Rate} \\
\hline & & 1/2 & 2/3 & 3/4 & 5/6 \\
\hline \multirow{2}{*}{$\mathbf{S P I D} \boldsymbol{=} \mathbf{0}$} & X & 1 & 10 & 101 & 10101 \\
\hline & Y & 1 & 11 & 110 & 11010 \\
\hline \multirow{2}{*}{$\mathrm{SPID}=\mathbf{1}$} & X & 1 & 01 & 011 & 01011 \\
\hline & Y & 1 & 11 & 101 & 10101 \\
\hline \multirow{2}{*}{$\mathbf{S P I D} \boldsymbol{=} \mathbf{2}$} & X & 1 & 10 & 110 & 10110 \\
\hline & Y & 1 & 11 & 011 & 01011 \\
\hline \multirow{2}{*}{$\mathbf{S P I D} \boldsymbol{=} \mathbf{3}$} & X & 1 & 01 & 101 & 01101 \\
\hline & Y & 1 & 11 & 110 & 10110 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\subsection*{8.4.9.2.3 Convolutional turbo codes (optional)}

The CTC encoder, including its constituent encoder, is depicted in Figure 257. The CTC defined in this subclause can be used for the support of the optional hybrid ARQ (HARQ).

A_{\theta}, B_{\theta}, \ldots, A_{N-1}, B_{N-1}, Y_{1,0}, Y_{1,1}, \ldots, Y_{1, N-1}, Y_{2,0}, Y_{2,1}, \ldots, Y_{2, N-1}, W_{1,0}, W_{1,1}, \ldots, W_{1, N-1}, W_{2,0}, W_{2,1}, \ldots, W_{2, N-1} \\
A_{0}, A_{1}, \ldots, A_{N-1}, B_{0}, B_{1}, \ldots, B_{N-1}, Y_{1,0}, Y_{1,1}, \ldots, Y_{1, N-1}, Y_{2,0}, Y_{2,1}, \ldots, Y_{2, N-1} \\
W_{1,0}, W_{1,1}, \ldots, W_{1, N-1}, W_{2,0}, W_{2,1}, \ldots, W_{2, N-1}

$$

\section*{Change the subclause as indicated:}

The interleaver requires the parameters $P_{0}, P_{1}, P_{2}$ and $P_{t 3}$, shown in Table 326 .

Let the sequence $\underline{u}_{0}=\left[\left(A_{0}, \underline{B}_{0}\right),\left(A_{1}, \underline{B}_{1}\right),\left(A_{2}, \underline{B}_{2}\right),\left(A_{3}, \underline{B}_{3}\right), \ldots,\left(A_{N-1}, \underline{B}_{N-1}\right)\right]$ be the input to first encoding $C_{1}$.
for $\dot{j} \underline{i}=0 \ldots N-1$
if $\left(j_{\text {mod }_{2}}=0\right)$ let $(B, A)-(A, B)$ (i.e., switch the couple)
if (i $\bmod 2==1$ ) let $\left(A_{\underline{i}}, B_{\underline{i}}\right) \rightarrow\left(B_{\underline{i}}, A_{\underline{i}}\right)$ (i.e., switch the couple)
This step gives a sequence $u_{1}=\left[\left(A_{0}, B_{0}\right),\left(B_{1}, A_{1}\right),\left(A_{2}, B_{2}\right),\left(B_{2}, A_{3}\right), \ldots\right.$,
$\left.\left(B_{N-1}, A_{N-1}\right)\right]=\left[u_{1}(0), u_{1}(1), u_{1}(2), u_{1}(3), \ldots, u_{1}(N-1)\right]$.
Step 2: $\boldsymbol{P}_{\boldsymbol{i}}(\boldsymbol{j})$
The function $P_{i}(j)$ provides the interleaved address $i$ of the consider couple $j$ the address of the couple of the sequence $u_{1}$ that shall be mapped onto the address $j$ of the interleaved sequence $\left(\right.$ i.e., $\left.u_{2}(j)=u_{1}(P(j))\right)$.
for $j=0 \ldots N-1$
switch $j_{\text {mod }}$.
ease 0:- $\left(P_{0} j+1\right)_{\bmod N}$
ease 1: $=\left(P_{0} \cdot j+1+N / 2+P_{1}\right)_{\text {mod }}$
ease 2: $=\left(P_{0} j+1+P_{2}\right)_{\bmod }$
ease 3:- $\left(P_{0} j+1+N / 2+P_{3}\right)_{\text {mod }}$
switch $j \bmod 4$ :
case $0: P(j)=\left(P_{0} \cdot j+1\right)_{\text {modt }}$
case 1: $(j)=\left(P_{0} \cdot j+1+N / 2+P_{1}\right)_{\text {mod }}$
case $2 \cdot P(j)=\left(P_{0} \cdot j+1+P_{2}\right)_{\text {mod }}$
case 3: $(j)=\left(P_{0} \cdot j+1+N / 2+P_{3}\right)_{\text {mod }}$
This step gives a sequence $u_{2}=\left[u_{1}(P(0)), u_{1}(P(1)), u_{1}(P(2)), u_{1}(P(3)), \ldots, u_{1}(P(N-1))\right]=\left[\left(B_{P(0)}\right.\right.$, $\left.\left.\underline{A}_{P(0)}\right),\left(A_{P(1)}, B_{P(1)}\right),\left(B_{P(2)}, A_{P(2)}\right),\left(A_{P(3)}, B_{P(3)}\right), \ldots,\left(A_{P(N-1)}, B_{P(N-1)}\right)\right]$. Sequence $u_{2}$ is the input to the second encoding $C_{2}$.

\section*{Change the subclause as indicated:}

The procedure of HARQ CTC subpacket generation is as follows: Padding, CRC addition, Fragmentation, Randomization and CTC encoding. HARQ HARQ implementation is optional. The randomization block in 8.4.9.1, the concatenation scheme in 8.4.9.2.3.1, and the interleaving in 8.4.9.3 shall not be applied for the encoding described in this subclause.

\subsection*{8.4.9.2.3.5.1 Padding}

\section*{Change the last sentence of the first paragraph as indicated:}

\subsection*{8.4.9.2.3.5.2 CRC encoding}

\section*{Change the second paragraph as indicated:}

The size of the CRC is 16 bits. CRC16-CCITT, as defined in ITU-T Recommendation X.25, shall be included at the end of the padded and randomized packet. The CRC covers both the padded bits and the information part of the padded and randomized packet. After the CRC operation, Tthe packet size shall belong to set $\{48,96,144,192,288,384,480,960,1920,2880,3840,4800,9600,14400,19200,24000\}$.

\section*{Renumber subclause 8.4.9.2.3.5.4 as 8.4.9.2.3.5.3}

\subsection*{8.4.9.2.3.5.4 Randomization}

\section*{Change the first two paragraphs as indicated:}

The randomization is performed on each allocation (burst)encoder packet, which means that for each allocation of a data blockencoder packet the randomizer shall be usedinitialized independently.

The PRBS generator shall be $1+X^{14}+X^{15}$ as shown in Figure 260. Each data byte to be transmitted shall enter sequentially into the randomizer, MSB first. Preambles are not randomized. The seed value shall be used to calculate the randomization bits, which are combined in an XOR operation with the serialized bit stream of each FEC block. The randemizer sequence is applied to the output from the padding block. The bit issued from the randomizer shall be applied to the CRC encoder.

\subsection*{8.4.9.2.3.5.6 Modulation order of DL traffic burst}

\section*{Change the first paragraph as indicated:}

For DL, the modulation order (2 for QPSK, 4 for 16 -QAM, and 6 for $64-\mathrm{QAM}$ ) shall be set for all the allowed transmission formats as shown in Table 331. The transmission format is given by the $N_{E P}$ (Encoding Packet Size) and the $N_{S C H}$ (number of allotted subchannelsslots). $N_{E P}$ per an encoding packet is $\{144$, $192,288,384,480,960,1920,2880,3840,4800\}$. The $N_{S C H}$ per an encoding packet is $\{1, \ldots, 480\}$. In Table 331 , the numbers in the first row are $N_{E P}$ 's and the numbers in the remaining rows are $N_{S C H}$ 's and related parameters. In Table 331, the modulation order is denoted by MOD, and it has the values of two for QPSK, four for 16-QAM, and six for 64-QAM. SCH denotes for the number of allocated slots.

\section*{Change the third paragraph below Equation (128) as indicated:}

The information of $N_{E P}$ and $N_{S C H}$ shall be signaled in HEHARQ MAP. Instead of the actual values of $N_{E P}$ and $N_{S C H}$, the encoded value of $N_{E P}$ ( $N_{E P}$ code) and $N_{S C H}$ ( $N_{S C H}$ code) shall be used for the signaling. They are encoded by 4 bits, respectively. The encoding of $N_{E P}$ ( $N_{E P}$ code) is shown in Table 332. The encoding of $N_{S C H}$ (Nsch code) is performed per $N_{E P}$ value. For each $N_{E P}$, there are less than 16 kinds of $N_{S C H}$ values and they are encoded from " 0 "(the smallest number of subchannelsslots) to " 15 " in increasing order. When the number of $N_{S C H}$ values for a $N_{E P}$ is smaller than 16, the smallest number of the smallest codes are used. When the fragmentation is applied and the number of the subpackets for an allocation is $n, n^{*} N_{E P}$ and $N_{S C H}$ (the number of subchannelsslots allocated for a subpacket) should be signaled.

\section*{Insert new subclause 8.4.9.2.5:}

\subsection*{8.4.9.2.5 Low Density Parity Check code (optional)}

\section*{Insert new subclause 8.4.9.2.5.1:}

\subsection*{8.4.9.2.5.1 Code description}

The LDPC code is based on a set of one or more fundamental LDPC codes. Each of the fundamental codes is a systematic linear block code. Using the described methods in 8.4.9.2.5.2 Code Rate and Block Size Adjustment, the fundamental codes can accommodate various code rates and packet sizes.

Each LDPC code in the set of LDPC codes is defined by a matrix $\mathbf{H}$ of size $m$-by- $n$, where $n$ is the length of the code and $m$ is the number of parity check bits in the code. The number of systematic bits is $k=n-m$.

The matrix $\mathbf{H}$ is defined as:
$$
\mathbf{H}=\left[\begin{array}{cccccc}
\mathbf{P}_{0,0} & \mathbf{P}_{0,1} & \mathbf{P}_{0,2} & \ldots & \mathbf{P}_{0, n_{b}-2} & \mathbf{P}_{0, n_{b}-1} \\
\mathbf{P}_{1,0} & \mathbf{P}_{1,1} & \mathbf{P}_{1,2} & \ldots & \mathbf{P}_{1, n_{b}-2} & \mathbf{P}_{1, n_{b}-1} \\
\mathbf{P}_{2,0} & \mathbf{P}_{2,1} & \mathbf{P}_{2,2} & \ldots & \mathbf{P}_{2, n_{b}-2} & \mathbf{P}_{0, n_{b}-1} \\
\ldots & \ldots & \ldots & \ldots & \ldots & \ldots \\
\mathbf{P}_{m_{b}-1,0} & \mathbf{P}_{m_{b}-1,1} & \mathbf{P}_{m_{b}-1,2} & \ldots & \mathbf{P}_{m_{b}-1, n_{b}-2} & \mathbf{P}_{m_{b}-1, n_{b}-1}
\end{array}\right]=\mathbf{P}^{H_{b}}
$$
where $\mathbf{P}_{i, j}$ is one of a set of $z$-by- $z$ permutation matrices or a $z$-by- $z$ zero matrix. The matrix $\mathbf{H}$ is expanded from a binary base matrix $\mathbf{H}_{\mathrm{b}}$ of size $m_{b}$-by- $n_{b}$, where $n=z \cdot n_{b}$ and $m=z \cdot m_{b}$, with $z$ an integer $\check{\mathrm{S}} 1$. The base matrix is expanded by replacing each 1 in the base matrix with a $z$-by- $z$ permutation matrix, and each 0 with a $z$-by- $z$ zero matrix. The base matrix size $n_{b}$ is an integer equal to 24 .

The permutations used are circular right shifts, and the set of permutation matrices contains the $z \times z$ identity matrix and circular right shifted versions of the identity matrix. Because each permutation matrix is specified by a single circular right shift, the binary base matrix information and permutation replacement information can be combined into a single compact model matrix $\mathbf{H}_{\mathrm{bm}}$. The model matrix $\mathbf{H}_{\mathrm{bm}}$ is the same size as the binary base matrix $\mathbf{H}_{\mathrm{b}}$, with each binary entry $(i, j)$ of the base matrix $\mathbf{H}_{\mathrm{b}}$ replaced to create the model matrix $\mathbf{H}_{\mathrm{bm}}$. Each 0 in $\mathbf{H}_{\mathrm{b}}$ is replaced by a blank or negative value (e.g., by -1 ) to denote a $z \times z$ all-zero matrix, and each 1 in $\mathbf{H}_{\mathrm{b}}$ is replaced by a circular shift size $p(i, j) \geq 0$. The model matrix $\mathbf{H}_{\mathrm{bm}}$ can then be directly expanded to $\mathbf{H}$.
$\mathbf{H}_{\mathrm{b}}$ is partitioned into two sections, where $\mathbf{H}_{\mathrm{b} 1}$ corresponds to the systematic bits and $\mathbf{H}_{\mathrm{b} 2}$ corresponds to the parity-check bits, such that $H_{b}=\left\lfloor\left(H_{b 1}\right)_{m_{b} \times k_{b}} \mid\left(H_{b 2}\right)_{m_{b} \times m_{b}}\right\rfloor$.
Section $\mathbf{H}_{\mathrm{b} 2}$ is further partitioned into two sections, where vector $\mathbf{h}_{\mathrm{b}}$ has odd weight, and $\mathbf{H}^{\prime}{ }_{\mathrm{b} 2}$ has a dualdiagonal structure with matrix elements at row $i$, column $j$ equal to 1 for $i=j, 1$ for $i=j+1$, and 0 elsewhere:

The base matrix has $h_{b}(0)=1, h_{b}\left(m_{b}-1\right)=1$, and a third value $h_{b}(j), 0<j<\left(m_{b}-1\right)$ equal to 1 . The base matrix structure avoids having multiple weight -1 columns in the expanded matrix.
$$
\begin{aligned}
& H_{b 2}=\left[\begin{array}{ll}
h_{b}^{\prime} H_{b 2}^{\prime} \\
h_{b}^{\prime}
\end{array}\right] \\
& =\left[\begin{array}{cccc}
h_{b}(0) & 1 & & \\
h_{b}(1) & 1 & 1 & 0 \\
\cdot & & 1 & \\
\cdot & & & 1 \\
\cdot & & 0 & 1 \\
h_{b}\left(m_{b}-1\right) & & & 1
\end{array}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

In particular, the non-zero sub-matrices are circularly right shifted by a particular circular shift value. Each 1 in $\mathbf{H}^{\prime}{ }_{\text {b2 }}$ is assigned a shift size of 0 , and is replaced by a $z \mathrm{xz}$ identity matrix when expanding to $\mathbf{H}$. The two located at the top and the bottom of $\mathbf{h}_{b}$ are assigned equal shift sizes, and the third 1 in the middle of $\mathbf{h}_{b}$ is given an unpaired shift size.

A base model matrix is defined for the largest code length $(n=2304)$ of each code rate. The set of shifts $\{p(i, j)\}$ in the base model matrix are used to determine the shift sizes for all other code lengths of the same code rate. Each base model matrix has $n_{b}=24$ columns, and the expansion factor $z_{f}$ is equal to $n / 24$ for code length $n$. Here f is the index of the code lengths for a given code rate, $f=0,1,2, \ldots 18$. For code length $n=2304$ the expansion factor is designated $z_{0}=96$.

For code rates $1 / 2,3 / 4 \mathrm{~A}$ and B code, $2 / 3 \mathrm{~B}$ code, and $5 / 6$ code, the shift sizes $\{p(f, i, j)\}$ for a code size corresponding to expansion factor $z_{f}$ are derived from $\{p(i, j)\}$ by scaling $p(i, j)$ proportionally,
$$
p(f, i, j)=\left\{\begin{array}{c}
p(i, j), p(i, j) \leq 0  \tag{129a}\\
\left\lfloor\frac{p(i, j) z_{f}}{z_{0}}\right\rfloor, p(i, j)>0
\end{array}\right.
$$
where $\lfloor x\rfloor$ denotes the flooring function that gives the nearest integer towards $-\infty$.
For code rate $2 / 3$ A code, the shift sizes $\{p(f, i, j)\}$ for a code size corresponding to expansion factor $z_{f}$ are derived from $\{p(i, j)\}$ by using a modulo function.
$$
p(f, i, j)=\left\{\begin{array}{c}
p(i, j), p(i, j) \leq 0 \\
\bmod \left(p(i, j), z_{f}\right), p(i, j)>0
\end{array}\right.
$$

\section*{Insert new subclause 8.4.9.2.5.2:}

\subsection*{8.4.9.2.5.2 Code rate and block size adjustment}

The LDPC code flexibly supports different block sizes for each code rate through the use of an expansion factor. Each base model matrix has $n_{b}=24$ columns, and the expansion factor ( $z$ factor) is equal to $n / 24$ for code length $n$. In each case, the number of information bits is equal to the code rate times the coded length $n$.
