\section*{Insert new subclause 11.8.3.7.18:}

\subsection*{11.8.3.7.18 OFDMA Multiple Downlink Burst Profile Capability}

This value indicates Downlink/Uplink Burst Profile which shall be used for MS and BS. If this TLV is not included in SBCREQ message, BS shall not include this TLV in SBC-RSP message.

\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|}
\hline Type & Length & Value & Scope \\
\hline 179 & 1 & \begin{tabular}{l}
Bit \#0: Downlink burst profile for multiple FEC types (Table 304a) \\
Bit \#1: Uplink burst profile for multiple FEC types (Table 304b) \\
Bit \#2-7: Reserved (shall be set to 0)
\end{tabular} & SBC-REQ (see 6.3.2.3.23) SBC-RSP (see 6.3.2.3.24) \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\section*{Insert new subclause 11.8.3.7.19:}

\subsection*{11.8.3.7.19 HARQ buffer capability}

Downlink/Uplink HARQ buffering capability indicates the maximal number of data bits the SS is able to store for downlink/uplink HARQ. The buffering capability is separately indicated for $\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{EP}} / \mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{SCH}}$ based incremental redundancy used for CTC, and for DIUC/duration based HARQ methods (Chase combining and CC-IR), and separately for uplink and downlink transmissions. The buffering capability is indicated by two parameters:
1) Number of bits per channel-this is the total number of data bits that the SS may buffer per HARQ channel.
2) Aggregation flag-when this flag is clear, the number of bits is counted separately for each channel. When the flag is set, buffering capability may be shared between channels, as explained below.

The number of bits per channel is indicated as follows:
- For incremental redundancy CTC ( $\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{EP}}$ based): Number of bits is indicated by $\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{EP}}$ code, according to Table 330.
- For Chase combining and CC-IR (DIUC based): Number of bits is indicated by a value $K=0 . .63$ according to the following equation: Number of bits $=$ floor $(512 \times 2 K / 4)$ Bits.

When aggregation flag is clear, the number of bits that were allocated in each HARQ channel in the last transmission must not exceed "Number of bits per channel".

When aggregation flag is set, the sum over all HARQ channels, of the number of bits that were allocated in the HARQ channel in the last transmission, must not exceed the "Number of bits per channel" multiplied by the maximum number channels supported by the SS . Note that sum total of the data bits supported is the same in both cases is the same. The number of channels supported by the SS is indicated in 11.8.3.7.3.

The IR-CTC HARQ buffer capability shall also be applied to bursts for which ACK channel is not allocated (ACK disable is set).

\section*{Insert new subclause 11.8.3.7.19.1:}

\subsection*{11.8.3.7.19.1 HARQ incremental redundancy buffer capability}

\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|}
\hline Type & Length & Value & Scope \\
\hline 162 & 2 & \begin{tabular}{l}
Bits \#0-3: $\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{EP}}$ value indicating downlink HARQ buffering capability for incremental redundancy CTC. \\
Bit \#4: Aggregation Flag for DL \\
Bits \#5-7: Reserved \\
Bits \#8-11: $\mathrm{N}_{\text {EP }}$ value indicating uplink HARQ buffering capability for incremental redundancy CTC. \\
Bit \#12: Aggregation Flag for UL \\
Bits \#13-15: Reserved
\end{tabular} & \begin{tabular}{l}
SBC-REQ \\
SBC-RSP
\end{tabular} \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\section*{Insert new subclause 11.8.3.7.19.2:}

\subsection*{11.8.3.7.19.2 HARQ Chase combining and CC-IR buffer capability}

\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|}
\hline Type & Length & Value & Scope \\
\hline 163 & 2 & \begin{tabular}{l}
Bits \#0-5: Downlink HARQ buffering capability for chase combining (K) \\
Bit \#6: Aggregation Flag for DL \\
Bit \#7: Reserved. \\
Bits \#8-13: Uplink HARQ buffering capability for chase combining (K) \\
Bit \#14: Aggregation Flag for UL \\
Bit \#15: Reserved
\end{tabular} & SBC-REQ SBC-RSP \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\section*{Insert new subclause 11.8.4:}

\subsection*{11.8.4 Security Negotiation Parameters}

This field is a compound attribute indicating security capabilities to negotiate before performing the initial authorization procedure and the reauthorization procedure.

\begin{tabular}{|c|c|l|c|}
\hline Type & Length & \multicolumn{1}{|c|}{ Value } & \multicolumn{1}{c|}{ Scope } \\
\hline 25 & variable & \begin{tabular}{l} 
The compound field contains the sub-attributes \\
as defined in the table below.
\end{tabular} & \begin{tabular}{l} 
SBC-REQ \\
SBC-RSP
\end{tabular} \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\begin{tabular}{|l|l|}
\hline Sub-attribute & Contents \\
\hline PKM Version Support & Version of privacy sublayer supported \\
\hline Authorization Policy Support & Authorization policy to support \\
\hline Message Authentication Code Mode & Message authentication code to support \\
\hline PN Window Size & Size capability of the receiver PN window per SAID \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\subsection*{11.8.4.1 PKM Version Support}

The 'PKM Version Support' field indicates a PKM version. A bit value of 0 indicates "not supported" while 1 indicates "supported". Both an SS and a BS should negotiate only one PKM version.

\begin{tabular}{|c|c|l|}
\hline Type & Length & \multicolumn{1}{|c|}{ Value } \\
\hline 25.1 & 1 & \begin{tabular}{l} 
Bit \#0: PKM version 1 \\
Bit \#1: PKM version 2 \\
Bit \#2-7: Reserved. Set to 0
\end{tabular} \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\subsection*{11.8.4.2 Authorization policy support}

The 'Authorization policy support' field indicates authorization policy used by the MS and BS to negotiate and synchronize. A bit value of 0 indicates "not supported" while 1 indicates "supported".

\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|}
\hline Type & Length & Value \\
\hline 25.2 & 1 & \begin{tabular}{l}
Bit \#0: RSA-based authorization at the initial network entry \\
Bit \#1: EAP-based authorization at the initial network entry \\
Bit \#2: Authenticated EAP-based authorization at the initial network entry \\
Bit \#3: Reserved. Set to 0 \\
Bit \#4: RSA-based authorization at re-entry \\
Bit \#5: EAP-based authorization at re-entry \\
Bit \#6: Authenticated EAP-based authorization at re-entry \\
Bit \#7: Reserved. Set to 0
\end{tabular} \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

Authenticated EAP-based authorization basically means that a message containing EAP payload is protected by CMAC Digest. The CMAC_KEY_U and CMAC_KEY_D are generated with the EIK obtained from RSA-based authorization or EAP-based authorization.

The PKMv2 Auth-Request/Reply/Reject/Acknowledgement messages shall be used in the RSA-based authorization procedure.

The PKMv2 EAP-Transfer message shall be used in the EAP-based authorization procedure. The PKMv2 Authentication EAP-Transfer message shall be used in the Authenticated EAP-based authorization procedure.

Bits \#4-6 are only applied to the SBC-REQ message. Those bits shall be set to 0 in the SBC-RSP message. MS and BS will execute the reauthorization procedure according to the authorization policy negotiated in current BS when AK lifetime is expired and so on. After MS moves into another BS, MS and target BS will execute the reauthorization procedure according to the authorization policy of HO re-entry negotiated in the target BS when the lifetime of AK, which is negotiated between MS and target BS, is expired and so on.

The MS should support at least one authorization policy and inform BS of all supportable authorization policies by the SBC-REQ message. The BS negotiates the authorization policy. If all bits of this attribute included in the SBC-RSP message are 0, then no authorization is applied. Both the BS and the MS shall not use the authorization function.

The following table shows possible authorization policies that the MS can support.
The table shows the bit representation of Bits \#0-2 and Bits \#4-6 in 'Authorization Policy Support' field in an SBC-REQ and a PKMv2 SA-TEK-Request messages.

\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|l|}
\hline \multicolumn{3}{|c|}{Value} & \multirow{2}{*}{Description} & \multirow{2}{*}{Scope} \\
\hline Bit\#0 / Bit \#4 & Bit \#1 / Bit \#5 & Bit\#2 / Bit \#6 & & \\
\hline 0 & 0 & 0 & No Authorization (MS cannot support any authorization) & \multirow[t]{8}{*}{SBC-REQ, PKM-REQ} \\
\hline 0 & 0 & 1 & N/A & \\
\hline 0 & 1 & 0 & Only EAP-based authorization & \\
\hline 0 & 1 & 1 & Only EAP-based authorization or authenticated EAP-based authorization after EAP-based authorization & \\
\hline 1 & 0 & 0 & Only RSA-based authorization & \\
\hline 1 & 0 & 1 & Only RSA-based authorization or authenticated EAP-based authorization after RSA-based authorization & \\
\hline 1 & 1 & 0 & Only RSA-based authorization or Only EAP-based authorization or EAP-based authorization after RSA-based authorization & \\
\hline 1 & 1 & 1 & Only RSA-based authorization or Only EAP-based authorization or EAP-based authorization after RSA-based authorization or authenticated EAP-based authorization after RSA-based authorization or authenticated EAP-based authorization after EAP-based authorization & \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

The following table shows the bit representation of Bit \#0-2 in 'Authorization Policy Support' field in an SBC-RSP and a PKMv2 SA-TEK-Response messages.

\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|l|}
\hline \multicolumn{3}{|c|}{Value} & \multirow{2}{*}{Description} & \multirow{2}{*}{Scope} \\
\hline Bit \#0 & Bit \#1 & Bit \#2 & & \\
\hline 0 & 0 & 0 & No Authorization & \multirow[t]{8}{*}{SBC-RSP, PKM-RSP} \\
\hline 0 & 0 & 1 & N/A & \\
\hline 0 & 1 & 0 & Only EAP-based authorization & \\
\hline 0 & 1 & 1 & Authenticated EAP-based authorization after EAP-based authorization & \\
\hline 1 & 0 & 0 & Only RSA-based authorization & \\
\hline 1 & 0 & 1 & Authenticated EAP-based authorization after RSA-based authorization & \\
\hline 1 & 1 & 0 & EAP-based authorization after RSA-based authorization & \\
\hline 1 & 1 & 1 & N/A & \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

If MS and BS decide "No authorization" as their authorization policy, the MS and BS shall perform neither SA-TEK handshake nor TEK exchange procedure.

\subsection*{11.8.4.3 MAC (Message Authentication Code) Mode}

This field indicates a MAC (Message Authentication Code) mode that MS supports. Both MS and BS shall determine and use a MAC mode. A bit value of 0 indicates "not supported" while 1 indicates "supported". If this attribute is not present, only HMAC is supported.

\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|}
\hline Type & Length & Value \\
\hline 25.3 & 1 & \begin{tabular}{l}
Bit\# 0: HMAC \\
Bit\# 1: CMAC \\
Bit\# 2: 64-bit short-HMAC ${ }^{\text {a }}$ \\
Bit\# 3: 80-bit short-HMAC ${ }^{a}$ \\
Bit\# 4: 96-bit short-HMAC ${ }^{a}$ \\
Bit\# 5-7: Reserved. Set to 0
\end{tabular} \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{${ }^{\text {a }}$ If the short-HMAC mode is selected, then the short-HMAC Tuple shall be applied to the following messages: MOB_SLP-REQ/RSP, MOB_SCN-REQ/RSP, MOB_MSHO-REQ, MOB_BSHO-REQ/RSP, MOB_HO-IND, RNG-REQ/RSP. Otherwise, the HMAC Tuple shall be applied.}
\end{table}

The MS should support at least one MAC mode and inform BS of all supportable MAC modes by the SBCREQ message. The BS negotiates the MAC mode. If all bits of this attribute included in the SBC-RSP message are 0 , then no message authentication code is applied. Both the MS and the BS does not need to authenticate the MAC (Medium Access Control) messages.

Short HMAC can be used only for HMAC is enabled.

\subsection*{11.8.4.4 PN window size}

Specifies the size capability of the receiver PN window for SAs and management connections. The receiver shall track PNs within this window to prevent replay attacks (see 7.5.1.2.4).

\begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|}
\hline Type & Length & Value \\
\hline 25.4 & 2 & PN Window Size in PNs \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\section*{Insert new subclause 11.8.5:}

\subsection*{11.8.5 Power save class types capability}

For MS supporting sleep mode, this parameter defines the capability of the MS supporting different power save class types in sleep mode.

\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|}
\hline Type & Length & Value & Scope \\
\hline 26 & 1 & Bit \#0: power save class type I supported. Bit \#1: power save class type II supported. Bit \#2: power save class type III supported. Bits \#3-4: number of power save class instances supported from class types 1 and 2 Bits \#5-7: number of power save class instances supported from class type III & SBC-REQ, SBC-RSP \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\section*{Insert new subclause 11.8.6:}

\subsection*{11.8.6 Extension capability}

Specifies extension capability supports:

\begin{tabular}{|c|c|l|c|}
\hline Type & Length & \multicolumn{1}{|c|}{ Value } & Scope \\
\hline 27 & 1 & \begin{tabular}{l} 
Bit \#0: Support Extended subheader format \\
Bit \#1-7: Reserved
\end{tabular} & SBC-REQ/RSP \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\section*{Insert new subclause 11.8.7:}

\subsection*{11.8.7 HO Trigger metric support}

This field indicates trigger metrics that MS or BS supports. For each bit, a value of 0 indicates "not supported" while 1 indicates "supported".

\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|}
\hline Type & Length & Value & Scope \\
\hline 28 & 1 & \begin{tabular}{l}
Bit \#0: BS CINR mean \\
Bit \#1: BS RSSI mean \\
Bit \#2: Relative delay \\
Bit \#3: BS RTD \\
Bit \#4-7: Reserved; shall be set to zero
\end{tabular} & SBC-REQ SBC-RSP \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\section*{Insert new subclause 11.8.8:}

\subsection*{11.8.8 Association type support}

The Association type support field indicates the association level supported by the MS or the BS.

\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|}
\hline Type & Length & Value & Scope \\
\hline 167 & 1 & \begin{tabular}{l}
Bit \#0: Scanning without Association: Association not supported \\
Bit \#1: Association level 0: Scanning or association without coordination. \\
Bit \#2: Association level 1: association with coordination. \\
Bit \#3: Association level 2: network assisted association. \\
Bit \#4: Directed association support. \\
Bits \#5-7 Reserved
\end{tabular} & SBC-REQ (see 6.3.2.3.23) SBC-RSP (see 6.3.2.3.24) \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

If a bit is set to ' 1 ', then MS or BS indicates support at the respective association type and level. The MS may associate according to arrangements by the BS at levels up to and including the one for which the MS has indicated support.

\subsection*{11.9 PKM-REQ/RSP management message encodings}

\subsection*{11.9.3 TEK}

\section*{Change 11.9.3 as indicated:}

\begin{tabular}{|c|c|l|}
\hline Type & Length & \multicolumn{1}{|c|}{ Value (string) } \\
\hline 8 & 8 & Encrypted TEK for DES \\
& $\underline{16}$ & Encrypted TEK for AES \\
& $\underline{24}$ & Encrypted TEK for AES Key Wrap \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

When the TEK encryption algorithm identifier in the SA is $0 \times 01$, the length shall be 8 and the TEK shall be encrypted with 3DES in EDE mode according to the procedure defined in 7.5.2.1.

When the TEK encryption algorithm identifier in the SA is 0x03, the length shall be 16 and the TEK shall be encrypted with AES in ECB mode according to the procedure in 7.5.2.3.

When the TEK encryption algorithm identifier in the SA is 0x04, the length shall be 24 and the TEK shall be encrypted with the AES Key Wrap algorithm according to the procedure in 7.5.2.4.

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 371a-TEK encryption algorithm identifiers}
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|}
\hline Value & Description \\
\hline $\underline{0}$ & Reserved \\
\hline $\underline{1}$ & 3-DES EDE with 128-bit key \\
\hline $\underline{2}$ & RSA with 1024-bit key \\
\hline $\underline{3}$ & ECB mode AES with 128-bit key \\
\hline $\underline{4}$ & AES Key Wrap with 128-bit key \\
\hline 5-255 & Reserved \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\section*{Change 11.9.4 as indicated:}

\subsection*{11.9.4 Key lifetime}

Description: This attribute contains the lifetime, in seconds, of an AK or a TEK or a PAK, or a PMK. It is a 32-bit unsigned quantity representing the number of remaining seconds for which the associated key shall be valid. Note that this attribute can be used as a single TLV or as part of a compound TLV top level attribute (AK) as well as a subattribute (TEK).

\begin{tabular}{|c|c|l|}
\hline Type & Length & \multicolumn{1}{|c|}{ Value (uint32) } \\
\hline 9 & 4 & \begin{tabular}{l} 
32-bit quantity representing key lifetime \\
A key lifetime of zero indicates that the corresponding key AK or TEK is not \\
valid.
\end{tabular} \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\section*{Change 11.9.5 as indicated:}

\subsection*{11.9.5 Key-Sequence-Number}

Description: This attribute contains sequence number for a TEK or AK or a PAK, or a PMK. The 2-bit or 4bit quantity, however, is stored in a single byte, with the high-order 6 or 4 bits set to 0 . A summary of the Key-Sequence-Number attribute format is shown below. Note that this attribute can be used as a single TLV or as part of a compound TLV top level attribute (AK) as well as a subattribute (TEK

\begin{tabular}{|c|c|l|}
\hline Type & Length & \multicolumn{1}{|c|}{ Value (uint8) } \\
\hline 10 & 1 & \begin{tabular}{l} 
2-bit sequence number (TEK) \\
4-bit sequence number (AK, PAK, PMK)
\end{tabular} \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\subsection*{11.9.6 HMAC Digest}

\section*{Insert new subclause 11.9.6.1:}

\subsection*{11.9.6.1 Short-HMAC Digest}

Description: This attribute contains the highest-order bytes of the keyed hash used for message authentication. The HMAC algorithm is defined in IETF RFC 2104. The 20 byte HMAC result is truncated to the length indicated by the BS in the Short-HMAC digest length parameter (see 11.1.2.3), or to 10 bytes if the Short-HMAC digest length parameter was not specified.

\begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|}
\hline Type & Length & Value (uint16) \\
\hline 11 & \begin{tabular}{c} 
variable(8, 10, or 12 bytes \\
as described in 11.1.2.3)
\end{tabular} & The highest order bytes of the truncated HMAC-SHA1 keyed hash \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\subsection*{11.9.7 SAID}

\section*{Change text in 11.9.7 as indicated:}

Description: This attribute contains a 16 -bit SAID used by the Privacy Protocol to identify the SA. The SAID for the multicast service or the broadcast service is the GSAID. Null SAID shall used when "No authorization" is applied. The value of Null SAID is 0xffff.

\subsection*{11.9.8 TEK parameters}

\section*{Change 11.9.8 as indicated:}

Description: This attribute is a compound attribute, consisting of a collection of subattributes. These subattributes represent all security parameters relevant to a particular generation of an SAID's TEK. A summary of the TEK-Parameters attribute format is shown below. The GTEK and GKEK are defined only
for the multicast service or the broadcast service. The GTEK is the TEK for the multicast service or the broadcast service.

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 372-TEK-parameters subattributes}
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|}
\hline Attribute & Contents \\
\hline TEK & TEK, encrypted with the KEK GTEK, encrypted with the GKEK \\
\hline Key-Lifetime & TEK Remaining Lifetime \\
\hline Key-Sequence-Number & TEK Sequence Number \\
\hline CBC-IV & CBC Initialization Vector \\
\hline Associated GKEK Sequence Number & Associated GKEK sequence number with this TEK-Parameters \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\section*{Insert the following text at the end of the 11.9.8:}

The CBC-IV attribute is required when the data encryption algorithm identifier in the SA ciphersuite is $0 \times 01$ (DES in CBC mode).

The CBC-IV attribute is not required when the data encryption algorithm identifier in the SA ciphersuite is 0x02 (AES).

The CBC-IV attribute is required when the data encryption algorithm identifier in the SA ciphersuite is 0x03 (AES in CBC mode).

\hline 19 & variable & \begin{tabular}{l} 
The Compound field contains the subattributes as \\
defined in Table 374
\end{tabular} \\

\end{tabular}

\section*{Delete all of 11.9.16.}

\subsection*{41.9.16 Version}

\section*{Change 11.9.17 as indicated:}

\subsection*{11.9.17 SA-Descriptor}

Description: The SA-Descriptor attribute is a compound attribute whose subattributes describe the properties of a Security Association (SA). These properties include the SAID, the SA type, the SA service type, and the cryptographic suite employed within the SA.

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Table 383-SA-Descriptor subattributes}
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|}
\hline Attribute & Contents \\
\hline SAID & Security Association ID \\
\hline SA-Type & Type of SA \\
\hline SA service type & Service type of the corresponding SA type. This shall be defined only when SA type is Static SA or Dynamic SA. \\
\hline Cryptographic-Suite & Cryptographic suite employed within the SA \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\section*{Change the table in 11.9.19 as indicated:}

\begin{tabular}{|c|c|l|c|}

\hline 27 & variable & Compound & Auth Reply, PMKv2-rsa reply, sa-tek-response \\

\section*{Insert new subclause 11.9.20:}

\subsection*{11.9.20 Nonce}

Description: A quantity used to protect message exchanges from Replay Attack. As always, values for nonces should be generated using reliable random or pseudo-random generators.

\begin{tabular}{|c|c|ll|}
\hline Type & Length & & Value (string) \\
\hline 29 & 4 & Randomly generated value & \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\section*{Insert new subclause 11.9.21:}

\subsection*{11.9.21 SS_RANDOM}

Description: This attribute contains a quantity that is pseudo random number generated from the MS and used as fresh number for mutual authorization message handshake.

\begin{tabular}{|c|c|ll|}
\hline Type & Length & & Value \\
\hline 33 & 8 & MS generated random number & \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\section*{Insert new subclause 11.9.22:}

\subsection*{11.9.22 BS_RANDOM}

Description: This attribute contains a quantity that is pseudo random number generated from the BS and used as fresh number for mutual authorization message handshake.

\begin{tabular}{|c|c|ll|}
\hline Type & Length & & Value \\
\hline 34 & 8 & BS generated random number & \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\section*{Insert new subclause 11.9.23:}

\subsection*{11.9.23 Pre-PAK}

Description: This PAK (Primary Authorization Key) is 16 byte quantity, from which annn AK, KEK, two MAC message authentication keys, and two EAP message protection keys are derived. This attribute contains a 128 byte quantity containing the PAK RSA-encrypted with the MS's 1024-bit RSA public key. Details of the RSA encryption procedure are given 7.x The ciphertext produced by the RSA algorithm shall be the length of the RSA modulus, i.e., 128 bytes.

\begin{tabular}{|c|c|l|}
\hline Type & Length & \multicolumn{1}{|c|}{ Value } \\
\hline 35 & 128 & \begin{tabular}{l} 
128 byte quantity representing an RSA-encrypted pre-PAK, which generates \\
PAK
\end{tabular} \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\section*{Insert new subclause 11.9.24:}

\subsection*{11.9.24 BS-Certificate}

Description: This attribute is a string attribute containing an X. 509 BS Certificate, as defined 7.x. A summary of the BS-Certificate attribute format is shown below. The fields are transmitted from left to right.

\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|}
\hline Type & Length & Value \\
\hline 37 & \begin{tabular}{l}
variable \\
Length shall not cause resulting MAC management message to exceed the maximum allowed size.
\end{tabular} & X. 509 BS Certificate (DER-encoded ASN.1) \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\section*{Insert new subclause 11.9.25:}

\subsection*{11.9.25 SigBS}

Description: This attribute contains a RSA signature computed over the PKMv2 RSA Reply message or the PKMv2 RSA Reject message with the BS's private key.

\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|}
\hline Type & Length & Value \\
\hline 38 & 128 & An RSA signature computed over all attributes included in the PKMv2 RSA Reply message or the PKMv2 RSA Reject message with the BS's private key. This value is calculated using PKCS \#1 OAEP 1.5 signing algorithm with SHA-1 hash. \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\section*{Insert new subclause 11.9.26:}

\subsection*{11.9.26 MS-MAC address}

Description: This attribute is the MAC address of MS.

\begin{tabular}{|c|c|ll|}
\hline Type & Length & & Value \\
\hline 39 & 6 & The MAC address of the MS & \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\section*{Insert new subclause 11.9.27:}

\subsection*{11.9.27 CMAC Digest}

Description: This attribute contains a packet number counter CMAC_PN_* incremented per packet on each direction and the Message Authentication Code value used for message authentication. The CMAC algorithm is defined in draft SP 800-38B.

The CMAC digest includes

\begin{tabular}{|c|c|lc|}
\hline Type & Length & Value \\
\hline 40 & 12 & See that follows & \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|}
\hline Field & Length (bits) & Note \\
\hline CMAC Packet Number counter, CMAC_PN_* & 32 & This context is different in UL, DL \\
\hline CMAC value & 64 & CMAC with AES 128 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\section*{Insert new subclause 11.9.28:}

\subsection*{11.9.28 Key Push Modes}

Description: The field, key push modes, is used to distinguish usage code of a PKMv2 Group Key Update Command message.

\begin{tabular}{|c|c|ll|}
\hline Type & Length & & Value \\
\hline 41 & 1 & 0, GKEK update mode & \\
& & 1, GTEK update mode & \\
2-255, Reserved & \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

A PKMv2 Group Key Update Command message for the GKEK update mode is to distribute new GKEK to each SS carried on the Primary Management connection. The BS transmits this message before the M\&B TEK Grace Time starts.

A PKMv2 Group Key Update Command message for the GTEK update mode is to distribute new GTEK to all SS carried on the Broadcast connection. The BS transmits this message after the M\&B TEK Grace Time starts.

Attributes of a PKMv2 Group Key Update Command message are different according to the value of the Key Push Modes as shown in the following table.

\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|}
\hline Attribute & GKEK update mode & GTEK update mode \\
\hline Key-Sequence-Number & AK Sequence Number & GKEK Sequence Number \\
\hline GSAID & Yes & Yes \\
\hline Key Push Modes & Yes & Yes \\
\hline Key Push Counter & Yes & Yes \\
\hline GTEK-Parameters & No & Yes \\
\hline GKEK-Parameters & Yes & No \\
\hline HMAC/CMAC-Digest & Yes & Yes \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

Key-Sequence-Number, GSAID, Key Push Modes, and HMAC/CMAC-Digest fields are included in two PKMv2 Group Key Update Command message regardless of the value of the Key Push Modes.

GKEK-Parameters attribute should be included in a PKMv2 Group Key Update Command message for the GKEK update mode. And GTEK-Parameters attribute should be included in that message for the GTEK update mode.

CBC-IV can be included only when the TEK encryption algorithm identifier in the cryptographic suite equal to 0 x 01 .

One of HMAC Digest or CMAC-Digest shall be used to authenticate the Key Update Command messages.

\section*{Insert new subclause 11.9.29:}

\subsection*{11.9.29 Key Push Counter}

Description: Key Push Counter is used to protect for replay attack. This value is one greater than (modulo 65536) that of older generation.

\begin{tabular}{|c|c|cc|}
\hline Type & Length & Value \\
\hline 42 & 2 & 16-bit counter & \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\section*{Insert new subclause 11.9.30:}

\subsection*{11.9.30 GKEK (Group Key Encryption Key)}

Description: 128-bit GKEK may be randomly generated in a BS or an ASA server. The 'GKEK (Group Key Encryption Key)' field is used to encrypt the GTEK for the multicast service or the broadcast service.

\begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|}
\hline Type & Length & Value \\
\hline 43 & 16 & GKEK, encrypted with the KEK derived from AK \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\section*{Insert subclause 11.9.31:}

\subsection*{11.9.31 SigSS}

Description: This attribute contains an RSA signature computed over the PKMv2 RSA Request message or the PKMv2 RSA Acknowledgement message with the SS's private key.

\begin{tabular}{|c|c|l|}
\hline Type & Length & \multicolumn{1}{|c|}{ Value } \\
\hline 44 & 128 & \begin{tabular}{l} 
An RSA signature computed over all attributes included in the PKMv2 RSA \\
Request message or the PKMv2 RSA Acknowledgement message with the \\
SS's private key. This value is calculated using RSASSA-PKCS-v1_5-Sign \\
algorithm with SHA_1 hash.
\end{tabular} \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\section*{Insert subclause 11.9.32:}

\subsection*{11.9.32 AKID}

Description: This attribute identifies the AK as defined in Table 133.

\begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|}
\hline Type & Length & \multicolumn{1}{c|}{ Value } \\
\hline 45 & 8 & AKID as defined in Table 133 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\section*{Insert subclause 11.9.33:}

\subsection*{11.9.33 EAP payload}

Description: This attribute contains the payload used in the upper EAP authorization layer. The security sublayer does not interpret this attribute.

\begin{tabular}{|c|c|ll|}
\hline Type & Length & Value \\
\hline 28 & variable & EAP payload & \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\section*{Insert subclause 11.9.34:}

\subsection*{11.9.34 Auth result code}

Description: This attribute contains the result code of the RSA-based authorization (only for PKMv 2 ).

\begin{tabular}{|c|c|lc|}
\hline Type & Length & & Value \\
\hline 30 & 1 & \begin{tabular}{l} 
0, Success \\
1, Reject \\
2-255, Reserved.
\end{tabular} & \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\section*{Insert subclause 11.9.35:}

\subsection*{11.9.35 SA service type}

Description: This attribute indicates service types of the corresponding SA type. This attribute shall be defined only when the SA type is Static SA or Dynamic SA. The GTEK shall be used to encrypt connection for group multicast service. The MTK shall be used to encrypt connection for MBS service.

\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|}
\hline Type & Length & Value \\
\hline 31 & 1 & \begin{tabular}{l}
0: Unicast service \\
1: Group multicast service \\
2: MBS service \\
3-255: Reserved.
\end{tabular} \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\section*{Insert subclause 11.9.36:}

\subsection*{11.9.36 PKMv2 configuration settings}

Description: This field defines the parameters associated with PKMv2 operation. It is composed of a number of encapsulated TLV fields.

\begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|c|}
\hline Type & Length & \begin{tabular}{c} 
Value \\
(compound)
\end{tabular} & Scope \\
\hline 27 & Variable & & Auth Reply,PKMv2 SA-TEK-response \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\section*{Insert new subclause 11.9.37:}

\subsection*{11.9.37 Frame number}

Description: This attribute contains a 24-bit absolute frame number in which the old PMK and all its associate AKs should be discarded. The value is in most significant bit first order.

\begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|}
\hline Type & Length & Value \\
\hline 32 & 3 & 24-bit Frame Number in MSB first order \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

Change third row of the second table in 11.11 as indicated:

\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|}
\hline Name & Type & Length & Value \\
\hline Channel Type request & $1.4 \underline{3}$ & 1 & \begin{tabular}{l}
0b00 = Normal subchannel, \\
0b01 = Band AMC Channel, \\
0b10 = Safety Channel, \\
0b11 = ReservedSounding
\end{tabular} \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\begin{table}
\captionsetup{labelformat=empty}
\caption{Insert the following entries into the second table of 11.11:}
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|}
\hline Name & Type & Length & Value \\
\hline Zone-specific physical CINR request & 1.4 & 3 & \begin{tabular}{l}
Bits \#0-2: Type of zone on which CINR is to be reported \\
0b000: PUSC zone with 'use all $\mathrm{SC}=0$ ' \\
0b001: PUSC zone with 'use all $\mathrm{SC}=1$ ' / PUSC AAS zone \\
0b010: FUSC zone \\
0b011: Optional FUSC zone \\
0b100: Safety Channel region \\
0b101: AMC zone (only applicable to AAS mode) \\
0b110-0b111: Reserved \\
Bit \#3: 1 if zone for which CINR should be estimated is STC zone, 0 otherwise. \\
Bit \#4: 1 if zone for which CINR should be estimated is AAS zone, 0 otherwise. \\
Bits \#5-6: PRBS_ID of the zone for which CINR should be estimated. Ignored for Safety Channel. \\
Bit \#7: Data/pilot-based CINR measurement: \\
0 - Report the CINR estimate from pilot subcarriers, \\
1 - Report the CINR estimate from data subcarriers \\
Bits \#8-13: Reported CINR shall only be estimated for the subchannels of PUSC major groups for which the corresponding bit is set. Bit \#( $k+7$ ) refers to major group $k$. Only applicable for CINR measurement on a PUSC zone \\
Bits \#14-17: $\alpha_{\text {avg }}$ in multiples of $1 / 16$ (range is $[1 / 16,16 / 16]$ ) \\
Bit \#18: 0 - report only mean of CINR \\
1 - report both mean and standard deviation of CINR \\
Bits \#19-23: Reserved, shall be set to zero
\end{tabular} \\
\hline Preamble physical CINR request & 1.5 & 1 & \begin{tabular}{l}
Bits \#0-1: Type of preamble physical CINR measurement \\
0b00 - Report the estimation of CINR measured from preamble for frequency reuse configuration $=1$ \\
0b01 - Report the estimation of CINR measured from preamble for frequency reuse configuration $=3$ \\
0b10 - Report the estimation of CINR measured from preamble for band AMC \\
0b11-Reserved \\
Bits \#2-5: $\alpha_{\text {avg }}$ in multiples of $1 / 16$ (range is $[1 / 16,16 / 16]$ ) \\
Bit \#6: 0 - report only mean of CINR \\
1 - report both mean and standard deviation of CINR \\
Bit \#7: Reserved, shall be set to zero
\end{tabular} \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|}
\hline Name & Type & Length & Value \\
\hline Zone-specific effective CINR request & 1.6 & 2 & \begin{tabular}{l}
Bits \#0-2: Type of zone on which effective CINR is to be reported \\
0b000: PUSC zone with 'use all $\mathrm{SC}=0$ ' \\
0b001: PUSC zone with 'use all $\mathrm{SC}=1$ ' / PUSC AAS zone \\
0b010: FUSC zone \\
0b011: Optional FUSC zone \\
0b100: Reserved \\
0b101: AMC zone (only applicable to AAS mode) \\
0b110-0b111: Reserved \\
Bit \#3: 1 if zone for which effective CINR should be reported is STC zone, 0 otherwise. \\
Bit \#4: 1 if zone for which effective CINR should be estimated is AAS zone, 0 otherwise. \\
Bits \#5-6: PRBS_ID of the zone for which effective CINR should be reported. Ignored for Safety Channel. \\
Bit \#7: Data/pilot-based effective CINR measurement: \\
0 - Report the CINR estimate from pilot subcarriers, \\
1 - Report the CINR estimate from data subcarriers \\
Bits \#8-13: Reported effective CINR shall only be estimated for the subchannels of PUSC major groups for which the corresponding bit is set. Bit \#( $k+7$ ) refers to major group $k$. Only applicable for CINR measurement on a PUSC zone \\
Bit \#14-15:Reserved, shall be set to zero
\end{tabular} \\
\hline Preamble effective CINR request & 1.7 & 1 & \begin{tabular}{l}
Bits \#0-1: Type of preamble-based effective CINR measurement \\
0b00 - Report the estimation of effective CINR measured from preamble for frequency reuse configuration $=1$ \\
0b01 - Report the estimation of effective CINR measured from preamble for frequency reuse configuration $=3$ \\
0b10-11 - Reserved \\
Bit \#2-7: Reserved, shall be set to zero
\end{tabular} \\
\hline Channel selectivity report & 1.8 & 1 & \begin{tabular}{l}
Bit \#0: 1 - include frequency selectivity report \\
Bit \#1-7: Reserved, shall be set to zero
\end{tabular} \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\section*{Insert the following text at the end of 11.12:}

For the type 2.4, 2.5, 2.6, 2.x, the following 5 bit, CINR measurement encoding shall be used:
$$
n=\left\{\begin{array}{cc}
0 & C I N R \leq-3 d B  \tag{154a}\\
n & (n-4) d B<C I N R \leq(n-3) d B, 0<n<31 \\
31 & C I N R>27 d B
\end{array}\right.
$$

For the TLVs with type 3.x and 4.x, the following 5 bit physical CINR measurement encoding shall be used:
$$
\text { Payload bits }= \begin{cases}0 & C I N R \leq-3 d B  \tag{154b}\\ n & (n-4)<C I N R \leq(n-3), 0<n<31 \\ 31 & C I N R>27 d B\end{cases}
$$

\hline $\underline{33}$ & MBS zone identifier assignment \\
\hline 34 & Traffic Indication Preference \\
\hline $\underline{35}$ & Global Service Class Name \\
\hline $\underline{36}$ & Reserved \\
\hline $\underline{37}$ & SN Feedback Enabled \\
\hline $\underline{38}$ & FSN size \\
\hline $\underline{39}$ & CID allocation for Active BSs \\
\hline $\underline{40}$ & Unsolicited Grant Interval \\
\hline $\underline{41}$ & Unsolicited Polling Interval \\
\hline $\underline{42}$ & PDU SN extended subheader for HARQ reordering \\
\hline $\underline{43}$ & MBS contents ID \\
\hline $\underline{44}$ & HARQ Service Flows \\
\hline $\underline{45}$ & Authorization Token \\
\hline $\underline{46}$ & HARQ Channel mapping \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\section*{Change the fourth paragraph as indicated:}

The CC indicates the status for the dynamic service (DSx-xxx) messages. The value may appears in the Confirmation Code field of a DSx message or as the value of a TLV encoded error parameter.

\section*{Delete the last paragraph:}

In the case CC = "reject-not-supported-parameter" or CC = "reject-not-supported-parameter-value", the eorresponding TLV(s) may be returned to caller in DSx-RSP message. In the case of CC - "reject-notsupported parameter value," the value field of the returned TLV should contain the closest value that is supported.

\subsection*{11.13.4 QoS parameter set type}

\section*{Change the first paragraph as indicated:}

This parameter shall appear within every service flow encoding. It specifies the proper application of the QoS Parameter Set: to the Provisioned set, the Admitted set, and/or the Active set. The QoS Parameter Set is a subset of the following parameter set:
$=$ Traffic priority (11.13.5)
= Maximum sustained traffic rate (11.13.6)
= Maximum traffic burst (11.13.7)
$=$ Minimum Reserved traffic rate (11.13.8)
= Vendor specific QoS parameters (11.13.10)
$=$ Tolerated jitter (11.13.13)
$=$ Maximum latency (11.13.14)
= Unsolicited grant interval (11.13.20)
= Unsolicited polling interval (11.13.21)
When two QoS Parameter Sets are the same, a multibit value of this parameter may be used to apply the QoS parameters to more than one set. A single message may contain multiple QoS parameter sets in separate type 145/146 service flow encodings for the same service flow. This allows specification of the QoS Parameter Sets when their parameters are different. Non-QoS parameters shall appear only in the first Service Flow Management Encodings. Bit 0 is the LSB of the Value field.

\subsection*{11.13.5 Traffic priority}

\section*{Change the second paragraph as indicated:}

For uplink service flows, the BS shall use this parameter when determining precedence in request service and grant generation, and the SS shall preferentially select contention Request opportunities for Priority Request CIDs based on this priority and its Request/Transmission Policy (see 11.13.12).

\section*{Change the Table in the subclause as indicated:}

\begin{tabular}{|c|c|l|c|}
\hline Type & Length & \multicolumn{1}{|c|}{ Value } & Scope \\
\hline$[145 / 146] .6$ & 1 & \begin{tabular}{l}
0 to 7-Higher numbers indicate higher priority \\
Default 0
\end{tabular} & DSx-REQ \\
& & & DSx-RSP \\
& & & DSx-ACK \\
REG-RSP \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\subsection*{11.13.6 Maximum sustained traffic rate}

\section*{Change the first paragraph as indicated}

This parameter defines the peak information rate of the service. The rate is expressed in bits per second and pertains to the SDUs at the input to the Convergence Sublayer-system. Hence Explicitly, this parameter does not include 802.16 MAC overhead such as MAC headers or CRCs. This parameter does not limit the instantaneous rate of the service since this is governed by the physical attributes of the ingress port. However, At the BS and SS in the uplink direction, the service shall be policed to conform to this parameter, on the average, over time. At the BS in the downlink direction, it may be assumed that the service was already policed at the ingress to the network and the BS is not required to do additional policing. If this parameter is omitted or set to zero, then there is no explicitly mandated maximum rate. This field specifies only a bound, not a guarantee that the rate is available.

The algorithm for measuring whether a flow exceeds its Maximum Sustained Traffic Rate policing this parameter is left to vendor differentiation and is outside the scope of the standard.

SDUs deemed to exceed the Maximum Sustained Traffic Rate may be, for instance, delayed or dropped according to the discretion of the vendor.

\section*{Change the table as indicated:}

\begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|c|}
\hline Type & Length & Value & Scope \\
\hline$[145 / 146] .7$ & 4 & Rate (in bits per second) & DSx-REQ \\
& & & DSx-RSP \\
& & & DSx-ACK \\
& & REG-RSP \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\subsection*{11.13.8 Minimum reserved traffic rate}

\section*{Change the content of the subclause as indicated}

This parameter specifies the minimum rate reserved for this service flow. The rate is expressed in bits per second and specifies the minimum amount of data to be transported on behalf of the service flow when averaged over time. The specified rate shall only be honored when sufficient data is available for scheduling. When insufficient data exists, the requirement imposed by this parameter shall be satisfied by assuring that the available data is transmitted as soon as possible.

The BS and SS shall be able to transport traffic satisfy bandwidth requests for a service flow up to its Minimum Reserved Traffic Rate. If less bandwidth than the its Minimum Reserved Traffic Rate is available requested for a service flow, the BS and SS may reallocate the excess reserved bandwidth for other purposes. The data for this parameter is measured at the input of the Convergence Sublayer. The aggregate Minimum Reserved Traffic Rate of all service flows may exceed the amount of available bandwidth. The value of this parameter is calculated from the byte following the MAC header HCS to the end of the MAC PDU payload. If this parameter is omitted, then it defaults to a value of 0 bits per second (i.e., no bandwidth is reserved for the flow).

\section*{Change the table as indicated:}

\begin{tabular}{|c|c|l|c|}
\hline Type & Length & Value & Scope \\
\hline$[145 / 146] .9$ & 4 & Rate (in bits per second) & DSx-REQ \\
& & & DSx-RSP \\
& & & DSx-ACK \\
& & REG-RSP \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\subsection*{11.13.11 Service-flow Uplink grant scheduling type}

\section*{Change the title and the content of the subclause as indicated:}

The value of this parameter specifies the Uplink grant scheduling service type that shall be enabled for the associated uplink service flow (see 6.3.5.2). If the parameter is omitted, BE service is assumed.

\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|}
\hline Type & Length & Value & Scope \\
\hline [145446]. 11 & 1 & \begin{tabular}{l}
0: Reserved \\
1: for Undefined (BS implementation-dependent ${ }^{\text {a }}$ ) \\
2: for BE (default) \\
3: for nrtPS \\
4: for rtPS \\
5: Reserved for Extended rtPS \\
6: for UGS \\
7-255: Reserved
\end{tabular} & \begin{tabular}{l}
DSA-REQ DSA-RSP \\
DSA-ACK
\end{tabular} \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
${ }^{\mathrm{a}}$ The specific implementation-dependent scheduling service type could be defined in a message of Type 145/146.143 (vendor-specific QoS parameters).

\subsection*{11.13.12 Request/transmission policy}

\section*{Change the content of the subclause as indicated}

The value of this parameter provides the capability to specify certain attributes for the associated service flow. These attributes include options for PDU formation and, for uplink service flows, restrictions on the types of bandwidth request options that may be used. An attribute is enabled by setting the corresponding bit position to 1 . For attributes affecting uplink bandwidth request types, a value of zero indicates the default actions described in the scheduling service description in 6.3.5 shall be used. A value of one indicates that the action associated with the attribute bit overrides the default action.

\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|}
\hline Type & Length & Value & Scope \\
\hline [145/146]. 12 & $4 \underline{1}$ & \begin{tabular}{l}
Bit \#0 - Service flow shall not use broadcast bandwidth request opportunities. (Uplink only) \\
Bit \#1 - Reserved; shall be set to zero Service flow shall not use multicast bandwidth request opportunities. (Uplink only) \\
Bit \#2 - The service flow shall not piggyback requests with data. (Uplink only) \\
Bit \#3 - The service flow shall not fragment data. \\
Bit \#4 - The service flow shall not suppress payload headers (CS parameter) \\
Bit \#5 - The service flow shall not pack multiple SDUs (or fragments) into single MAC PDUs \\
Bit \#6 - The service flow shall not include CRC in the MAC PDU. \\
Bit \#7 - Reserved; shall be set to zero
\end{tabular} & DSA-REQ DSA-RSP DSA-ACK \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\subsection*{11.13.14 Maximum latency}

\section*{Change the first paragraph as indicated:}

The value of this parameter specifies the maximum latency between the ingress of a packet to the Convergence Sublayer reception of a packet by the BS or SS on its network interface and the forwarding of the SDU packet to its RFAir Interface.

\section*{Change the Table in the subclause as indicated:}

\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|}
\hline Type & Length & Value & Scope \\
\hline [145/146]. 14 & 4 & ms & DSx-REQ DSx-RSP DSx-ACK REG-RSP \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\subsection*{11.13.17 Target SAID}

\section*{Change the first paragraph as indicated:}

The target SAID parameter indicates the SAID onto which the service flow that is being set up shall be mapped.

\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|}
\hline Type & Length & Value & Scope \\
\hline [145/146]. 17 & 2 & SAID onto which SF is mapped & DSA-REQ DSA-RSP DSC-REQ DSC-RSP \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\subsection*{11.13.18.8 ARQ_BLOCK_SIZE}

\hline [145/146]. 26 1.26 & 2 & \begin{tabular}{l}
0-15: Reserved 4-204016, 32, 64, 128, 256, 512, or 1024 or 2048: Desired/Agreed size in bytes \\
2041-65535: Reserved
\end{tabular} & DSA-REQ, DSA-RSP REG-REQ, REG-RSP \\

\section*{Insert new subclause 11.8.18.9:}

\subsection*{11.13.18.9 RECEIVER_ARQ_ACK_PROCESSING_TIME}

The BS or SS may provide this parameter. The DSA-REQ and DSA-RSP messages may contain the value of this parameter. This optional parameter indicates the number of ms required by the ARQ receiver to process the received ARQ blocks and provide a valid ACK or NAK. This does not mean that the receiver would actually transmit an ACK or NAK after this time, but rather it can be optionally used by the transmitter that receives an ACK bit-map to determine which bits are re-transmissions of historical NAKs or ACKs, that are not based on newly received ARQ blocks.

\begin{tabular}{|r|c|l|c|}
\hline \multicolumn{1}{|c|}{ Type } & Length & Value & Scope \\
\hline$[145 / 146] .27$ & 1 & $0-255$ & \\
1.27 & & & DSA-REQ, DSA-RSP \\
& & & REG-REQ, REG-RSP \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\subsection*{11.13.19.3 Packet CS encodings for configuration and MAC messaging}

\section*{Delete 11.13.19.3.3 and all its subclauses.}

\subsection*{41.13.19.3.3-Glassifier error parameter set}

\subsection*{11.13.19.3.4 Packet classification rule}

\subsection*{11.13.19.3.4.3 Protocol}

\section*{Change content of 11.13.19.3.4.3 as indicated:}

The value of the field specifies a list of matching values for the IP Protocol field. For IPv6 (IETF RFC 2460), this refers to next header entry in the last header of the IP header chain. The encoding of the value field is that defined by the IANA document "Protocol Numbers." If this parameter is omitted, then comparison of the IP header Protocol field for this entry is irrelevant.

\begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|}
\hline Type & Length & Value \\
\hline$[145 / 146]$. cst.3.3 & $n \underline{l}$ & prot1, prot2,...prot $n$-protocol \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\subsection*{11.13.19.3.4.4 IP masked source address}

\section*{Change content of 11.13.19.3.4.4 as indicated:}

This parameter specifies an list of IP source addresses (designated "srci") and their its corresponding address masks (designated "smaski"). An IP packet with IP source address "ip-src" matches this parameter if srci = (ip-src AND smaski) for any i from 1 to n. If this parameter is omitted, then comparison of the IP packet source address for this entry is irrelevant.

\begin{tabular}{|c|l|l|}
\hline Type & \multicolumn{1}{|c|}{ Length } & \multicolumn{1}{|c|}{ Value } \\
\hline$[145 / 146]$. cst.3.4 & \begin{tabular}{l}
$n^{*} 8$ (IPv4) or \\
$n^{*} 32$ (IPv6)
\end{tabular} & \begin{tabular}{l} 
sre $_{t}$, smask $_{t}, \ldots$, sre $_{t}$, smask $_{t}, \ldots$, sre $_{H}$, smask $_{H-}$ \\
src, smask
\end{tabular} \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\section*{Change the title of 11.13.19.3.4.5 as indicated:}

\subsection*{11.13.19.3.4.5 IP masked destination address}

\section*{Change content of 11.13.19.3.4.5 as indicated:}

This parameter specifies an tist of IP destination addresses (designated "dsti") and their its corresponding address masks (designated "dmaski"). An IP packet with IP destination address "ip-dst" matches this parameter if dsti $=(\mathrm{ip}-\mathrm{dst}$ AND dmaski) for any i from 1 to n . If this parameter is omitted, then comparison of the IP packet destination address for this entry is irrelevant.

\begin{tabular}{|c|l|c|}
\hline Type & \multicolumn{1}{|c|}{ Length } & \multicolumn{1}{|c|}{ Value } \\
\hline$[145 / 146]$. cst.3.5 & \begin{tabular}{l}
$n^{*} 8$ (IPv4) or \\
$n^{*} 32$ (IPv6)
\end{tabular} & \begin{tabular}{l} 
dst $_{t}$, dmask $_{t}, \ldots$, dst $_{t}$, dmask $_{t}, \ldots$, dst $_{t}$, dmask $_{t}$ \\
dst, dmask
\end{tabular} \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\subsection*{11.13.19.3.4.6 Protocol source port range}

\section*{Change content of 11.13.19.3.4.6 as indicated:}

The value of the field specifies a list of noneverlapping ranges of protocol source port values. Classifier rules with port numbers are protocol specific; i.e., a rule on port numbers without a protocol specification shall not be defined. An IP packet with protocol port value "src-port" matches this parameter if src-port is greater than or equal to sportlow and src-port is less than or equal to sporthigh. If this parameter is omitted, the protocol source port is irrelevant. This parameter is irrelevant for protocols without port numbers.

\begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|}
\hline Type & Length & \multicolumn{1}{|c|}{ Value } \\
\hline$[145 / 146]$. cst.3.6 & $n^{* 4}$ & \begin{tabular}{l} 
sportlow1, sporthigh2,..., sportlown, \\
sporthighn, sportlow, sporthigh
\end{tabular} \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\subsection*{11.13.19.3.4.7 Protocol destination port range}

\section*{Change content of 11.13.19.3.4.7 as indicated:}

The value of the field specifies a list of nonerlapping ranges of protocol destination port values. Classifier rules with port numbers are protocol specific; i.e., a rule on port numbers without a protocol specification shall not be defined. An IP packet with protocol port value "dst-port" matches this parameter if dst-port is greater than or equal to dportlow and dst-port is less than or equal to dporthigh. If this parameter is omitted, the protocol destination port is irrelevant. This parameter is irrelevant for protocols without port numbers.

\begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|}
\hline Type & Length & \multicolumn{1}{|c|}{ Value } \\
\hline$[145 / 146]$. cst.3.7 & $n^{*} 4$ & \begin{tabular}{l} 
dportlow1, dporthigh2,..., dportlown, \\
dporthighn, dportlow, dporthigh
\end{tabular} \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\subsection*{11.13.19.3.4.8 IEEE 802.3/Ethernet destination MAC address}

\section*{Change content of 11.13.19.3.4.8 as indicated:}

This parameter specifies a list of MAC destination addresses (designated "dsti") and their its corresponding address masks (designated "mski"). An IEEE 802.3/Ethernet packet with MAC destination address
"etherdst" corresponds to this parameter if dsti = (etherdst AND mski)for any i from 1 to n. If this parameter is omitted, then comparison of the IEEE 802.3/Ethernet destination MAC address for this entry is irrelevant.

\begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|}
\hline Type & Length & Value \\
\hline$[145 / 146]$. cst.3.8 & $n^{*} 12$ & \begin{tabular}{l}
$\mathrm{dst}_{4}, \mathrm{msk}_{4}, \ldots, \mathrm{dst}_{t}, \mathrm{msk}_{t}, \ldots, \mathrm{dst}_{H}, \mathrm{msk}_{h} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{dst}_{t}}$ \\
$\underline{\mathrm{msk}}$
\end{tabular} \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\subsection*{11.13.19.3.4.9 IEEE 802.3/Ethernet source MAC address}

\section*{Change content of 11.13.19.3.4.9 as indicated:}

This parameter specifies a list of MAC source addresses (designated "srci") and their its corresponding address masks (designated "mski’"). An IEEE 802.3/Ethernet packet with MAC source address "ethersrc" corresponds to this parameter if srci $=($ ethersrc AND mski $)$ for any i from 1 to n . If this parameter is omitted, then comparison of the IEEE 802.3/Ethernet source MAC address for this entry is irrelevant.

\begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|}
\hline Type & Length & Value \\
\hline$[145 / 146]$. cst.3.9 & $n^{*} 12$ & \begin{tabular}{l}
$\mathrm{sre}_{4}, \mathrm{msk}_{4}, \ldots, \mathrm{sre}_{t}, \mathrm{msk}_{t}, \ldots, \mathrm{sre}_{H}, \mathrm{msk}_{H} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{src},}$ \\
$\underline{\mathrm{msk}}$
\end{tabular} \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\section*{Insert new subclause 11.13.19.3.4.16:}

\subsection*{11.13.19.3.4.16 Large Context ID for ROHC- or ECRTP-compressed packet or ROHC feedback packet}

The values of the field specify the context ID for ROHC- or ECRTP-compressed packets. The CS will attempt to match the context ID with the payload packet's one-byte or two-byte embedded Context ID field according to the scheme described in RFC 3095 section 5.1.3.

\begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|}
\hline Type & Length & Value \\
\hline$[145 / 146]$. cst.3.16 & 2 & $0-65535:$ Context ID \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\section*{Insert new subclause 11.13.19.3.4.17:}

\subsection*{11.13.19.3.4.17 Classifier Action Rule}

The value of this field specifies an action associate with the classifier rule.
If this classification action rule exists, its action shall be applied on the packets that match this classifier rule.

\begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|}
\hline Type & Length & \multicolumn{1}{|c|}{ Value } \\
\hline$[145 / 146]$. cst.3.19 & 1 & See below \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

Bit \#0:
$0=$ none.
1 = Discard packet
Bit \#1-7: Reserved.

\section*{Insert new subclause 11.13.19.3.4.18:}

\subsection*{11.13.19.3.4.18 Short-format Context ID for ROHC- or ECRTP-compressed packet or ROHC feedback packet}

The values of the field specify a short-format context ID for ROHC- or ECRTP-compressed packets. The CS will attempt to match the context ID with the payload packet's zero- or one-byte prefix Context ID field according to the scheme described in RFC 3095 section 5.1.3.

\begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|}
\hline Type & Length & \multicolumn{1}{|c|}{ Value } \\
\hline$[145 / 146]$. cst.3.18 & 1 & 0 -15: Context ID \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\section*{Delete 11.13.19.3.6 and all its subclauses.}

\subsection*{41.13.19.3.6 PHS-error parameter set}

\subsection*{11.13.19.3.7.1 PHSI}

\section*{Change the first paragraph as indicated:}

The PHSI has a value between 1 and 255, which uniquely references the suppressed byte string. The index is unique per service flow. The uplink and downlink PHSI values are independent of each other.

\subsection*{11.13.19.3.7.4 PHSS}

\section*{Change the second paragraph as indicated:}

This TLV is used when a service flow is being created. For all packets that get classified and assigned to a service flow with PHS enabled, suppression shall be performed over the specified number of bytes as indicated by the PHSS and according to the PHSM. A PHS rule shall contain the PHSS TLV. The range of valid values for PHSS is $1-255$. If this TLV is not included in a service flow definition, or is included with a value of 0 bytes, then PHS is disabled. A nonzero value indicates PHS is enabled.

Change subclause 11.13.19.3.8 to 11.13.19.3.4.16 and change its contents as indicated, and renumber the following subclauses accordingly.

\subsection*{11.13.19.3.8 IPv6 Flow label}

\section*{Change the subclause as indicated:}

The value of this field specifies a tistof matching values for the IPv6 Flow label field. As the flow label field has a length of 20 bits, the first 4 bits of the most significant byte shall be set to $0 \times 0$ and disregarded.

\begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|}
\hline Type & Length & Scope \\
\hline$[145 / 146] .[101 / 105 / 107] .3 .15$ & $\mathrm{n}^{*} 3$ & Flow Label \#'1...Flow label\#n \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\section*{Change the title of subclause 11.13.19.4 as indicated:}

\subsection*{11.13.19.4 ATM CS Encodings for Gconfiguration and MAC Mmessaging}

\subsection*{11.13.19.4.2 ATM Classifier TLV}

\subsection*{11.13.19.4.2.3 ATM Classifier ID}

\section*{Change the 'Length' value in the Table from 16 to 2.}

\section*{Delete subclause 11.13.19.4.4.}

\subsection*{11.13.19.4.4 ATM Classifier Error Parameter Set}

\section*{Insert new subclause 11.13.20:}

\subsection*{11.13.20 Unsolicited grant interval}

The value of this parameter specifies the nominal interval between successive data grant opportunities for this service flow. The ideal schedule for enforcing this parameter is defined by a reference time $t_{0}$, with the desired transmission time $t_{i}=t_{0}+i \times$ interval. The actual grant time, $t_{i}^{\prime}$ shall be in the range $t_{i}<=t_{i}^{\prime}<=t_{i}+$ jitter, where interval is the value specified with this TLV, and jitter is the Tolerated Jitter.

\begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|c|}
\hline Type & Length & Value & Scope \\
\hline$[145 / 146] .40$ & 2 & ms & DSA-REQ, \\
& & & DSA-RSP, \\
& & & DSC-REQ, \\
& & & DSC-RSP \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\section*{Insert new subclause 11.13.21:}

\subsection*{11.13.21 Unsolicited polling interval}

The value of this parameter specifies the maximal nominal interval between successive polling grants opportunities for this Service Flow. The ideal schedule for enforcing this parameter is defined by a reference time $t_{0}$, with the desired polling time $t_{i}=t_{(i-1)}+$ interval.

\begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|c|}
\hline Type & Length & Value & Scope \\
\hline$[145 / 146] .41$ & $\underline{2}$ & $\underline{\mathrm{~ms}}$ & $\underline{\text { DSA-REQ },}$ \\
& & & $\underline{\text { DSA-RSP, }}$ \\
& & & $\underline{\text { DSC-REQ, }}$ \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\section*{Insert new subclause 11.13.22:}

\subsection*{11.13.22 FSN size}

This TLV indicates the size of the FSN for the connection that is being setup. A value of 0 indicates that FSN is 3-bit long and a value of 1 indicates that FSN is 11-bit long.

\begin{tabular}{|c|c|l|c|}
\hline Type & Length & \multicolumn{1}{|c|}{ Value } & Scope \\
\hline$[145 / 146] .42$ & 1 & $\frac{0=3 \text {-bit FSN }}{1=11 \text {-bit FSN }}$ & $\frac{\text { DSA-REQ, }}{\text { Default }=1}$ \\
& & & $\frac{\text { DSA-RSP, }}{\text { DSA-ACK }}$ \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\section*{Insert new subclause 11.13.23:}

\subsection*{11.13.23 MBS service}

This TLV indicates whether or not the MBS service is being requested for the connection that is being setup. A value of 1 indicates Single-BS-MBS is requested and a value of 2 indicates Multi-BS-MBS is requested. If MS or BS want to initiate MBS service, DSA-REQ with MBS service shall be used. The DSA-RSP message shall contain the acceptance or rejection of request and if there is no available MBS, MBS service value may be set to 0 . ARQ shall not be enabled for this connection.

\begin{tabular}{|c|c|l|l|}
\hline Type & Length & \multicolumn{1}{|c|}{ Value } & \multicolumn{1}{c|}{ Scope } \\
\hline$[145 / 146] .4$ & 1 & \begin{tabular}{l} 
0: No available MBS \\
1: Single-BS-MBS \\
2: Multi-BS-MBS
\end{tabular} & DSx-REQ \\
& & DSx-RSP \\
& & DSx-ACK \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\section*{Insert new subclause 11.13.24:}

\subsection*{11.13.24 Global Service Class Name}

The value of this field refers to a predefined BS service configuration to be used for this service flow. The Global Service Class Name itself contains coded references to extensible tables defining QoS Parameters.

\begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|c|}
\hline Type & Length & Value & Scope \\
\hline$[145 / 146] .35$ & 6 & Variable: combination of ASCII characters and hex values & DSx-REQ \\
& & & DSx-RSP \\
DSx-ACK \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

When the Global Service Class Name is used in a service flow encoding, it indicates that all the unspecified QoS Parameters of the service flow need to be provided by the BS. Global Service Class Names are by definition synchronized among all BS.

\section*{Insert new subclause 11.13.25:}

\subsection*{11.13.25 Type of Data Delivery Services}

The value of this parameter specifies type of Data Delivery Service as defined in 6.3.18.

\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|}
\hline Type & Length & Value & Scope \\
\hline [145/146]. 29 & 1 & \begin{tabular}{l}
0: Continuing Grant Service \\
1: Real Time-Variable Rate Service \\
2: Non-Real Time-Variable Rate Service \\
3: Best Efforts Service \\
4: Extended Real-Time Variable Rate Service
\end{tabular} & DSA-REQ, DSA-RSP DSC-REQ, DSC-RSP REG-REQ, REG-RSP \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\section*{Insert new subclause 11.13.26:}

\subsection*{11.13.26 SDU Inter-arrival Interval}

This parameter specifies nominal interval between consequent SDU arrivals as measured at MAC SAP.

\begin{tabular}{|c|c|l|c|}
\hline Type & Length & \multicolumn{1}{|c|}{ Value } & Scope \\
\hline$[145 / 146] .30$ & 2 & \begin{tabular}{l} 
SDU Inter-arrival Interval in the resolution \\
of 0.5 milliseconds
\end{tabular} & \begin{tabular}{l} 
DSA-REQ, DSA-RSP \\
DSC-REQ, DSC-RSP \\
REG-REQ, REG-RSP
\end{tabular} \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\section*{Insert new subclause 11.13.27:}

\subsection*{11.13.27 Time Base}

This parameter specifies time base for rate measurement as defined in 6.3.20.

\begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|c|}
\hline Type & Length & Value & Scope \\
\hline$[145 / 146] .31$ & 2 & Time base in milliseconds & \begin{tabular}{l} 
DSA-REQ, DSA-RSP \\
DSC-REQ, DSC-RSP \\
REG-REQ, REG-RSP
\end{tabular} \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\section*{Insert new subclause 11.13.28:}

\subsection*{11.13.28 Traffic Indication Preference}

This parameter specifies whether traffic on a service flow should generate MOB_TRF-IND messages to an MS in sleep mode.

\begin{tabular}{|c|c|l|l|}
\hline Type & Length & \multicolumn{1}{|c|}{ Value } & \multicolumn{1}{c|}{ DSX } \\
\hline$[145 / 146] .34$ & 1 & \begin{tabular}{l} 
0: No traffic indication \\
1: Traffic indication
\end{tabular} & \begin{tabular}{l} 
DSx-REQ \\
DSx-RSP \\
DSx-ACK
\end{tabular} \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\section*{Insert new subclause 11.13.29:}

\subsection*{11.13.29 MBS zone identifier assignment}

The DSA-RSP message may contain the value of this parameter to specify a MBS Zone identifier. This parameter indicates a MBS zone through which the connection or virtual connection for the associated service flow is valid.

\begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|l|}
\hline Type & Length & Value & \multicolumn{1}{|c|}{ Scope } \\
\hline$[145 / 146] .33$ & 8 & MBS zone identifier & \begin{tabular}{l} 
DSA-REQ/RSP \\
DCD
\end{tabular} \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\section*{Insert new subclause 11.13.30:}

\subsection*{11.13.30 Paging preference}

This parameter specifies whether a service flow may generate paging.

\begin{tabular}{|c|c|l|l|}
\hline Type & Length & \multicolumn{1}{|c|}{ Value } & \multicolumn{1}{c|}{ DSX } \\
\hline$[145 / 146] .32$ & 1 & \begin{tabular}{l} 
0: No paging generation \\
1: Paging generation
\end{tabular} & \begin{tabular}{l} 
DSx-REQ \\
DSx-RSP \\
DSx-ACK
\end{tabular} \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\section*{Insert new subclause 11.13.31:}

\subsection*{11.13.31 SN Feedback Enabled}

The 'SN Feedback Enabled' field indicates whether or not SN feedback is enabled for the given connection. A value of 0 indicates that SN feedback is not enabled. A value of 1 indicates that SN feedback is enabled.

\begin{tabular}{|c|c|l|c|}
\hline Type & Length & \multicolumn{1}{|c|}{ Value } & Scope \\
\hline$[145 / 146] .37$ & 1 & \begin{tabular}{l}
0 - SN feedback is disabled (default) \\
1 - SN feedback is enabled
\end{tabular} & \begin{tabular}{l} 
DSA-REQ, DSA-RSP, \\
DSC-REQ, DSC-REP
\end{tabular} \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\section*{Insert new subclause 11.13.32:}

\subsection*{11.13.32 HARQ Service Flows}

The 'HARQ Service Flows' field specifies whether the connection uses HARQ or not.
The relevance connections of this parameter when appears in REG-REQ/RSP messages are Basic, Primary, and Secondary CIDs.

Transport CIDs that have HARQ Connection enabled indication must only be transmitted inside HARQ PHY burst type. Basic, Primary, and secondary CIDs that have HARQ Connection enabled indication can be either transmitted inside HARQ or non-HARQ PHY burst type.

\begin{tabular}{|c|c|l|l|}
\hline Type & Length & \multicolumn{1}{|c|}{ Value } & \multicolumn{1}{c|}{ Scope } \\
\hline$[145 / 146] .44$ & 1 & \begin{tabular}{l}
$0=$ Non HARQ (default) \\
$1=$ HARQ Connection
\end{tabular} & \begin{tabular}{l} 
DSA-REQ, DSA-RSP, \\
REG-REQ, REG-RSP
\end{tabular} \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\section*{Insert new subclause 11.13.33:}

\subsection*{11.13.33 CID allocation for Active BSs}

The value of this field specifies a list of CIDs assigned by active BSs in the diversity set except for the anchor BS for the service flow with non-null admittedQoSParamSet or ActiveQoSParamSet. There is one CID per active BS and the CID is used when the active BS becomes the anchor BS . If CID assignment is sent for each active BS in MOB_BSHO-RSP and MOB_BSHO-REQ messages, the DSx messages shall contain CID allocation for Active BSs. The CID for anchor BS is defined by 11.13.2. The value of (Num of active BS) is used to calculate length is the number of BSs in the diversity set.

\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|}
\hline Type & Length & Value & Scope \\
\hline [145/146]. 39 & \begin{tabular}{l}
variable \\
Length is defined as: (Num of active BS -1) * 2
\end{tabular} & List of CIDs for the active BSs. Starting from the first byte, every 2 bytes contains one CID value per active BS. CIDs are listed based on the TEMP_BS_ID of the active BS. The BS. The TEMP_BS_IDs are sorted in an ascending order & DSA-REQ/RSP DSC-REQ/RSP \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\section*{Insert new subclause 11.13.34:}

\subsection*{11.13.34 Authorization Token}

The value of this field specifies an authorization token that may be used when MS creates or modifies a service flow by sending DSA-REQ or DSC-REQ message. An authorization token identifies a session and its QoS parameters, and is used for authorizing the QoS for one or more IP flows generated by higher-level service creation/modification procedures. The token is provided to the MS by the higher-level service through some mechanism that is outside the scope of this specification. The MS must include the token in this TLV exactly as received from the higher-level service and must treat the token as an opaque octet string whose
meaning is of significance only to those higher-level services. The field should not be included in the DSAREQ or DSC-REQ messages that are sent by BS.

\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|}
\hline Type & Length & Value & Scope \\
\hline [145/146]. 45 & variable & Authorization token that is used for authorizing the QoS for one or service flows generated by MS-initiated higher-level service flow creation or modification procedures & DSA-REQ DSC-REQ \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\section*{Insert new subclause 11.13.35:}

\subsection*{11.13.35 HARQ Channel mapping}

This TLV is valid only in HARQ enabled connection. It specifies a HARQ channel number that may be used to carry data from this connection. This TLV may specify more then one channel per connection. HARQ channels may share more then one connection. An absent of this TLV means all HARQ channels can be used by this connection.

The absence of this TLV in any of the REQ or RSP messages of the connection creation means all HARQ channels can be used by this connection.

The relevance connections of this parameter when appears in REG-REQ/RSP messages are Basic, Primary, and Secondary CIDs.

\begin{tabular}{|c|c|l|c|}
\hline Type & Length & \multicolumn{1}{|c|}{ Value } & Scope \\
\hline$[145 / 146] .46$ & variable & \begin{tabular}{l} 
HARQ channel Index (1 byte \\
each)
\end{tabular} & \begin{tabular}{l} 
DSA-REQ, DSA-RSP, \\
REG-REQ, REG-RSP
\end{tabular} \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\section*{Insert new subclause 11.13.36:}

\subsection*{11.13.36 PDU SN extended subheader for HARQ reordering}

This TLV is valid only in HARQ enabled connection. It specifies whether PDU SN extended subheader should be applied by the transmitter on every PDU on this connection. This SN may be used by the receiver to ensure PDU ordering.

This counter should start at 0 and should be reset after HHO/FBSS operations

The relevance connections of this parameter when appears in REG-REQ/RSP messages are Basic, Primary, and Secondary CIDs (each should have its own PDU numbering)

Value of 0 in either of the messages means the endpoint does not support the PDU SN number for the specific connection. If both end points support PDU SN for the connection, the larger SN number should be chosen.

\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|}
\hline Type & Length & Value & Scope \\
\hline [145/146]. 42 & 1 & \begin{tabular}{l}
0-No support for PDU SN in this connection (default) \\
1-PDU SN (short) extended SH 2-PDU SN (long) extended SH 3-256-Reserved.
\end{tabular} & DSA-REQ, DSA-RSP, REG-REQ, REG-RSP \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\section*{Insert new subclause 11.13.37:}

\subsection*{11.13.37 MBS contents IDs}

If MS sends DSA-REQ message which requests a MBS service as described in 11.13.20, BS may respond to it with DSA-RSP message including MBS Contents Identifier TLV in order to establish an MBS connection with multiple MBS contents.

TLV values shall be composed of 2 byte-long MBS Contents IDs to distinguish the logical MBS connection for each MBS contents. Since MBS Contents ID is vendor-specific and dependent on application-level implementation, it is unnecessary to specify in this standard.

A 1 byte-long Logical Channel ID, which pairs with Multicast CID in MBS_DATA_IE, is allocated to each 2 byte-long MBS Contents IDs in order that it is included in TLV value. For example, Logical Channel ID ' 0 ' is allocated to MBS Contents ID(0), Logical Channel ID ' 1 ' is allocated to MBS Contents ID(1) and so on. Logical Channel ID is used for MS to discriminate the MBS message in MBS data burst.

According to the Dynamic Service Addition (DSA) procedure described in 6.3.13.1, a BS may send MS a DSA-REQ message to establish connection. Therefore, BS may also send MS a DSA-REQ message including MBS Contents Identifier TLV in order to make an establishment of MBS connection with multiple MBS contents.

\begin{tabular}{|c|l|l|l|}
\hline Type & Length & \multicolumn{1}{|c|}{ Value } & \multicolumn{1}{c|}{ Scope } \\
\hline$[145 / 146] .43$ & variable $(2 * n)$ & MBS Contents ID $(0)$, & DSA-REQ \\
& & MBS Contents ID $(1), \ldots$ & DSA-RSP \\
& & MBS Contents ID $(n-1)$ & DSA-ACK \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\section*{Insert a new subclause 11.14:}

\subsection*{11.14 DREG-CMD/REQ message encodings}

\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|}
\hline Name & Type & Length & Value \\
\hline Paging Information & 1 & 5 & \begin{tabular}{l}
Bits 15:0 - PAGING_CYCLE-Cycle in which the paging message is transmitted within the paging group \\
Bits 23:16 - PAGING OFFSET-Determines the frame within the cycle in which the paging message is transmitted. Must be smaller than PAGING CYCLE value \\
Bits 39:24- Paging-group-ID-ID of the paging group the MS is assigned to
\end{tabular} \\
\hline REQ-duration & 2 & 1 & Waiting value for the DREG-REQ message re-transmission (measured in frames) \\
\hline Paging Controller ID & 3 & 6 & This is a logical network identifier for the serving BS or other network entity retaining MS service and operational information and/ or administering paging activity for the MS while in IDLE Mode \\
\hline MAC Hash Skip Threshold & 5 & 2 & Maximum number of successive MOB_PAG-ADV messages that may be sent from a BS without individual notification for an MS for which BS is allowed to skip MS MAC Address Hash when the Action Code for the MS is 0b00, 'No Action Required'. If the value is set to 0xFF, a BS shall omit MAC Address hash of the MS with 'No Action Required' for every MOB_PAG-ADV message.On the contrary, if the value is set to zero, a BS shall include the MS MAC Address hash in every MOB_PAG-ADV message. \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|}
\hline Name & Type & Length & Value \\
\hline Idle Mode Retain Information & 4 & 1 & \begin{tabular}{l}
Idle Mode Retain Information is provided as part of this message is indicative only. Network Re-entry from Idle Mode process requirements may change at time of actual reentry. For each Bit location, a value of 0 indicates the information for the associated reentry management messages shall not be retained and managed, a value of 1 indicates the information for the associated re-entry management message shall be retained and managed \\
Bit \#0: Retain MS service and operational information associated with SBC-REQ/RSP messages \\
Bit \#1: Retain MS service and operational information associated with PKM-REQ/RSP messages \\
Bit \#2: Retain MS service and operational information associated with REG-REQ/RSP messages \\
Bit \#3: Retain MS service and operational information associated with Network Address \\
Bit \#4: Retain MS service and operational information associated with Time of Day \\
Bit \#5: Retain MS service and operational information associated with TFTP messages \\
Bit \#6: Retain MS service and operational information associated with Full service (MAC state machines, CS classifier information, etc.). The information retained by setting Bit \#6 does not include those information associated with SBC-REQ/RSP messages, PKMREQ/RSP messages, REG-REQ/RSP messages, Network Address, Time of Day, and TFTP messages unless otherwise specified by setting one or more Bits \#0-\#5. \\
Bit \#7: Consider Paging Preference of each Service Flow in resource retention. Bit \#7 is meaningful when Bit \#2 and Bit \#6 have a value of 1. If Bit \#2, Bit \#6 and Bit \#7 is 1, MS service and operational information associated with Full service (MAC state machines, CS classifier information, etc.) are retained for Service Flows with positive Paging Preference. If Bit \#2 and Bit \#6 are 1 and Bit \#7 is 0, MS service and operational information associated with Full service (MAC state machines, CS classifier information, etc.) are retained for all Service Flows.
\end{tabular} \\
\hline Paging cycle request & 52 & 2 & Requested Cycle in which the paging message is transmitted within the paging group. \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\section*{Insert new subclause 11.15:}

\subsection*{11.15 Handover management encodings}

\section*{Insert new subclause 11.15.1:}

\subsection*{11.15.1 Resource_Retain_Time}

The Resource_Retain_Time is Time duration for MS's connection information that will be retained in serving BS. This value is measured in 100 milliseconds. If this value is set to 0 , the serving BS will retain the MS's connection information during Resource Retain Time negotiated at early registration stage. If this value is set to non-zero, it is the proposed Resource Retain Time by serving BS and the serving BS will retain the MS's connection information during that time after reception of MOB_HO-IND message. $\left(\mathrm{HO} \_\mathrm{IND} \_\right.$type $\left.=0 \mathrm{~b} 00\right)$

\begin{tabular}{|c|c|l|c|}
\hline Type & Length & \multicolumn{1}{|c|}{ Value } & Scope \\
\hline 1 & 2 & \begin{tabular}{l} 
0: The serving BS will retain the MS's connection \\
information during Resource Retain Time negotiated \\
at registration stage. \\
1-255: Resource Retain Time (100 milliseconds unit)
\end{tabular} & \begin{tabular}{l} 
MOB_BSHO-REQ \\
MOB_BSHO-RSP
\end{tabular} \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\section*{Insert new subclause 11.16:}

\subsection*{11.16 Sleep mode management encodings}

\section*{Insert new subclause 11.16.1:}

\subsection*{11.16.1 SLPID_Update}

The SLPID_Update TLV specifies a new SLPID that replaces an old SLPID. This TLV may include multiple Old_New_SLPID values for the MSs negatively indicated in MOB_TRF-IND message.

\begin{tabular}{|c|c|l|l|}
\hline Type & Length & \multicolumn{1}{|c|}{ Value } & \multicolumn{1}{c|}{ Scope } \\
\hline 1 & variable & See following table & RNG-RSP \\
& & & MOB_TRF-IND \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|}
\hline Field & \begin{tabular}{c} 
Length \\
(bits)
\end{tabular} & Note \\
\hline Old New SLPID & 20 & \begin{tabular}{l} 
First 10 bits indicates old SLPID and the \\
second 10 bits indicates new SLPID
\end{tabular} \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\section*{Insert new subclause 11.16.2:}

\subsection*{11.16.2 Next Periodic Ranging}

This value indicates offset of the frame in which the periodic ranging will be performed with respect to the frame where MOB_SLP-RSP is transmitted. If MS receives MOB_SLP-RSP message with 'Next Periodic Ranging' $=0$, it shall deactivate all active Power Saving Classes and return to Normal Operation.

\begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|c|}
\hline Type & Length & Value & Scope \\
\hline 2 & 2 & Offset in frames & MOB_SLP-RSP \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\section*{Insert new subclause 11.17:}

\subsection*{11.17 MOB_PAG-ADV management message encodings}

The encoding described in this subclause is specific to the MOB_PAG-ADV message (6.3.2.3.59).

\section*{Insert new subclause 11.17.1:}

\subsection*{11.17.1 CDMA code and transmission opportunity assignment}

The 'CDMA code and transmission opportunity assignment' field indicates the assigned code and transmission opportunity for an MS that is paged to use over dedicated CDMA ranging region.

\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|}
\hline Type & Length (bits) & Value & Scope \\
\hline 150 & variable; $8+$ N_assign × 16 ) & \begin{tabular}{l}
Bit \#0 - \#7: N_assign \\
Subsequent (N_assign x 16) bits: \\
for ( $\mathrm{i}=0, \mathrm{i}<$ N_assign, $\mathrm{i}++$ ) \{ \\
8-bits code index assigned to an MS who is paged 8-bits transmission opportunity offset assigned to an MS who is paged \\
\}
\end{tabular} & OFDMA \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\section*{Insert new subclause 11.17.2:}

\subsection*{11.17.2 Page-Response Window}

The 'Page-Response Window' TLV indicates the Page-Response window for an MS who is paged to transmit the assigned code for CDMA ranging channel.

\begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|c|}
\hline Type & Length & \multicolumn{1}{|c|}{ Value } & \multicolumn{1}{c|}{ PHY Scope } \\
\hline 152 & 1 & Page-Response window, in frames & OFDMA \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\section*{Insert new subclause:]}

\subsection*{11.17.3 Enabled-Action-Triggered}

This value indicates the enabled action that MS performs upon reaching trigger condition in sleep mode. MS may include this TLV item in MOB_SLP-REQ message to request an activation of type of Power Saving Class. BS shall include this TLV in MOB_SLP-RSP message transmitted in response to the MOB_SLPREQ message.

\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|}
\hline Type & Length & Value & Scope \\
\hline 3 & 1 & \begin{tabular}{l}
Indicates action performed upon reaching trigger condition in sleep mode \\
If bit\#0 is set to 1, respond on trigger with MOB_SCN-REPORT If bit\#1 is set to 1 , respond on trigger with MOB_MSHO-REQ If bit\#2 is set to 1 , on trigger, MS starts neighboring BS scanning process by sending MOB_SCN-REQ \\
Bit\#3-bit\#7: Reserved. Shall be set to 0 .
\end{tabular} & MOB_SLP-REQ/RSP \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\section*{Insert new subclause 11.18:}

\subsection*{11.18 MOB_NBR-ADV management message encodings}

The encodings described in this subclause are specific to the MOB_NBR-ADV message (6.3.2.3.47).

\subsection*{11.18.1 PHY Mode ID}
